description:
Introduced in: v1.1
Calculates the absolute value of x . Has no effect if x is of an unsigned type. If x is of a signed type, it returns an unsigned number.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Introduced in: v1.1
Calculates the absolute value of x . Has no effect if x is of an unsigned type. If x is of a signed type, it returns an unsigned number.<sep>[SYNTAX]: abs(x)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - x вҖ” Value to get the absolute value of
Returned value
The absolute value of x<sep>[RETURN]: The absolute value of x<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT abs(-0.5) ; 0.5

description:
Introduced in: v23.10
Reverses the bytes of an integer, i.e. changes its endianness .
The below example can be worked out in the following manner:
- Convert the base-10 integer to its equivalent hexadecimal format in big-endian format, i.e. 3351772109 -> C7 C7 FB CD (4 bytes)
- Reverse the bytes, i.e. C7 C7 FB CD -> CD FB C7 C7
- Convert the result back to an integer assuming big-endian, i.e. CD FB C7 C7 -> 3455829959 One use case of this function is reversing IPv4s:
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Introduced in: v23.10
Reverses the bytes of an integer, i.e. changes its endianness .
The below example can be worked out in the following manner:
- Convert the base-10 integer to its equivalent hexadecimal format in big-endian format, i.e. 3351772109 -> C7 C7 FB CD (4 bytes)
- Reverse the bytes, i.e. C7 C7 FB CD -> CD FB C7 C7
- Convert the result back to an integer assuming big-endian, i.e. CD FB C7 C7 -> 3455829959 One use case of this function is reversing IPv4s:<sep>[SYNTAX]: byteSwap(x)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - x вҖ” An integer value. (U)Int*
Returned value
Returns x with bytes reversed. (U)Int*<sep>[RETURN]: Returns x with bytes reversed. (U)Int*<sep>[DEMO]: в”Ңв”ҖtoIPv4(byteSwap(toUInt32(toIPv4('205.251.199.199'))))в”Җв”җ ; SELECT byteSwap(3351772109) ; 3455829959

description:
Introduced in: v1.1
Calculates the quotient of two values a and b . The result type is always Float64 . Integer division is provided by the intDiv function.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Introduced in: v1.1
Calculates the quotient of two values a and b . The result type is always Float64 . Integer division is provided by the intDiv function.<sep>[SYNTAX]: divide(x, y)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - x вҖ” Dividend - y вҖ” Divisor
Returned value
The quotient of x and y<sep>[RETURN]: The quotient of x and y<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT divide(25,5) AS quotient, toTypeName(quotient) ; 5 Float64 ; SELECT divide(25,0)

description:
Introduced in: v22.12
Performs division on two decimals. Result value will be of type Decimal256 . Result scale can be explicitly specified by result_scale argument (const Integer in range [0, 76] ). If not specified, the result scale is the max scale of given arguments.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Introduced in: v22.12
Performs division on two decimals. Result value will be of type Decimal256 . Result scale can be explicitly specified by result_scale argument (const Integer in range [0, 76] ). If not specified, the result scale is the max scale of given arguments.<sep>[SYNTAX]: divideDecimal(x, y[, result_scale])<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - x вҖ” First value: Decimal . - y вҖ” Second value: Decimal . - result_scale вҖ” Scale of result. Type Int/UInt .
Returned value
The result of division with given scale. Decimal256<sep>[RETURN]: The result of division with given scale. Decimal256<sep>[DEMO]: в”Ңв”ҖdivideDecimal(toDecimal256(-12, 0), toDecimal32(2.1, 1), 10)в”Җв”җ ; SELECT toDecimal64(-12, 1) / toDecimal32(2.1, 1); ; в”Ңв”Җdivide(toDecimal64(-12, 1), toDecimal32(2.1, 1))в”Җв”җ
example:
SELECT toDecimal64(-12, 1) / toDecimal32(2.1, 1);
SELECT toDecimal64(-12, 1) as a, toDecimal32(2.1, 1) as b, divideDecimal(a, b, 1), divideDecimal(a, b, 5);

в”Ңв”Җdivide(toDecimal64(-12, 1), toDecimal32(2.1, 1))в”Җв”җ
в”Ӯ                                             -5.7 в”Ӯ
в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җaв”Җв”¬в”Җв”Җв”Җbв”Җв”¬в”ҖdivideDecimal(toDecimal64(-12, 1), toDecimal32(2.1, 1), 1)в”Җв”¬в”ҖdivideDecimal(toDecimal64(-12, 1), toDecimal32(2.1, 1), 5)в”Җв”җ
в”Ӯ -12 в”Ӯ 2.1 в”Ӯ                                                       -5.7 в”Ӯ                                                   -5.71428 в”Ӯ
в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ

description:
Introduced in: v25.5
Same as divide but returns NULL when dividing by zero.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Introduced in: v25.5
Same as divide but returns NULL when dividing by zero.<sep>[SYNTAX]: divideOrNull(x, y)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - x вҖ” Dividend - y вҖ” Divisor
Returned value
The quotient of x and y, or NULL.<sep>[RETURN]: The quotient of x and y, or NULL.<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT divideOrNull(25, 0) ; \N

description:
Introduced in: v1.1
Returns the greatest common divisor of two values a and b.
An exception is thrown when dividing by zero or when dividing a minimal negative number by minus one.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Introduced in: v1.1
Returns the greatest common divisor of two values a and b.
An exception is thrown when dividing by zero or when dividing a minimal negative number by minus one.<sep>[SYNTAX]: gcd(x, y)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - x вҖ” First integer - y вҖ” Second integer
Returned value
The greatest common divisor of x and y .<sep>[RETURN]: The greatest common divisor of x and y .<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT gcd(12, 18) ; 6

description:
Introduced in: v20.3
Checks whether a floating point value is finite.
You can get a similar result by using the ternary operator : isFinite(x) ? x : y .
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Introduced in: v20.3
Checks whether a floating point value is finite.
You can get a similar result by using the ternary operator : isFinite(x) ? x : y .<sep>[SYNTAX]: ifNotFinite(x,y)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - x вҖ” Value to check if infinite. Float*
- y вҖ” Fallback value. Float*
Returned value
- x if x is finite.
- y if x is not finite.<sep>[RETURN]: - x if x is finite.
- y if x is not finite.<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT 1/0 AS infimum, ifNotFinite(infimum,42) ; inf  42

description:
Introduced in: v1.1
Performs an integer division of two values x by y . In other words it computes the quotient rounded down to the next smallest integer.
The result has the same width as the dividend (the first parameter).
An exception is thrown when dividing by zero, when the quotient does not fit in the range of the dividend, or when dividing a minimal negative number by minus one.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Introduced in: v1.1
Performs an integer division of two values x by y . In other words it computes the quotient rounded down to the next smallest integer.
The result has the same width as the dividend (the first parameter).
An exception is thrown when dividing by zero, when the quotient does not fit in the range of the dividend, or when dividing a minimal negative number by minus one.<sep>[SYNTAX]: intDiv(x, y)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - x вҖ” Left hand operand. - y вҖ” Right hand operand.
Returned value
Result of integer division of x and y<sep>[RETURN]: Result of integer division of x and y<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT intDiv(toFloat64(1), 0.001) AS res, toTypeName(res) ; в”Ңв”Җв”Җresв”Җв”¬в”ҖtoTypeName(intDiv(toFloat64(1), 0.001))в”Җв”җ ; SELECT

description:
Introduced in: v25.5
Same as intDiv but returns NULL when dividing by zero or when dividing a minimal negative number by minus one.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Introduced in: v25.5
Same as intDiv but returns NULL when dividing by zero or when dividing a minimal negative number by minus one.<sep>[SYNTAX]: intDivOrNull(x, y)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - x вҖ” Left hand operand. (U)Int*
- y вҖ” Right hand operand. (U)Int*
Returned value
Result of integer division of x and y , or NULL.<sep>[RETURN]: Result of integer division of x and y , or NULL.<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT intDivOrNull(1, 0) ; \N ; SELECT intDivOrNull(-9223372036854775808, -1)

description:
Introduced in: v1.1
Same as intDiv but returns zero when dividing by zero or when dividing a minimal negative number by minus one.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Introduced in: v1.1
Same as intDiv but returns zero when dividing by zero or when dividing a minimal negative number by minus one.<sep>[SYNTAX]: intDivOrZero(a, b)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - a вҖ” Left hand operand. (U)Int*
- b вҖ” Right hand operand. (U)Int*
Returned value
Result of integer division of a and b, or zero.<sep>[RETURN]: Result of integer division of a and b, or zero.<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT intDivOrZero(1, 0) ; 0 ; SELECT intDivOrZero(0.05, -1)

description:
Introduced in: v1.1
Returns 1 if the Float32 or Float64 argument not infinite and not a NaN , otherwise this function returns 0 .
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Introduced in: v1.1
Returns 1 if the Float32 or Float64 argument not infinite and not a NaN , otherwise this function returns 0 .<sep>[SYNTAX]: isFinite(x)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - x вҖ” Number to check for finiteness. Float*
Returned value
1 if x is not infinite and not NaN , otherwise 0 .<sep>[RETURN]: 1 if x is not infinite and not NaN , otherwise 0 .<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT isFinite(inf) ; 0

description:
Introduced in: v1.1
Returns 1 if the Float32 or Float64 argument is infinite, otherwise this function returns 0 . Note that 0 is returned for a NaN .
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Introduced in: v1.1
Returns 1 if the Float32 or Float64 argument is infinite, otherwise this function returns 0 . Note that 0 is returned for a NaN .<sep>[SYNTAX]: isInfinite(x)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - x вҖ” Number to check for infiniteness. Float*
Returned value
1 if x is infinite, otherwise 0 (including for NaN ).<sep>[RETURN]: 1 if x is infinite, otherwise 0 (including for NaN ).<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT isInfinite(inf), isInfinite(NaN), isInfinite(10)) ; 1 0 0

description:

detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Introduced in: v1.1
Returns 1 if the Float32 and Float64 argument is NaN , otherwise returns 0 .<sep>[SYNTAX]: isNaN(x)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - x вҖ” Argument to evaluate for if it is NaN . Float*
Returned value
1 if NaN , otherwise 0<sep>[RETURN]: 1 if NaN , otherwise 0<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT isNaN(NaN) ; 1

description:

An exception is thrown when dividing by zero or when dividing a minimal negative number by minus one.

Returned value
Returns the least common multiple of x and y . (U)Int*<sep>[RETURN]: Returns the least common multiple of x and y . (U)Int*<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT lcm(6, 8) ; 24

detail:

description:
Introduced in: v1.1
Calculates the difference of two values a and b . The result is always signed. Similar to plus, it is possible to subtract an integer from a date or date with time. Additionally, subtraction between date with time is supported, resulting in the time difference between them.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Introduced in: v1.1
Calculates the difference of two values a and b . The result is always signed. Similar to plus, it is possible to subtract an integer from a date or date with time. Additionally, subtraction between date with time is supported, resulting in the time difference between them.<sep>[SYNTAX]: minus(x, y)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - x вҖ” Minuend. - y вҖ” Subtrahend.
Returned value
x minus y<sep>[RETURN]: x minus y<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT minus(10, 5) ; 5 ; SELECT minus(toDate('2025-01-01'),5)

description:
Introduced in: v1.1
Calculates the remainder of the division of two values a by b.
The result type is an integer if both inputs are integers. If one of the inputs is a floating-point number, the result type is Float64.
The remainder is computed like in C++. Truncated division is used for negative numbers.
An exception is thrown when dividing by zero or when dividing a minimal negative number by minus one.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Introduced in: v1.1
Calculates the remainder of the division of two values a by b.
The result type is an integer if both inputs are integers. If one of the inputs is a floating-point number, the result type is Float64.
The remainder is computed like in C++. Truncated division is used for negative numbers.
An exception is thrown when dividing by zero or when dividing a minimal negative number by minus one.<sep>[SYNTAX]: modulo(a, b)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - a вҖ” The dividend - b вҖ” The divisor (modulus)
Returned value
The remainder of a % b<sep>[RETURN]: The remainder of a % b<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT modulo(5, 2) ; 1

description:
Introduced in: v25.5
Calculates the remainder when dividing a by b . Similar to function modulo except that moduloOrNull will return NULL if the right argument is 0.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Introduced in: v25.5
Calculates the remainder when dividing a by b . Similar to function modulo except that moduloOrNull will return NULL if the right argument is 0.<sep>[SYNTAX]: moduloOrNull(x, y)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - x вҖ” The dividend. (U)Int* or Float*
- y вҖ” The divisor (modulus). (U)Int* or Float*
Returned value
Returns the remainder of the division of x by y , or null when the divisor is zero.<sep>[RETURN]: Returns the remainder of the division of x by y , or null when the divisor is zero.<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT moduloOrNull(5, 0) ; \N

description:
Introduced in: v20.3
Like modulo but returns zero when the divisor is zero, as opposed to an exception with the modulo function.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Introduced in: v20.3
Like modulo but returns zero when the divisor is zero, as opposed to an exception with the modulo function.<sep>[SYNTAX]: moduloOrZero(a, b)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - a вҖ” The dividend. (U)Int* or Float*
- b вҖ” The divisor (modulus). (U)Int* or Float*
Returned value
Returns the remainder of a % b, or 0 when the divisor is 0 .<sep>[RETURN]: Returns the remainder of a % b, or 0 when the divisor is 0 .<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT moduloOrZero(5, 0) ; 0

detail:

Calculates the product of two values x and y .<sep>[SYNTAX]: multiply(x, y)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - x вҖ” factor. (U)Int* or Float* or Decimal
- y вҖ” factor. (U)Int* or Float* or Decimal
Returned value
Returns the product of x and y<sep>[RETURN]: Returns the product of x and y<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT multiply(5,5) ; 25

description:
Introduced in: v22.12
Performs multiplication on two decimals. Result value will be of type Decimal256 . Result scale can be explicitly specified by result_scale argument (const Integer in range [0, 76] ). If not specified, the result scale is the max scale of given arguments.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Introduced in: v22.12
Performs multiplication on two decimals. Result value will be of type Decimal256 . Result scale can be explicitly specified by result_scale argument (const Integer in range [0, 76] ). If not specified, the result scale is the max scale of given arguments.<sep>[SYNTAX]: multiplyDecimal(a, b[, result_scale])<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - a вҖ” First value. Decimal
- b вҖ” Second value. Decimal
- result_scale вҖ” Scale of result. (U)Int*
Returned value
The result of multiplication with the given scale. Type: Decimal256<sep>[RETURN]: The result of multiplication with the given scale. Type: Decimal256<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT multiplyDecimal(toDecimal256(-12, 0), toDecimal32(-2.1, 1), 1) ; 25.2 ; в”Ңв”Җmultiply(toDecimal64(-12.647, 3), toDecimal32(2.1239, 4))в”Җв”җ
example:
в”Ңв”Җmultiply(toDecimal64(-12.647, 3), toDecimal32(2.1239, 4))в”Җв”җ
в”Ӯ                                               -26.8609633 в”Ӯ
в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
в”Ңв”ҖmultiplyDecimal(toDecimal64(-12.647, 3), toDecimal32(2.1239, 4))в”Җв”җ
в”Ӯ                                                         -26.8609 в”Ӯ
в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ

SELECT
    toDecimal64(-12.647987876, 9) AS a,
    toDecimal64(123.967645643, 9) AS b,
    multiplyDecimal(a, b);
SELECT
    toDecimal64(-12.647987876, 9) AS a,
    toDecimal64(123.967645643, 9) AS b,
    a * b;

в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җaв”Җв”¬в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җbв”Җв”¬в”ҖmultiplyDecimal(toDecimal64(-12.647987876, 9), toDecimal64(123.967645643, 9))в”Җв”җ
в”Ӯ -12.647987876 в”Ӯ 123.967645643 в”Ӯ                                                               -1567.941279108 в”Ӯ
в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ

While processing toDecimal64(-12.647987876, 9) AS a, toDecimal64(123.967645643, 9) AS b, a * b. (DECIMAL_OVERFLOW)

Creates a NumericIndexedVector from a map. The mapвҖҷs keys represent the vector's index and map's value represents the vector's value.

Creates a NumericIndexedVector from a map. The mapвҖҷs keys represent the vector's index and map's value represents the vector's value.<sep>[SYNTAX]: SELECT numericIndexedVectorBuild(map)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - map вҖ” A mapping from index to value.

description:
Introduced in: v1.1
Calculates the sum of two values x and y . Alias: x + y (operator). It is possible to add an integer and a date or date with time. The former operation increments the number of days in the date, the latter operation increments the number of seconds in the date with time.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Introduced in: v1.1
Calculates the sum of two values x and y . Alias: x + y (operator). It is possible to add an integer and a date or date with time. The former operation increments the number of days in the date, the latter operation increments the number of seconds in the date with time.<sep>[SYNTAX]: plus(x, y)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - x вҖ” Left hand operand. - y вҖ” Right hand operand.
Returned value
Returns the sum of x and y<sep>[RETURN]: Returns the sum of x and y<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT plus(5,5) ; 10 ; SELECT plus(toDate('2025-01-01'),5)

description:
Introduced in: v22.11
Calculates the remainder when dividing x by y . Similar to function modulo except that positiveModulo always return non-negative number.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Introduced in: v22.11
Calculates the remainder when dividing x by y . Similar to function modulo except that positiveModulo always return non-negative number.<sep>[SYNTAX]: positiveModulo(x, y)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - x вҖ” The dividend. (U)Int* or Float* or Decimal
- y вҖ” The divisor (modulus). (U)Int* or Float* or Decimal
Returned value
Returns the difference between x and the nearest integer not greater than x divisible by y .<sep>[RETURN]: Returns the difference between x and the nearest integer not greater than x divisible by y .<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT positiveModulo(-1, 10) ; 9

description:
Introduced in: v1.1
Creates an array from the function arguments.
The arguments should be constants and have types that share a common supertype. At least one argument must be passed, because otherwise it isn't clear which type of array to create. This means that you can't use this function to create an empty array. To do so, use the emptyArray* function.
Use the [ ] operator for the same functionality.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Introduced in: v1.1
Creates an array from the function arguments.
The arguments should be constants and have types that share a common supertype. At least one argument must be passed, because otherwise it isn't clear which type of array to create. This means that you can't use this function to create an empty array. To do so, use the emptyArray* function.
Use the [ ] operator for the same functionality.<sep>[SYNTAX]: array(x1 [, x2, ..., xN])<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - x1 вҖ” Constant value of any type T. If only this argument is provided, the array will be of type T. - [, x2, ..., xN] вҖ” Additional N constant values sharing a common supertype with x1
Returned value
Returns an array, where 'T' is the smallest common type out of the passed arguments. Array(T)<sep>[RETURN]: Returns an array, where 'T' is the smallest common type out of the passed arguments. Array(T)<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT array(toInt32(1), toUInt16(2), toInt8(3)) AS a, toTypeName(a) ; в”Ңв”Җaв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”ҖtoTypeName(a)в”Җв”җ ; SELECT array(toInt32(5), toDateTime('1998-06-16'), toInt8(5)) AS a, toTypeName(a)
example: SELECT array(toInt32(1), toUInt16(2), toInt8(3)) AS a, toTypeName(a)
SELECT array(toInt32(5), toDateTime('1998-06-16'), toInt8(5)) AS a, toTypeName(a)

description:
Introduced in: v20.4
Calculates the area under the precision-recall (PR) curve. A precision-recall curve is created by plotting precision on the y-axis and recall on the x-axis across all thresholds. The resulting value ranges from 0 to 1, with a higher value indicating better model performance. The PR AUC is particularly useful for imbalanced datasets, providing a clearer comparison of performance compared to ROC AUC on those cases. For more details, please see here , here and here .
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Introduced in: v20.4
Calculates the area under the precision-recall (PR) curve. A precision-recall curve is created by plotting precision on the y-axis and recall on the x-axis across all thresholds. The resulting value ranges from 0 to 1, with a higher value indicating better model performance. The PR AUC is particularly useful for imbalanced datasets, providing a clearer comparison of performance compared to ROC AUC on those cases. For more details, please see here , here and here .<sep>[SYNTAX]: arrayAUCPR(scores, labels[, partial_offsets])<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - cores вҖ” Scores prediction model gives. Array((U)Int*) or Array(Float*)
- labels вҖ” Labels of samples, usually 1 for positive sample and 0 for negative sample. Array((U)Int*) or Array(Enum)
- partial_offsets вҖ”
- Optional. An Array(T) of three non-negative integers for calculating a partial area under the PR curve (equivalent to a vertical band of the PR space) instead of the whole AUC. This option is useful for distributed computation of the PR AUC. The array must contain the following elements [ higher_partitions_tp , higher_partitions_fp , total_positives ]. higher_partitions_tp : The number of positive labels in the higher-scored partitions. higher_partitions_fp : The number of negative labels in the higher-scored partitions. total_positives : The total number of positive samples in the entire dataset.
Returned value
Returns area under the precision-recall (PR) curve. Float64<sep>[RETURN]: Returns area under the precision-recall (PR) curve. Float64<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT arrayAUCPR([0.1, 0.4, 0.35, 0.8], [0, 0, 1, 1]); ; в”Ңв”ҖarrayAUCPR([0.1, 0.4, 0.35, 0.8], [0, 0, 1, 1])в”Җв”җ

tree: arrayAll(func(x[, y1, ..., yN]), source_arr[, cond1_arr, ... , condN_arr])
description:
Introduced in: v1.1
Returns 1 if lambda func(x [, y1, y2, ... yN]) returns true for all elements. Otherwise, it returns 0 .
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Introduced in: v1.1
Returns 1 if lambda func(x [, y1, y2, ... yN]) returns true for all elements. Otherwise, it returns 0 .<sep>[SYNTAX]: arrayAll(func(x[, y1, ..., yN]), source_arr[, cond1_arr, ... , condN_arr])<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - func(x[, y1, ..., yN]) вҖ” A lambda function which operates on elements of the source array ( x ) and condition arrays ( y ). Lambda function
- source_arr вҖ” The source array to process. Array(T)
- cond1_arr, ... вҖ” Optional. N condition arrays providing additional arguments to the lambda function. Array(T)
Returned value

description:
Introduced in: v1.1
Gets the element of the provided array with index n where n can be any integer type. If the index falls outside of the bounds of an array, it returns a default value (0 for numbers, an empty string for strings, etc.), except for arguments of a non-constant array and a constant index 0. In this case there will be an error Array indices are 1-based .
Negative indexes are supported. In this case, the corresponding element is selected, numbered from the end. For example, arr[-1] is the last item in the array.
Operator [n] provides the same functionality.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Introduced in: v1.1
Gets the element of the provided array with index n where n can be any integer type. If the index falls outside of the bounds of an array, it returns a default value (0 for numbers, an empty string for strings, etc.), except for arguments of a non-constant array and a constant index 0. In this case there will be an error Array indices are 1-based .
Negative indexes are supported. In this case, the corresponding element is selected, numbered from the end. For example, arr[-1] is the last item in the array.
Operator [n] provides the same functionality.<sep>[SYNTAX]: arrayElement(arr, n)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - arr вҖ” The array to search. Array(T) . - n вҖ” Position of the element to get. (U)Int* .
Returned value
Returns a single combined array from the provided array arguments Array(T)<sep>[RETURN]: Returns a single combined array from the provided array arguments Array(T)<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT arrayElement(arr, 2) FROM (SELECT [1, 2, 3] AS arr) ; 2 ; SELECT arrayElement(arr, -1) FROM (SELECT [1, 2, 3] AS arr)

description:

Negative indexes are supported. In this case, it selects the corresponding element numbered from the end. For example, arr[-1] is the last item in the array.

description:
Introduced in: v1.1
Returns the array [1, 2, 3, ..., length (arr)]
This function is normally used with the ARRAY JOIN clause. It allows counting something just once for each array after applying ARRAY JOIN . This function can also be used in higher-order functions. For example, you can use it to get array indexes for elements that match a condition.

description:

tree: arrayFill(func(x [, y1, ..., yN]), source_arr[, cond1_arr, ... , condN_arr])
description:
Introduced in: v20.1
The arrayFill function sequentially processes a source array from the first element to the last, evaluating a lambda condition at each position using elements from the source and condition arrays. When the lambda function evaluates to false at position i, the function replaces that element with the element at position i-1 from the current state of the array. The first element is always preserved regardless of any condition.

When the lambda function evaluates to false at position i, the function replaces that element with the element at position i-1 from the current state of the array. The first element is always preserved regardless of any condition.<sep>[SYNTAX]: arrayFill(func(x [, y1, ..., yN]), source_arr[, cond1_arr, ... , condN_arr])<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - func(x [, y1, ..., yN]) вҖ” A lambda function func(x [, y1, y2, ... yN]) вҶ’ F(x [, y1, y2, ... yN]) which operates on elements of the source array ( x ) and condition arrays ( y ). - source_arr вҖ” The source array to process. Lambda function
- [, cond1_arr, ... , condN_arr] вҖ” Optional. N condition arrays providing additional arguments to the lambda function. Array(T)
Returned value
Returns an array Array(T)<sep>[RETURN]: Returns an array Array(T)<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT arrayFill(x -> not isNull(x), [1, null, 2, null]) AS res ; [1, 1, 2, 2] ; SELECT arrayFill(x, y, z -> x > y AND x < z, [5, 3, 6, 2], [4, 7, 1, 3], [10, 2, 8, 5]) AS res

Array(T) .

description:

Converts an array of arrays to a flat array.
Function:
- Applies to any depth of nested arrays.
- Does not change arrays that are already flat.
The flattened array contains all the elements from all source arrays.
detail:

Converts an array of arrays to a flat array.
Function:
- Applies to any depth of nested arrays.
- Does not change arrays that are already flat.
The flattened array contains all the elements from all source arrays.<sep>[SYNTAX]: arrayFlatten(arr)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - arr вҖ” A multidimensional array. Array(Array(T))

Returns a flattened array from the multidimensional array Array(T)<sep>[RETURN]: Returns a flattened array from the multidimensional array Array(T)<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT arrayFlatten([[[1]], [[2], [3]]]); ; [1, 2, 3]

tree: arrayFold(О»(acc, x1 [, x2, x3, ... xN]), arr1 [, arr2, arr3, ... arrN], acc)
description:
Introduced in: v23.10
Applies a lambda function to one or more equally-sized arrays and collects the result in an accumulator.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Introduced in: v23.10
Applies a lambda function to one or more equally-sized arrays and collects the result in an accumulator.<sep>[SYNTAX]: arrayFold(О»(acc, x1 [, x2, x3, ... xN]), arr1 [, arr2, arr3, ... arrN], acc)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - О»(x, x1 [, x2, x3, ... xN]) вҖ” A lambda function О»(acc, x1 [, x2, x3, ... xN]) вҶ’ F(acc, x1 [, x2, x3, ... xN]) where F is an operation applied to acc and array values from x with the result of acc re-used. Lambda function
- arr1 [, arr2, arr3, ... arrN] вҖ” N arrays over which to operate. Array(T)
- acc вҖ” Accumulator value with the same type as the return type of the Lambda function.
Returned value
Returns the final acc value.<sep>[RETURN]: Returns the final acc value.<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT arrayFold(acc,x -> acc + x*2, [1, 2, 3, 4], 3::Int64) AS res; ; 23 ; SELECT arrayFold(acc, x -> (acc.2, acc.2 + acc.1),range(number),(1::Int64, 0::Int64)).1 AS fibonacci FROM numbers(1,10);
example: SELECT arrayFold(acc,x -> acc + x*2, [1, 2, 3, 4], 3::Int64) AS res;
SELECT arrayFold(acc, x -> (acc.2, acc.2 + acc.1),range(number),(1::Int64, 0::Int64)).1 AS fibonacci FROM numbers(1,10);

description:

Returned value
Returns an array with distinct elements that are present in all N arrays Array(T)<sep>[RETURN]: Returns an array with distinct elements that are present in all N arrays Array(T)<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT ; в”Ңв”Җnon_empty_intersectionв”Җв”¬в”Җempty_intersectionв”Җв”җ

description:
Introduced in: v1.1
The arrayJoin function takes a row that contains an array and unfolds it, generating multiple rows вҖ“ one for each element in the array. This is in contrast to Regular Functions in ClickHouse which map input values to output values within the same row, and Aggregate Functions which take a group of rows and "compress" or "reduce" them into a single summary row (or a single value within a summary row if used with GROUP BY ).
All the values in the columns are simply copied, except the values in the column where this function is applied; these are replaced with the corresponding array value.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Introduced in: v1.1
The arrayJoin function takes a row that contains an array and unfolds it, generating multiple rows вҖ“ one for each element in the array. This is in contrast to Regular Functions in ClickHouse which map input values to output values within the same row, and Aggregate Functions which take a group of rows and "compress" or "reduce" them into a single summary row (or a single value within a summary row if used with GROUP BY ).
All the values in the columns are simply copied, except the values in the column where this function is applied; these are replaced with the corresponding array value.<sep>[SYNTAX]: arrayJoin(arr)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - arr вҖ” An array to unfold. Array(T)
Returned value
Returns a set of rows unfolded from arr .<sep>[RETURN]: Returns a set of rows unfolded from arr .<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT arrayJoin([1, 2, 3] AS src) AS dst, 'Hello', src ; в”Ңв”Җdstв”Җв”¬в”Җ\'Hello\'в”Җв”¬в”Җsrcв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ ; в”Ңв”Җimpressionsв”Җв”җ
example: SELECT arrayJoin([1, 2, 3] AS src) AS dst, 'Hello', src

tree: arrayLast(func(x[, y1, ..., yN]), source[, cond1, ... , condN_arr])
description:
Introduced in: v1.1
Returns the last element in the source array for which a lambda func(x [, y1, y2, ... yN]) returns true, otherwise it returns a default value.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Introduced in: v1.1
Returns the last element in the source array for which a lambda func(x [, y1, y2, ... yN]) returns true, otherwise it returns a default value.<sep>[SYNTAX]: arrayLast(func(x[, y1, ..., yN]), source[, cond1, ... , condN_arr])<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - func(x[, y1, ..., yN]) вҖ” A lambda function which operates on elements of the source array ( x ) and condition arrays ( y ). Lambda function . - source вҖ” The source array to process. Array(T) . - [, cond1, ... , condN] вҖ” Optional. N condition arrays providing additional arguments to the lambda function. Array(T) .
Returned value
Returns the last element of the source array for which func is true, otherwise returns the default value of T .<sep>[RETURN]: Returns the last element of the source array for which func is true, otherwise returns the default value of T .<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT arrayLast(x, y -> x=y, ['a', 'b', 'c'], ['a', 'b', 'c']) ; c ; SELECT arrayFirst(x, y -> x=y, [0, 1, 2], [3, 3, 3]) AS res, toTypeName(res)
example: SELECT arrayFirst(x, y -> x=y, [0, 1, 2], [3, 3, 3]) AS res, toTypeName(res)

description:
Introduced in: v23.2
This function is the same as arrayReverseSort but with an additional limit argument allowing partial sorting.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Introduced in: v23.2
This function is the same as arrayReverseSort but with an additional limit argument allowing partial sorting.<sep>[SYNTAX]: arrayPartialReverseSort([f,] arr [, arr1, ... ,arrN], limit)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - f(arr[, arr1, ... ,arrN]) вҖ” The lambda function to apply to elements of array x . Lambda function
- arr вҖ” Array to be sorted. Array(T)
- arr1, ... ,arrN вҖ” N additional arrays, in the case when f accepts multiple arguments. Array(T)
- limit вҖ” Index value up until which sorting will occur. (U)Int*
Returned value
Returns an array of the same size as the original array where elements in the range [1..limit] are sorted in descending order. The remaining elements (limit..N] are in an unspecified order.<sep>[RETURN]: Returns an array of the same size as the original array where elements in the range [1..limit] are sorted in descending order. The remaining elements (limit..N] are in an unspecified order.<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT arrayPartialReverseSort(2, [5, 9, 1, 3]) ; [9, 5, 1, 3] ; SELECT arrayPartialReverseSort(2, ['expenses','lasso','embolism','gladly'])

description:
Introduced in: v23.2
Returns an array of the same size as the original array where elements in range [1..limit] are a random subset of the original array. Remaining (limit..n] shall contain the elements not in [1..limit] range in undefined order. Value of limit shall be in range [1..n] . Values outside of that range are equivalent to performing full arrayShuffle :
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Introduced in: v23.2
Returns an array of the same size as the original array where elements in range [1..limit] are a random subset of the original array. Remaining (limit..n] shall contain the elements not in [1..limit] range in undefined order. Value of limit shall be in range [1..n] . Values outside of that range are equivalent to performing full arrayShuffle :<sep>[SYNTAX]: arrayPartialShuffle(arr [, limit[, seed]])<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - arr вҖ” The array to shuffle. Array(T)
- seed вҖ” Optional. The seed to be used with random number generation. If not provided, a random one is used. (U)Int*
- limit вҖ” Optional. The number to limit element swaps to, in the range [1..N] . (U)Int*
Returned value

description:
Introduced in: v23.2
This function is the same as arraySort but with an additional limit argument allowing partial sorting.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Introduced in: v23.2
This function is the same as arraySort but with an additional limit argument allowing partial sorting.<sep>[SYNTAX]: arrayPartialSort([f,] arr [, arr1, ... ,arrN], limit)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - f(arr[, arr1, ... ,arrN]) вҖ” The lambda function to apply to elements of array x . Lambda function
- arr вҖ” Array to be sorted. Array(T)
- arr1, ... ,arrN вҖ” N additional arrays, in the case when f accepts multiple arguments. Array(T)
- limit вҖ” Index value up until which sorting will occur. (U)Int*
Returned value
Returns an array of the same size as the original array where elements in the range [1..limit] are sorted in ascending order. The remaining elements (limit..N] are in an unspecified order.<sep>[RETURN]: Returns an array of the same size as the original array where elements in the range [1..limit] are sorted in ascending order. The remaining elements (limit..N] are in an unspecified order.<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT arrayPartialSort(2, [5, 9, 1, 3]) ; [1, 3, 5, 9] ; SELECT arrayPartialSort(2, ['expenses', 'lasso', 'embolism', 'gladly'])

description:
Introduced in: v20.4
Calculates the area under the receiver operating characteristic (ROC) curve. A ROC curve is created by plotting True Positive Rate (TPR) on the y-axis and False Positive Rate (FPR) on the x-axis across all thresholds. The resulting value ranges from zero to one, with a higher value indicating better model performance.
The ROC AUC (also known as simply AUC) is a concept in machine learning. For more details, please see here , here and here .
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Introduced in: v20.4
Calculates the area under the receiver operating characteristic (ROC) curve. A ROC curve is created by plotting True Positive Rate (TPR) on the y-axis and False Positive Rate (FPR) on the x-axis across all thresholds. The resulting value ranges from zero to one, with a higher value indicating better model performance.
The ROC AUC (also known as simply AUC) is a concept in machine learning. For more details, please see here , here and here .<sep>[SYNTAX]: arrayROCAUC(scores, labels[, scale[, partial_offsets]])<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - scores вҖ” Scores prediction model gives. Array((U)Int*) or Array(Float*)
- labels вҖ” Labels of samples, usually 1 for positive sample and 0 for negative sample. Array((U)Int*) or Enum
- scale вҖ” Optional. Decides whether to return the normalized area. If false, returns the area under the TP (true positives) x FP (false positives) curve instead. Default value: true. Bool
- partial_offsets вҖ”
- An array of four non-negative integers for calculating a partial area under the ROC curve (equivalent to a vertical band of the ROC space) instead of the whole AUC. This option is useful for distributed computation of the ROC AUC. The array must contain the following elements [ higher_partitions_tp , higher_partitions_fp , total_positives , total_negatives ]. Array of non-negative Integers . Optional. higher_partitions_tp : The number of positive labels in the higher-scored partitions. higher_partitions_fp : The number of negative labels in the higher-scored partitions. total_positives : The total number of positive samples in the entire dataset. total_negatives : The total number of negative samples in the entire dataset.
Returned value
Returns area under the receiver operating characteristic (ROC) curve. Float64<sep>[RETURN]: Returns area under the receiver operating characteristic (ROC) curve. Float64<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT arrayROCAUC([0.1, 0.4, 0.35, 0.8], [0, 0, 1, 1]); ; 0.75

description:
Introduced in: v23.10
Returns a subset with samples -many random elements of an input array. If samples exceeds the size of the input array, the sample size is limited to the size of the array, i.e. all array elements are returned but their order is not guaranteed. The function can handle both flat arrays and nested arrays.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Introduced in: v23.10
Returns a subset with samples -many random elements of an input array. If samples exceeds the size of the input array, the sample size is limited to the size of the array, i.e. all array elements are returned but their order is not guaranteed. The function can handle both flat arrays and nested arrays.<sep>[SYNTAX]: arrayRandomSample(arr, samples)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - arr вҖ” The input array or multidimensional array from which to sample elements. Array(T)
- samples вҖ” The number of elements to include in the random sample. (U)Int*
Returned value
An array containing a random sample of elements from the input array Array(T)<sep>[RETURN]: An array containing a random sample of elements from the input array Array(T)<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT arrayRandomSample(['apple', 'banana', 'cherry', 'date'], 2) as res; ; ['cherry','apple'] ; SELECT arrayRandomSample([[1, 2], [3, 4], [5, 6]], 2) as res;

description:
Introduced in: v1.1
Applies an aggregate function to array elements and returns its result. The name of the aggregation function is passed as a string in single quotes 'max' , 'sum' . When using parametric aggregate functions, the parameter is indicated after the function name in parentheses 'uniqUpTo(6)' .
detail:

Applies an aggregate function to array elements and returns its result. The name of the aggregation function is passed as a string in single quotes 'max' , 'sum' . When using parametric aggregate functions, the parameter is indicated after the function name in parentheses 'uniqUpTo(6)' .<sep>[SYNTAX]: arrayReduce(agg_f, arr1 [, arr2, ... , arrN)]<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - agg_f вҖ” The name of an aggregate function which should be a constant. String
- arr1 [, arr2, ... , arrN)] вҖ” N arrays corresponding to the arguments of agg_f . Array(T)
Returned value
Returns the result of the aggregate function<sep>[RETURN]: Returns the result of the aggregate function<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT arrayReduce('max', [1, 2, 3]); ; в”Ңв”ҖarrayReduce('max', [1, 2, 3])в”Җв”җ ; в”Ңв”ҖarrayReduce('maxIf', [3, 5], [1, 0])в”Җв”җ

description:
Introduced in: v20.4
Applies an aggregate function to array elements in the given ranges and returns an array containing the result corresponding to each range. The function will return the same result as multiple arrayReduce(agg_func, arraySlice(arr1, index, length), ...) .
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Introduced in: v20.4
Applies an aggregate function to array elements in the given ranges and returns an array containing the result corresponding to each range. The function will return the same result as multiple arrayReduce(agg_func, arraySlice(arr1, index, length), ...) .<sep>[SYNTAX]: arrayReduceInRanges(agg_f, ranges, arr1 [, arr2, ... ,arrN)]<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - agg_f вҖ” The name of the aggregate function to use. String
- ranges вҖ” The range over which to aggregate. An array of tuples, (i, r) containing the index i from which to begin from and the range r over which to aggregate. Array(T) or Tuple(T)
- arr1 [, arr2, ... ,arrN)] вҖ” N arrays as arguments to the aggregate function. Array(T)
Returned value
Returns an array containing results of the aggregate function over the specified ranges Array(T)<sep>[RETURN]: Returns an array containing results of the aggregate function over the specified ranges Array(T)<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT arrayReduceInRanges( ; в”Ңв”Җresв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ

description:

- size вҖ” -The new length of the array. If size is less than the original size of the array, the array is truncated from the right. If size is larger than the initial size of the array, the array is extended to the right with extender values or default values for the data type of the array items.
- extender вҖ” Value to use for extending the array. Can be NULL .

tree: arrayReverseFill(func(x[, y1, ..., yN]), source_arr[, cond1_arr, ... , condN_arr])
description:
Introduced in: v20.1
The arrayReverseFill function sequentially processes a source array from the last element to the first, evaluating a lambda condition at each position using elements from the source and condition arrays. When the condition evaluates to false at position i, the function replaces that element with the element at position i+1 from the current state of the array. The last element is always preserved regardless of any condition.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Introduced in: v20.1
The arrayReverseFill function sequentially processes a source array from the last element to the first, evaluating a lambda condition at each position using elements from the source and condition arrays. When the condition evaluates to false at position i, the function replaces that element with the element at position i+1 from the current state of the array. The last element is always preserved regardless of any condition.<sep>[SYNTAX]: arrayReverseFill(func(x[, y1, ..., yN]), source_arr[, cond1_arr, ... , condN_arr])<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - func(x[, y1, ..., yN]) вҖ” A lambda function which operates on elements of the source array ( x ) and condition arrays ( y ). Lambda function
- source_arr вҖ” The source array to process. Array(T)
- [, cond1_arr, ... , condN_arr] вҖ” Optional. N condition arrays providing additional arguments to the lambda function. Array(T)
Returned value
Returns an array with elements of the source array replaced by the results of the lambda. Array(T)<sep>[RETURN]: Returns an array with elements of the source array replaced by the results of the lambda. Array(T)<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT arrayReverseFill(x -> not isNull(x), [1, null, 2, null]) AS res ; [1, 2, 2, NULL] ; SELECT arrayReverseFill(x, y, z -> x > y AND x < z, [5, 3, 6, 2], [4, 7, 1, 3], [10, 2, 8, 5]) AS res;
example: SELECT arrayReverseFill(x -> not isNull(x), [1, null, 2, null]) AS res
SELECT arrayReverseFill(x, y, z -> x > y AND x < z, [5, 3, 6, 2], [4, 7, 1, 3], [10, 2, 8, 5]) AS res;

description:
Introduced in: v1.1
Sorts the elements of an array in descending order. If a function f is specified, the provided array is sorted according to the result of the function applied to the elements of the array, and then the sorted array is reversed. If f accepts multiple arguments, the arrayReverseSort function is passed several arrays that the arguments of func will correspond to.
If the array to sort contains -Inf , NULL , NaN , or Inf they will be sorted in the following order:
- -Inf
- Inf
- NaN
- NULL
arrayReverseSort is a higher-order function .
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Introduced in: v1.1
Sorts the elements of an array in descending order. If a function f is specified, the provided array is sorted according to the result of the function applied to the elements of the array, and then the sorted array is reversed. If f accepts multiple arguments, the arrayReverseSort function is passed several arrays that the arguments of func will correspond to.
If the array to sort contains -Inf , NULL , NaN , or Inf they will be sorted in the following order:
- -Inf
- Inf
- NaN
- NULL
arrayReverseSort is a higher-order function .<sep>[SYNTAX]: arrayReverseSort([f,] arr [, arr1, ... ,arrN)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - f(y1[, y2 ... yN]) вҖ” The lambda function to apply to elements of array x . - arr вҖ” An array to be sorted. Array(T) - arr1, ..., yN вҖ” Optional. N additional arrays, in the case when f accepts multiple arguments.
Returned value
Returns the array x sorted in descending order if no lambda function is provided, otherwise it returns an array sorted according to the logic of the provided lambda function, and then reversed. Array(T) .<sep>[RETURN]: Returns the array x sorted in descending order if no lambda function is provided, otherwise it returns an array sorted according to the logic of the provided lambda function, and then reversed. Array(T) .<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT arrayReverseSort((x, y) -> y, [4, 3, 5], ['a', 'b', 'c']) AS res; ; [5,3,4] ; SELECT arrayReverseSort((x, y) -> -y, [4, 3, 5], [1, 2, 3]) AS res;
example: SELECT arrayReverseSort((x, y) -> y, [4, 3, 5], ['a', 'b', 'c']) AS res;

tree: arrayReverseSplit(func(x[, y1, ..., yN]), source_arr[, cond1_arr, ... , condN_arr])
description:
Introduced in: v20.1
Split a source array into multiple arrays. When func(x[, y1, ..., yN]) returns something other than zero, the array will be split to the right of the element. The array will not be split after the last element.

detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Introduced in: v1.1
Returns a slice of the array, with NULL elements included.<sep>[SYNTAX]: arraySlice(arr, offset [, length])<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - arr вҖ” Array to slice. Array(T)
- offset вҖ” Indent from the edge of the array. A positive value indicates an offset on the left, and a negative value is an indent on the right. Numbering of the array items begins with 1 . (U)Int*
- length вҖ” The length of the required slice. If you specify a negative value, the function returns an open slice [offset, array_length - length] . If you omit the value, the function returns the slice [offset, the_end_of_array] . (U)Int*
Returned value
Returns a slice of the array with length elements from the specified offset Array(T)<sep>[RETURN]: Returns a slice of the array with length elements from the specified offset Array(T)<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT arraySlice([1, 2, NULL, 4, 5], 2, 3) AS res; ; [2, NULL, 4]

description:
Introduced in: v1.1
Sorts the elements of the provided array in ascending order. If a lambda function f is specified, sorting order is determined by the result of the lambda applied to each element of the array. If the lambda accepts multiple arguments, the arraySort function is passed several arrays that the arguments of f will correspond to.
If the array to sort contains -Inf , NULL , NaN , or Inf they will be sorted in the following order:
- -Inf
- Inf
- NaN
- NULL
arraySort is a higher-order function .
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Introduced in: v1.1
Sorts the elements of the provided array in ascending order. If a lambda function f is specified, sorting order is determined by the result of the lambda applied to each element of the array. If the lambda accepts multiple arguments, the arraySort function is passed several arrays that the arguments of f will correspond to.
If the array to sort contains -Inf , NULL , NaN , or Inf they will be sorted in the following order:
- -Inf
- Inf
- NaN
- NULL
arraySort is a higher-order function .<sep>[SYNTAX]: arraySort([f,] arr [, arr1, ... ,arrN])<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - f(y1[, y2 ... yN]) вҖ” The lambda function to apply to elements of array x . - arr вҖ” An array to be sorted. Array(T) - arr1, ..., yN вҖ” Optional. N additional arrays, in the case when f accepts multiple arguments.
Returned value
Returns the array arr sorted in ascending order if no lambda function is provided, otherwise it returns an array sorted according to the logic of the provided lambda function. Array(T) .<sep>[RETURN]: Returns the array arr sorted in ascending order if no lambda function is provided, otherwise it returns an array sorted according to the logic of the provided lambda function. Array(T) .<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT arraySort([1, 3, 3, 0]); ; [0,1,3,3] ; SELECT arraySort(['hello', 'world', '!']);
example: SELECT arraySort([1, nan, 2, NULL, 3, nan, -4, NULL, inf, -inf]);

tree: arraySplit(func(x[, y1, ..., yN]), source_arr[, cond1_arr, ... , condN_arr])
description:
Introduced in: v20.1
Split a source array into multiple arrays. When func(x [, y1, ..., yN]) returns something other than zero, the array will be split to the left of the element. The array will not be split before the first element.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Introduced in: v20.1
Split a source array into multiple arrays. When func(x [, y1, ..., yN]) returns something other than zero, the array will be split to the left of the element. The array will not be split before the first element.<sep>[SYNTAX]: arraySplit(func(x[, y1, ..., yN]), source_arr[, cond1_arr, ... , condN_arr])<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - func(x[, y1, ..., yN]) вҖ” A lambda function which operates on elements of the source array ( x ) and condition arrays ( y ). Lambda function . - source_arr вҖ” The source array to split Array(T) . - [, cond1_arr, ... , condN_arr] вҖ” Optional. N condition arrays providing additional arguments to the lambda function. Array(T) .
Returned value
Returns an array of arrays Array(Array(T))<sep>[RETURN]: Returns an array of arrays Array(Array(T))<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT arraySplit((x, y) -> y, [1, 2, 3, 4, 5], [1, 0, 0, 1, 0]) AS res ; [[1, 2, 3], [4, 5]]
example: SELECT arraySplit((x, y) -> y, [1, 2, 3, 4, 5], [1, 0, 0, 1, 0]) AS res

tree: arrayMax([func(x[, y1, ..., yN])], source_arr[, cond1_arr, ... , condN_arr])
description:
Introduced in: v21.1
Returns the sum of elements in the source array.
If a lambda function func is specified, returns the sum of elements of the lambda results.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Introduced in: v21.1
Returns the sum of elements in the source array.
If a lambda function func is specified, returns the sum of elements of the lambda results.<sep>[SYNTAX]: arrayMax([func(x[, y1, ..., yN])], source_arr[, cond1_arr, ... , condN_arr])<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - func(x[, y1, ..., yN]) вҖ” Optional. A lambda function which operates on elements of the source array ( x ) and condition arrays ( y ). Lambda function
- source_arr вҖ” The source array to process. Array(T)
- , cond1_arr, ... , condN_arr] вҖ” Optional. N condition arrays providing additional arguments to the lambda function. Array(T)
Returned value
Returns the sum of elements in the source array, or the sum of elements of the lambda results if provided.<sep>[RETURN]: Returns the sum of elements in the source array, or the sum of elements of the lambda results if provided.<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT arraySum([1, 2, 3, 4]); ; 10 ; SELECT arraySum(x, y -> x+y, [1, 1, 1, 1], [1, 1, 1, 1]);

description:
Introduced in: v1.1
For a single argument passed, counts the number of different elements in the array. For multiple arguments passed, it counts the number of different tuples made of elements at matching positions across multiple arrays.
For example SELECT arrayUniq([1,2], [3,4], [5,6]) will form the following tuples:
- Position 1: (1,3,5)
- Position 2: (2,4,6)
It will then count the number of unique tuples. In this case 2 .
All arrays passed must have the same length.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Introduced in: v1.1
For a single argument passed, counts the number of different elements in the array. For multiple arguments passed, it counts the number of different tuples made of elements at matching positions across multiple arrays.
For example SELECT arrayUniq([1,2], [3,4], [5,6]) will form the following tuples:
- Position 1: (1,3,5)
- Position 2: (2,4,6)
It will then count the number of unique tuples. In this case 2 .
All arrays passed must have the same length.<sep>[SYNTAX]: arrayUniq(arr1[, arr2, ..., arrN])<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - arr1 вҖ” Array for which to count the number of unique elements. Array(T)
- [, arr2, ..., arrN] вҖ” Optional. Additional arrays used to count the number of unique tuples of elements at corresponding positions in multiple arrays. Array(T)
Returned value
For a single argument returns the number of unique elements. For multiple arguments returns the number of unique tuples made from elements at corresponding positions across the arrays. UInt32<sep>[RETURN]: For a single argument returns the number of unique elements. For multiple arguments returns the number of unique tuples made from elements at corresponding positions across the arrays. UInt32<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT arrayUniq([1, 1, 2, 2]) ; 2 ; SELECT arrayUniq([1, 2, 3, 1], [4, 5, 6, 4])

description:

detail:

description:
Introduced in: v1.1
Checks whether one array is a subset of another.
- An empty array is a subset of any array.
- Null is processed as a value.
- The order of values in both the arrays does not matter.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Introduced in: v1.1
Checks whether one array is a subset of another.
- An empty array is a subset of any array.
- Null is processed as a value.
- The order of values in both the arrays does not matter.<sep>[SYNTAX]: hasAll(set, subset)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - set вҖ” Array of any type with a set of elements. Array(T)
- subset вҖ” Array of any type that shares a common supertype with set containing elements that should be tested to be a subset of set . Array(T)
Returned value
- 1 , if set contains all of the elements from subset .
- 0 , otherwise.
Raises a NO_COMMON_TYPE exception if the set and subset elements do not share a common supertype.<sep>[RETURN]: - 1 , if set contains all of the elements from subset .
- 0 , otherwise.
Raises a NO_COMMON_TYPE exception if the set and subset elements do not share a common supertype.<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT hasAll([], []) ; 1 ; SELECT hasAll([1, Null], [Null])

description:
Introduced in: v20.6
Checks whether all the elements of array2 appear in a array1 in the same exact order. Therefore, the function will return 1 , if and only if array1 = prefix + array2 + suffix.
In other words, the functions will check whether all the elements of array2 are contained in array1 like the hasAll function. In addition, it will check that the elements are observed in the same order in both array1 and array2.
- The function will return 1 if array2 is empty.
- Null is processed as a value. In other words hasSubstr([1, 2, NULL, 3, 4], [2,3]) will return 0 . However, hasSubstr([1, 2, NULL, 3, 4], [2,NULL,3]) will return 1
- The order of values in both the arrays does matter.
Raises a NO_COMMON_TYPE exception if any of the elements of the two arrays do not share a common supertype.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Introduced in: v20.6
Checks whether all the elements of array2 appear in a array1 in the same exact order. Therefore, the function will return 1 , if and only if array1 = prefix + array2 + suffix.
In other words, the functions will check whether all the elements of array2 are contained in array1 like the hasAll function. In addition, it will check that the elements are observed in the same order in both array1 and array2.
- The function will return 1 if array2 is empty.
- Null is processed as a value. In other words hasSubstr([1, 2, NULL, 3, 4], [2,3]) will return 0 . However, hasSubstr([1, 2, NULL, 3, 4], [2,NULL,3]) will return 1
- The order of values in both the arrays does matter.
Raises a NO_COMMON_TYPE exception if any of the elements of the two arrays do not share a common supertype.<sep>[SYNTAX]: hasSubstr(arr1, arr2)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - arr1 вҖ” Array of any type with a set of elements. Array(T)
- arr2 вҖ” Array of any type with a set of elements. Array(T)
Returned value
Returns 1 if array arr1 contains array arr2 . Otherwise, returns 0 . UInt8<sep>[RETURN]: Returns 1 if array arr1 contains array arr2 . Otherwise, returns 0 . UInt8<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT hasSubstr([], []) ; 1 ; SELECT hasSubstr([1, Null], [Null])

description:
Introduced in: v1.1
Calculates the length of a string or array.
- For String or FixedString arguments: calculates the number of bytes in the string.
- For Array arguments: calculates the number of elements in the array.
- If applied to a FixedString argument, the function is a constant expression.
Please note that the number of bytes in a string is not the same as the number of Unicode "code points" and it is not the same as the number of Unicode "grapheme clusters" (what we usually call "characters") and it is not the same as the visible string width.
It is ok to have ASCII NULL bytes in strings, and they will be counted as well.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Introduced in: v1.1
Calculates the length of a string or array.
- For String or FixedString arguments: calculates the number of bytes in the string.
- For Array arguments: calculates the number of elements in the array.
- If applied to a FixedString argument, the function is a constant expression.
Please note that the number of bytes in a string is not the same as the number of Unicode "code points" and it is not the same as the number of Unicode "grapheme clusters" (what we usually call "characters") and it is not the same as the visible string width.
It is ok to have ASCII NULL bytes in strings, and they will be counted as well.<sep>[SYNTAX]: length(x)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - x вҖ” Value for which to calculate the number of bytes (for String/FixedString) or elements (for Array). String or FixedString or Array(T)
Returned value
Returns the number of number of bytes in the String/FixedString x / the number of elements in array x UInt64<sep>[RETURN]: Returns the number of number of bytes in the String/FixedString x / the number of elements in array x UInt64<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT length('Hello, world!') ; 13 ; SELECT length(['Hello', 'world'])
example:
WITH 'hello' || toString(number) AS str
SELECT str,
isConstant(length(str)) AS str_length_is_constant,
isConstant(length(str::FixedString(6))) AS fixed_str_length_is_constant
FROM numbers(3)

в”Ңв”Җstrв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җstr_length_is_constantв”Җв”¬в”Җfixed_str_length_is_constantв”Җв”җ
в”Ӯ hello0 в”Ӯ                      0 в”Ӯ                            1 в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ hello1 в”Ӯ                      0 в”Ӯ                            1 в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ hello2 в”Ӯ                      0 в”Ӯ                            1 в”Ӯ

SELECT 'С‘Р»РәР°' AS str1, length(str1), lengthUTF8(str1), normalizeUTF8NFKD(str1) AS str2, length(str2), lengthUTF8(str2)

description:
Introduced in: v1.1
Checks whether the input array is non-empty.
An array is considered non-empty if it contains at least one element.
The function also works for strings or UUID .
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Introduced in: v1.1
Checks whether the input array is non-empty.
An array is considered non-empty if it contains at least one element.
The function also works for strings or UUID .<sep>[SYNTAX]: notEmpty(arr)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - arr вҖ” Input array. Array(T)
Returned value
Returns 1 for a non-empty array or 0 for an empty array UInt8<sep>[RETURN]: Returns 1 for a non-empty array or 0 for an empty array UInt8<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT notEmpty([1,2]); ; 1

description:
Introduced in: v1.1
Returns an array of numbers from start to end - 1 by step .
The supported types are:
- UInt8/16/32/64
- Int8/16/32/64]
- All arguments start , end , step must be one of the above supported types. Elements of the returned array will be a super type of the arguments.
- An exception is thrown if the function returns an array with a total length more than the number of elements specified by setting function_range_max_elements_in_block .
- Returns NULL if any argument has Nullable(nothing) type. An exception is thrown if any argument has NULL value (Nullable(T) type).
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Introduced in: v1.1
Returns an array of numbers from start to end - 1 by step .
The supported types are:
- UInt8/16/32/64
- Int8/16/32/64]
- All arguments start , end , step must be one of the above supported types. Elements of the returned array will be a super type of the arguments.
- An exception is thrown if the function returns an array with a total length more than the number of elements specified by setting function_range_max_elements_in_block .
- Returns NULL if any argument has Nullable(nothing) type. An exception is thrown if any argument has NULL value (Nullable(T) type).<sep>[SYNTAX]: range([start, ] end [, step])<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - start вҖ” Optional. The first element of the array. Required if step is used. Default value: 0 . - end вҖ” Required. The number before which the array is constructed. - step вҖ” Optional. Determines the incremental step between each element in the array. Default value: 1 .
Returned value
Array of numbers from start to end - 1 by step . Array(T)<sep>[RETURN]: Array of numbers from start to end - 1 by step . Array(T)<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT range(5), range(1, 5), range(1, 5, 2), range(-1, 5, 2); ; в”Ңв”Җrange(5)в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җrange(1, 5)в”Җв”¬в”Җrange(1, 5, 2)в”Җв”¬в”Җrange(-1, 5, 2)в”Җв”җ
example:
в”Ңв”Җrange(5)в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җrange(1, 5)в”Җв”¬в”Җrange(1, 5, 2)в”Җв”¬в”Җrange(-1, 5, 2)в”Җв”җ
в”Ӯ [0,1,2,3,4] в”Ӯ [1,2,3,4]   в”Ӯ [1,3]          в”Ӯ [-1,1,3]        в”Ӯ
в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ

description:

detail:

Reverses the order of the elements in the input array or the characters in the input string.<sep>[SYNTAX]: reverse(arr | str)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - arr | str вҖ” The source array or string. Array(T) or String
Returned value
Returns an array or string with the order of elements or characters reversed.<sep>[RETURN]: Returns an array or string with the order of elements or characters reversed.<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT reverse([1, 2, 3, 4]); ; [4, 3, 2, 1] ; SELECT reverse('abcd');

description:
This is a very unusual function.
Normal functions do not change a set of rows, but just change the values in each row (map). Aggregate functions compress a set of rows (fold or reduce). The arrayJoin function takes each row and generates a set of rows (unfold).
This function takes an array as an argument, and propagates the source row to multiple rows for the number of elements in the array. All the values in columns are simply copied, except the values in the column where this function is applied; it is replaced with the corresponding array value.
For example:
The arrayJoin function affects all sections of the query, including the WHERE section. Notice in that the result of the query below is 2 , even though the subquery returned 1 row.
A query can use multiple arrayJoin functions. In this case, the transformation is performed multiple times and the rows are multiplied. For example:
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: This is a very unusual function.
Normal functions do not change a set of rows, but just change the values in each row (map). Aggregate functions compress a set of rows (fold or reduce). The arrayJoin function takes each row and generates a set of rows (unfold).
This function takes an array as an argument, and propagates the source row to multiple rows for the number of elements in the array. All the values in columns are simply copied, except the values in the column where this function is applied; it is replaced with the corresponding array value.
For example:
The arrayJoin function affects all sections of the query, including the WHERE section. Notice in that the result of the query below is 2 , even though the subquery returned 1 row.
A query can use multiple arrayJoin functions. In this case, the transformation is performed multiple times and the rows are multiplied. For example:<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT arrayJoin([1, 2, 3] AS src) AS dst, 'Hello', src ; в”Ңв”Җdstв”Җв”¬в”Җ\'Hello\'в”Җв”¬в”Җsrcв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ ; SELECT sum(1) AS impressions
example: SELECT arrayJoin([1, 2, 3] AS src) AS dst, 'Hello', src

в”Ңв”Җdstв”Җв”¬в”Җ\'Hello\'в”Җв”¬в”Җsrcв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
в”Ӯ   1 в”Ӯ Hello     в”Ӯ [1,2,3] в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ   2 в”Ӯ Hello     в”Ӯ [1,2,3] в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ   3 в”Ӯ Hello     в”Ӯ [1,2,3] в”Ӯ
в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ

SELECT
    sum(1) AS impressions,
    arrayJoin(cities) AS city,
    arrayJoin(browsers) AS browser
FROM
(
    SELECT
        ['Istanbul', 'Berlin', 'Babruysk'] AS cities,
        ['Firefox', 'Chrome', 'Chrome'] AS browsers
)
GROUP BY
    2,
    3

в”Ңв”Җimpressionsв”Җв”¬в”Җcityв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җbrowserв”Җв”җ
в”Ӯ           2 в”Ӯ Istanbul в”Ӯ Chrome  в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ           1 в”Ӯ Istanbul в”Ӯ Firefox в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ           2 в”Ӯ Berlin   в”Ӯ Chrome  в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ           1 в”Ӯ Berlin   в”Ӯ Firefox в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ           2 в”Ӯ Babruysk в”Ӯ Chrome  в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ           1 в”Ӯ Babruysk в”Ӯ Firefox в”Ӯ
в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ

SELECT
    sum(1) AS impressions,
    city,
    browser
FROM
(
    SELECT
        ['Istanbul', 'Berlin', 'Babruysk'] AS cities,
        ['Firefox', 'Chrome', 'Chrome'] AS browsers
)
ARRAY JOIN
    cities AS city,
    browsers AS browser
GROUP BY
    2,
    3

в”Ңв”Җimpressionsв”Җв”¬в”Җcityв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җbrowserв”Җв”җ
в”Ӯ           1 в”Ӯ Istanbul в”Ӯ Firefox в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ           1 в”Ӯ Berlin   в”Ӯ Chrome  в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ           1 в”Ӯ Babruysk в”Ӯ Chrome  в”Ӯ
в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ

SELECT
    sum(1) AS impressions,
    (arrayJoin(arrayZip(cities, browsers)) AS t).1 AS city,
    t.2 AS browser
FROM
(
    SELECT
        ['Istanbul', 'Berlin', 'Babruysk'] AS cities,
        ['Firefox', 'Chrome', 'Chrome'] AS browsers
)
GROUP BY
    2,
    3

Returned value

example:
в”Ңв”Җaв”Җв”¬в”Җbв”Җв”¬в”ҖbitAnd(a, b)в”Җв”җ
в”Ӯ 0 в”Ӯ 0 в”Ӯ            0 в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ 0 в”Ӯ 1 в”Ӯ            0 в”Ӯ

description:

Calculates the number of bits set to one in the binary representation of a number.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Introduced in: v20.3
Calculates the number of bits set to one in the binary representation of a number.<sep>[SYNTAX]: bitCount(x)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - x вҖ” An integer or float value. (U)Int* or Float*
Returned value
Returns the number of bits set to one in x . UInt8 .<sep>[RETURN]: Returns the number of bits set to one in x . UInt8 .<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT bin(333), bitCount(333); ; в”Ңв”Җbin(333)в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”ҖbitCount(333)в”Җв”җ

description:
Introduced in: v21.1
Returns the Hamming Distance between the bit representations of two numbers. Can be used with SimHash functions for detection of semi-duplicate strings. The smaller the distance, the more similar the strings are.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Introduced in: v21.1
Returns the Hamming Distance between the bit representations of two numbers. Can be used with SimHash functions for detection of semi-duplicate strings. The smaller the distance, the more similar the strings are.<sep>[SYNTAX]: bitHammingDistance(x, y)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - x вҖ” First number for Hamming distance calculation. (U)Int* or Float*
- y вҖ” Second number for Hamming distance calculation. (U)Int* or Float*
Returned value
Returns the hamming distance between x and y UInt8<sep>[RETURN]: Returns the hamming distance between x and y UInt8<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT bitHammingDistance(111, 121); ; в”Ңв”ҖbitHammingDistance(111, 121)в”Җв”җ

description:
Introduced in: v1.1
Performs the bitwise NOT operation.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Introduced in: v1.1
Performs the bitwise NOT operation.<sep>[SYNTAX]: bitNot(a)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - a вҖ” Value for which to apply bitwise NOT operation. (U)Int* or Float* or String
Returned value
Returns the result of ~a i.e a with bits flipped.<sep>[RETURN]: Returns the result of ~a i.e a with bits flipped.<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT ; в”Ңв”Җoriginalв”Җв”¬в”Җoriginal_binaryв”Җв”¬в”Җresultв”Җв”¬в”Җresult_binaryв”Җв”җ
example:
SELECT
    CAST('5', 'UInt8') AS original,
    bin(original) AS original_binary,
    bitNot(original) AS result,
    bin(bitNot(original)) AS result_binary;

Returned value

description:
Introduced in: v1.1
Rotate bits left by a certain number of positions. Bits that fall off wrap around to the right.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Introduced in: v1.1
Rotate bits left by a certain number of positions. Bits that fall off wrap around to the right.<sep>[SYNTAX]: bitRotateLeft(a, N)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - a вҖ” A value to rotate. (U)Int8/16/32/64
- N вҖ” The number of positions to rotate left. UInt8/16/32/64
Returned value
Returns the rotated value with type equal to that of a . (U)Int8/16/32/64<sep>[RETURN]: Returns the rotated value with type equal to that of a . (U)Int8/16/32/64<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT 99 AS a, bin(a), bitRotateLeft(a, 2) AS a_rotated, bin(a_rotated); ; в”Ңв”Җв”Җaв”Җв”¬в”Җbin(a)в”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җa_rotatedв”Җв”¬в”Җbin(a_rotated)в”Җв”җ
example: SELECT 99 AS a, bin(a), bitRotateLeft(a, 2) AS a_rotated, bin(a_rotated);

- N вҖ” The number of positions to rotate right. UInt8/16/32/64
Returned value
Returns the rotated value with type equal to that of a . (U)Int8/16/32/64<sep>[RETURN]: Returns the rotated value with type equal to that of a . (U)Int8/16/32/64<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT 99 AS a, bin(a), bitRotateRight(a, 2) AS a_rotated, bin(a_rotated); ; в”Ңв”Җв”Җaв”Җв”¬в”Җbin(a)в”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җa_rotatedв”Җв”¬в”Җbin(a_rotated)в”Җв”җ
example: SELECT 99 AS a, bin(a), bitRotateRight(a, 2) AS a_rotated, bin(a_rotated);

description:
Introduced in: v1.1
Shifts the binary representation of a value to the left by a specified number of bit positions.
A FixedString or a String is treated as a single multibyte value.
Bits of a FixedString value are lost as they are shifted out. On the contrary, a String value is extended with additional bytes, so no bits are lost.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Introduced in: v1.1
Shifts the binary representation of a value to the left by a specified number of bit positions.
A FixedString or a String is treated as a single multibyte value.
Bits of a FixedString value are lost as they are shifted out. On the contrary, a String value is extended with additional bytes, so no bits are lost.<sep>[SYNTAX]: bitShiftLeft(a, N)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - a вҖ” A value to shift. (U)Int* or String or FixedString
- N вҖ” The number of positions to shift. UInt8/16/32/64
Returned value
Returns the shifted value with type equal to that of a .<sep>[RETURN]: Returns the shifted value with type equal to that of a .<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT 99 AS a, bin(a), bitShiftLeft(a, 2) AS a_shifted, bin(a_shifted); ; в”Ңв”Җв”Җaв”Җв”¬в”Җbin(99)в”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җa_shiftedв”Җв”¬в”Җbin(bitShiftLeft(99, 2))в”Җв”җ ; SELECT 'abc' AS a, hex(a), bitShiftLeft(a, 4) AS a_shifted, hex(a_shifted);
example: SELECT 99 AS a, bin(a), bitShiftLeft(a, 2) AS a_shifted, bin(a_shifted);
SELECT 'abc' AS a, hex(a), bitShiftLeft(a, 4) AS a_shifted, hex(a_shifted);

в”Ңв”Җaв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җhex('abc')в”Җв”¬в”Җa_shiftedв”Җв”¬в”Җhex(bitShiftLeft('abc', 4))в”Җв”җ
в”Ӯ abc в”Ӯ 616263     в”Ӯ &0        в”Ӯ 06162630                    в”Ӯ
в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
SELECT toFixedString('abc', 3) AS a, hex(a), bitShiftLeft(a, 4) AS a_shifted, hex(a_shifted);

в”Ңв”Җaв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җhex(toFixedString('abc', 3))в”Җв”¬в”Җa_shiftedв”Җв”¬в”Җhex(bitShiftLeft(toFixedString('abc', 3), 4))в”Җв”җ
в”Ӯ abc в”Ӯ 616263                       в”Ӯ &0        в”Ӯ 162630                                        в”Ӯ
в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
