A FixedString or a String is treated as a single multibyte value.
Bits of a FixedString value are lost as they are shifted out. On the contrary, a String value is extended with additional bytes, so no bits are lost.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Introduced in: v1.1
Shifts the binary representation of a value to the right by a specified number of bit positions.
A FixedString or a String is treated as a single multibyte value.
Bits of a FixedString value are lost as they are shifted out. On the contrary, a String value is extended with additional bytes, so no bits are lost.<sep>[SYNTAX]: bitShiftRight(a, N)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - a вҖ” A value to shift. (U)Int* or String or FixedString
- N вҖ” The number of positions to shift. UInt8/16/32/64
Returned value
Returns the shifted value with type equal to that of a .<sep>[RETURN]: Returns the shifted value with type equal to that of a .<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT 101 AS a, bin(a), bitShiftRight(a, 2) AS a_shifted, bin(a_shifted); ; в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җaв”Җв”¬в”Җbin(101)в”Җв”¬в”Җa_shiftedв”Җв”¬в”Җbin(bitShiftRight(101, 2))в”Җв”җ ; SELECT 'abc' AS a, hex(a), bitShiftLeft(a, 4) AS a_shifted, hex(a_shifted);
example: SELECT 101 AS a, bin(a), bitShiftRight(a, 2) AS a_shifted, bin(a_shifted);
SELECT 'abc' AS a, hex(a), bitShiftLeft(a, 4) AS a_shifted, hex(a_shifted);

в”Ңв”Җaв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җhex('abc')в”Җв”¬в”Җa_shiftedв”Җв”¬в”Җhex(bitShiftRight('abc', 12))в”Җв”җ
в”Ӯ abc в”Ӯ 616263     в”Ӯ           в”Ӯ 0616                          в”Ӯ
в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
SELECT toFixedString('abc', 3) AS a, hex(a), bitShiftRight(a, 12) AS a_shifted, hex(a_shifted);

в”Ңв”Җaв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җhex(toFixedString('abc', 3))в”Җв”¬в”Җa_shiftedв”Җв”¬в”Җhex(bitShiftRight(toFixedString('abc', 3), 12))в”Җв”җ
в”Ӯ abc в”Ӯ 616263                       в”Ӯ           в”Ӯ 000616                                          в”Ӯ
в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ

description:
Introduced in: v22.2
Returns a substring starting with the bit from the 'offset' index that is 'length' bits long.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Introduced in: v22.2
Returns a substring starting with the bit from the 'offset' index that is 'length' bits long.<sep>[SYNTAX]: bitSlice(s, offset[, length])<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - s вҖ” The String or Fixed String to slice. String or FixedString
- offset вҖ” Returns the starting bit position (1-based indexing).
- Positive values: count from the beginning of the string.
- Negative values: count from the end of the string. (U)Int8/16/32/64 or Float*
- length вҖ” Optional. The number of bits to extract.
- Positive values: extract length bits.
- Negative values: extract from the offset to (string_length - |length|) .
- Omitted: extract from offset to end of string.
- If length is not a multiple of 8, the result is padded with zeros on the right. (U)Int8/16/32/64 or Float*
Returned value
Returns a string containing the extracted bits, represented as a binary sequence. The result is always padded to byte boundaries (multiples of 8 bits) String<sep>[RETURN]: Returns a string containing the extracted bits, represented as a binary sequence. The result is always padded to byte boundaries (multiples of 8 bits) String<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT bin('Hello'), bin(bitSlice('Hello', 1, 8)); ; в”Ңв”Җbin('Hello')в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җbin(bitSlice('Hello', 1, 8))в”Җв”җ
example:
в”Ңв”Җbin('Hello')в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җbin(bitSlice('Hello', 1, 8))в”Җв”җ
в”Ӯ 0100100001100101011011000110110001101111 в”Ӯ 01001000                     в”Ӯ
в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
в”Ңв”Җbin('Hello')в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җbin(bitSlice('Hello', 1, 2))в”Җв”җ
в”Ӯ 0100100001100101011011000110110001101111 в”Ӯ 01000000                     в”Ӯ
в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
в”Ңв”Җbin('Hello')в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җbin(bitSlice('Hello', 1, 9))в”Җв”җ
в”Ӯ 0100100001100101011011000110110001101111 в”Ӯ 0100100000000000             в”Ӯ
в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
в”Ңв”Җbin('Hello')в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җbin(bitSlice('Hello', -4, 8))в”Җв”җ
в”Ӯ 0100100001100101011011000110110001101111 в”Ӯ 11110000                      в”Ӯ
в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ

description:
Introduced in: v1.1
Takes any number and converts it into binary form , then returns the value of the bit at a specified position. Counting is done right-to-left, starting at 0.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Introduced in: v1.1
Takes any number and converts it into binary form , then returns the value of the bit at a specified position. Counting is done right-to-left, starting at 0.<sep>[SYNTAX]: bitTest(a, i)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - a вҖ” Number to convert. (U)Int8/16/32/64 or Float*
- i вҖ” Position of the bit to return. (U)Int8/16/32/64 or Float*
Returned value
Returns the value of the bit at position i in the binary representation of a UInt8<sep>[RETURN]: Returns the value of the bit at position i in the binary representation of a UInt8<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT bin(2), bitTest(2, 1); ; в”Ңв”Җbin(2)в”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”ҖbitTest(2, 1)в”Җв”җ

description:
Introduced in: v1.1
Returns result of the logical conjunction (AND operator) of all bits at the given positions. Counts right-to-left, starting at 0.
The logical AND between two bits is true if and only if both input bits are true.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Introduced in: v1.1
Returns result of the logical conjunction (AND operator) of all bits at the given positions. Counts right-to-left, starting at 0.
The logical AND between two bits is true if and only if both input bits are true.<sep>[SYNTAX]: bitTestAll(a, index1[, index2, ... , indexN])<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - a вҖ” An integer value. (U)Int8/16/32/64
- index1, ... вҖ” One or multiple positions of bits. (U)Int8/16/32/64
Returned value
Returns the result of the logical conjunction UInt8<sep>[RETURN]: Returns the result of the logical conjunction UInt8<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT bitTestAll(43, 0, 1, 3, 5); ; в”Ңв”Җbin(43)в”Җв”Җв”¬в”ҖbitTestAll(43, 0, 1, 3, 5)в”Җв”җ ; SELECT bitTestAll(43, 0, 1, 3, 5, 2);

description:
Introduced in: v1.1
Returns result of the logical disjunction (OR operator) of all bits at the given positions in a number. Counts right-to-left, starting at 0.
The logical OR between two bits is true if at least one of the input bits is true.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Introduced in: v1.1
Returns result of the logical disjunction (OR operator) of all bits at the given positions in a number. Counts right-to-left, starting at 0.
The logical OR between two bits is true if at least one of the input bits is true.<sep>[SYNTAX]: bitTestAny(a, index1[, index2, ... , indexN])<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - a вҖ” An integer value. (U)Int8/16/32/64
- index1, ... вҖ” One or multiple positions of bits. (U)Int8/16/32/64
Returned value
Returns the result of the logical disjunction UInt8<sep>[RETURN]: Returns the result of the logical disjunction UInt8<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT bitTestAny(43, 0, 2); ; в”Ңв”Җbin(43)в”Җв”Җв”¬в”ҖbitTestAny(43, 0, 2)в”Җв”җ ; SELECT bitTestAny(43, 4, 2);

description:
Introduced in: v1.1
Performs bitwise exclusive or (XOR) operation between two values.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Introduced in: v1.1
Performs bitwise exclusive or (XOR) operation between two values.<sep>[SYNTAX]: bitXor(a, b)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - a вҖ” First value. (U)Int* or Float*
- b вҖ” Second value. (U)Int* or Float*
Returned value
Returns the result of bitwise operation a XOR b<sep>[RETURN]: Returns the result of bitwise operation a XOR b<sep>[DEMO]: в”Ңв”Җaв”Җв”¬в”Җbв”Җв”¬в”ҖbitXor(a, b)в”Җв”җ
example:
в”Ңв”Җaв”Җв”¬в”Җbв”Җв”¬в”ҖbitXor(a, b)в”Җв”җ
в”Ӯ 0 в”Ӯ 0 в”Ӯ            0 в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ 0 в”Ӯ 1 в”Ӯ            1 в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ 1 в”Ӯ 0 в”Ӯ            1 в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ 1 в”Ӯ 1 в”Ӯ            0 в”Ӯ
в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ

description:
Introduced in: v20.1
Computes the logical conjunction (AND) of two bitmaps.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Introduced in: v20.1
Computes the logical conjunction (AND) of two bitmaps.<sep>[SYNTAX]: bitmapAnd(bitmap1, bitmap2)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - bitmap1 вҖ” First bitmap object. AggregateFunction(groupBitmap, T) . - bitmap2 вҖ” Second bitmap object. AggregateFunction(groupBitmap, T) .
Returned value
Returns a bitmap containing bits present in both input bitmaps AggregateFunction(groupBitmap, T)<sep>[RETURN]: Returns a bitmap containing bits present in both input bitmaps AggregateFunction(groupBitmap, T)<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT bitmapToArray(bitmapAnd(bitmapBuild([1, 2, 3]), bitmapBuild([3, 4, 5]))) AS res; ; в”Ңв”Җresв”Җв”җ

description:
Introduced in: v20.1
Returns the cardinality of the logical conjunction (AND) of two bitmaps.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Introduced in: v20.1
Returns the cardinality of the logical conjunction (AND) of two bitmaps.<sep>[SYNTAX]: bitmapAndCardinality(bitmap1, bitmap2)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - bitmap1 вҖ” First bitmap object. AggregateFunction(groupBitmap, T) . - bitmap2 вҖ” Second bitmap object. AggregateFunction(groupBitmap, T) .
Returned value
Returns the number of set bits in the intersection of the two bitmaps UInt64<sep>[RETURN]: Returns the number of set bits in the intersection of the two bitmaps UInt64<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT bitmapAndCardinality(bitmapBuild([1,2,3]), bitmapBuild([3,4,5])) AS res; ; в”Ңв”Җresв”Җв”җ

description:
Introduced in: v20.1
Computes the logical conjunction of two bitmaps and negates the result (AND-NOT).
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Introduced in: v20.1
Computes the logical conjunction of two bitmaps and negates the result (AND-NOT).<sep>[SYNTAX]: bitmapAndnot(bitmap1, bitmap2)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - bitmap1 вҖ” First bitmap object. AggregateFunction(groupBitmap, T) . - bitmap2 вҖ” Second bitmap object. AggregateFunction(groupBitmap, T) .
Returned value
Returns a bitmap containing set bits present in the first bitmap but not in the second AggregateFunction(groupBitmap, T)<sep>[RETURN]: Returns a bitmap containing set bits present in the first bitmap but not in the second AggregateFunction(groupBitmap, T)<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT bitmapToArray(bitmapAndnot(bitmapBuild([1, 2, 3]), bitmapBuild([3, 4, 5]))) AS res; ; в”Ңв”Җresв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ

description:
Introduced in: v20.1
Returns the cardinality of the AND-NOT operation of two bitmaps.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Introduced in: v20.1
Returns the cardinality of the AND-NOT operation of two bitmaps.<sep>[SYNTAX]: bitmapAndnotCardinality(bitmap1, bitmap2)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - bitmap1 вҖ” First bitmap object. AggregateFunction(groupBitmap, T) . - bitmap2 вҖ” Second bitmap object. AggregateFunction(groupBitmap, T) .
Returned value
Returns the number of set bits in the result of bitmap1 AND-NOT bitmap2 UInt64<sep>[RETURN]: Returns the number of set bits in the result of bitmap1 AND-NOT bitmap2 UInt64<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT bitmapAndnotCardinality(bitmapBuild([1,2,3]), bitmapBuild([3,4,5])) AS res; ; в”Ңв”Җresв”Җв”җ

description:
Introduced in: v20.1
Builds a bitmap from an unsigned integer array. It is the opposite of function bitmapToArray .
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Introduced in: v20.1
Builds a bitmap from an unsigned integer array. It is the opposite of function bitmapToArray .<sep>[SYNTAX]: bitmapBuild(array)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - array вҖ” Unsigned integer array. Array(UInt*)
Returned value
Returns a bitmap from the provided array AggregateFunction(groupBitmap, T)<sep>[RETURN]: Returns a bitmap from the provided array AggregateFunction(groupBitmap, T)<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT bitmapBuild([1, 2, 3, 4, 5]) AS res, toTypeName(res); ; в”Ңв”Җresв”Җв”¬в”ҖtoTypeName(bitmapBuild([1, 2, 3, 4, 5]))в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ

description:
Introduced in: v20.1
Returns the number of bits set (the cardinality) in the bitmap.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Introduced in: v20.1
Returns the number of bits set (the cardinality) in the bitmap.<sep>[SYNTAX]: bitmapCardinality(bitmap)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - bitmap вҖ” Bitmap object. AggregateFunction(groupBitmap, T) .
Returned value
Returns the number of bits set in the bitmap UInt64<sep>[RETURN]: Returns the number of bits set in the bitmap UInt64<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT bitmapCardinality(bitmapBuild([1, 3, 3, 5, 7, 7])) AS res ; в”Ңв”Җresв”Җв”җ

description:
Introduced in: v20.1
Checks if the bitmap contains a specific element.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Introduced in: v20.1
Checks if the bitmap contains a specific element.<sep>[SYNTAX]: bitmapContains(bitmap, value)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - bitmap вҖ” Bitmap object. AggregateFunction(groupBitmap, T) . - value вҖ” Element to check for. (U)Int8/16/32/64
Returned value
Returns 1 if the bitmap contains the specified value, otherwise 0 UInt8<sep>[RETURN]: Returns 1 if the bitmap contains the specified value, otherwise 0 UInt8<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT bitmapContains(bitmapBuild([1, 2, 3]), 2) AS res; ; в”Ңв”Җresв”Җв”җ

description:
Introduced in: v20.1
Checks if the first bitmap contains all set bits of the second bitmap.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Introduced in: v20.1
Checks if the first bitmap contains all set bits of the second bitmap.<sep>[SYNTAX]: bitmapHasAll(bitmap1, bitmap2)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - bitmap1 вҖ” First bitmap object. AggregateFunction(groupBitmap, T) . - bitmap2 вҖ” Second bitmap object. AggregateFunction(groupBitmap, T) .
Returned value
Returns 1 if all set bits of the second bitmap are present in the first bitmap, otherwise 0 UInt8<sep>[RETURN]: Returns 1 if all set bits of the second bitmap are present in the first bitmap, otherwise 0 UInt8<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT bitmapHasAll(bitmapBuild([1, 2, 3]), bitmapBuild([2, 3])) AS res; ; в”Ңв”Җresв”Җв”җ

description:
Introduced in: v20.1
Checks if the first bitmap contains any set bits of the second bitmap.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Introduced in: v20.1
Checks if the first bitmap contains any set bits of the second bitmap.<sep>[SYNTAX]: bitmapHasAny(bitmap1, bitmap2)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - bitmap1 вҖ” First bitmap object. AggregateFunction(groupBitmap, T) . - bitmap2 вҖ” Second bitmap object. AggregateFunction(groupBitmap, T) .
Returned value
Returns 1 if any bits of the second bitmap are present in the first bitmap, otherwise 0 UInt8<sep>[RETURN]: Returns 1 if any bits of the second bitmap are present in the first bitmap, otherwise 0 UInt8<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT bitmapHasAny(bitmapBuild([1, 2, 3]), bitmapBuild([3, 4, 5])) AS res; ; в”Ңв”Җresв”Җв”җ

description:

detail:

Returns the position of the greatest bit set in a bitmap, or 0 if the bitmap is empty.<sep>[SYNTAX]: bitmapMax(bitmap)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - bitmap вҖ” Bitmap object. AggregateFunction(groupBitmap, T) .
Returned value
Returns the position of the greatest bit set in the bitmap, otherwise 0 UInt64<sep>[RETURN]: Returns the position of the greatest bit set in the bitmap, otherwise 0 UInt64<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT bitmapMax(bitmapBuild([1, 2, 3, 4, 5])) AS res; ; в”Ңв”Җresв”Җв”җ

description:
Introduced in: v20.1
Returns the position of the smallest bit set in a bitmap. If all bits are unset, or UINT32_MAX ( UINT64_MAX if the bitmap contains more than 2^64 bits).
detail:

Returns the position of the smallest bit set in a bitmap. If all bits are unset, or UINT32_MAX ( UINT64_MAX if the bitmap contains more than 2^64 bits).<sep>[SYNTAX]: bitmapMin(bitmap)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - bitmap вҖ” Bitmap object. AggregateFunction(groupBitmap, T) .
Returned value
Returns the position of the smallest bit set in the bitmap, or UINT32_MAX / UINT64_MAX UInt64<sep>[RETURN]: Returns the position of the smallest bit set in the bitmap, or UINT32_MAX / UINT64_MAX UInt64<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT bitmapMin(bitmapBuild([3, 5, 2, 6])) AS res; ; в”Ңв”Җresв”Җв”җ

description:

Computes the logical disjunction (OR) of two bitmaps.<sep>[SYNTAX]: bitmapOr(bitmap1, bitmap2)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - bitmap1 вҖ” First bitmap object. AggregateFunction(groupBitmap, T) . - bitmap2 вҖ” Second bitmap object. AggregateFunction(groupBitmap, T) .

description:
Introduced in: v20.1
Returns the cardinality of the logical disjunction (OR) of two bitmaps.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Introduced in: v20.1
Returns the cardinality of the logical disjunction (OR) of two bitmaps.<sep>[SYNTAX]: bitmapOrCardinality(bitmap1, bitmap2)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - bitmap1 вҖ” First bitmap object. AggregateFunction(groupBitmap, T) . - bitmap2 вҖ” Second bitmap object. AggregateFunction(groupBitmap, T) .
Returned value
Returns the number of set bits in the union of the two bitmaps UInt64<sep>[RETURN]: Returns the number of set bits in the union of the two bitmaps UInt64<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT bitmapOrCardinality(bitmapBuild([1,2,3]), bitmapBuild([3,4,5])) AS res; ; в”Ңв”Җresв”Җв”җ

description:
The comparison functions below return 0 or 1 with type UInt8 . Only values within the same group can be compared (e.g. UInt16 and UInt64 ) but not across groups (e.g. UInt16 and DateTime ). Comparison of numbers and strings are possible, as is comparison of strings with dates and dates with times. For tuples and arrays, the comparison is lexicographic meaning that the comparison is made for each corresponding element of the left side and right side tuple/array.
The following types can be compared:
- numbers and decimals
- strings and fixed strings
- dates
- dates with times
- tuples (lexicographic comparison)
- arrays (lexicographic comparison)

description:
When NULL values are involved in conditionals, the result will also be NULL .
So you should construct your queries carefully if the types are Nullable .
The following example demonstrates this by failing to add equals condition to multiIf .
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: When NULL values are involved in conditionals, the result will also be NULL .
So you should construct your queries carefully if the types are Nullable .
The following example demonstrates this by failing to add equals condition to multiIf .<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT ; SELECT

SELECT
    NULL < 1,
    2 < NULL,
    NULL < NULL,
    NULL = NULL

в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ

description:
The CASE expression in ClickHouse provides conditional logic similar to the SQL CASE operator. It evaluates conditions and returns values based on the first matching condition.
ClickHouse supports two forms of CASE:
- CASE WHEN ... THEN ... ELSE ... END This form allows full flexibility and is internally implemented using the multiIf function. Each condition is evaluated independently, and expressions can include non-constant values.
- CASE <expr> WHEN <val1> THEN ... WHEN <val2> THEN ... ELSE ... END This more compact form is optimized for constant value matching and internally uses caseWithExpression() .
For example, the following is valid:
This form also does not require return expressions to be constants.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: The CASE expression in ClickHouse provides conditional logic similar to the SQL CASE operator. It evaluates conditions and returns values based on the first matching condition.
ClickHouse supports two forms of CASE:
- CASE WHEN ... THEN ... ELSE ... END This form allows full flexibility and is internally implemented using the multiIf function. Each condition is evaluated independently, and expressions can include non-constant values.
- CASE <expr> WHEN <val1> THEN ... WHEN <val2> THEN ... ELSE ... END This more compact form is optimized for constant value matching and internally uses caseWithExpression() .
For example, the following is valid:
This form also does not require return expressions to be constants.<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT ; SELECT ; SELECT
example:
SELECT
    number,
    CASE
        WHEN number % 2 = 0 THEN number + 1
        WHEN number % 2 = 1 THEN number * 10
        ELSE number
    END AS result
FROM system.numbers
WHERE number < 5;

-- is translated to
SELECT
    number,
    multiIf((number % 2) = 0, number + 1, (number % 2) = 1, number * 10, number) AS result
FROM system.numbers
WHERE number < 5

в”Ңв”Җnumberв”Җв”¬в”Җresultв”Җв”җ
в”Ӯ      0 в”Ӯ      1 в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ      1 в”Ӯ     10 в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ      2 в”Ӯ      3 в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ      3 в”Ӯ     30 в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ      4 в”Ӯ      5 в”Ӯ
в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ

SELECT
    number,
    CASE number
        WHEN 0 THEN 100
        WHEN 1 THEN 200
        ELSE 0
    END AS result
FROM system.numbers
WHERE number < 3;

-- is translated to

SELECT
    number,
    caseWithExpression(number, 0, 100, 1, 200, 0) AS result
FROM system.numbers
WHERE number < 3

в”Ңв”Җnumberв”Җв”¬в”Җresultв”Җв”җ
в”Ӯ      0 в”Ӯ    100 в”Ӯ

SELECT
    number,
    CASE
        WHEN number = 0 THEN fromUnixTimestamp64Milli(0, 'Asia/Kolkata')
        WHEN number = 1 THEN fromUnixTimestamp64Milli(0, 'America/Los_Angeles')
        ELSE fromUnixTimestamp64Milli(0, 'UTC')
    END AS tz
FROM system.numbers
WHERE number < 3;

-- is translated to

SELECT
    number,
    multiIf(number = 0, fromUnixTimestamp64Milli(0, 'Asia/Kolkata'), number = 1, fromUnixTimestamp64Milli(0, 'America/Los_Angeles'), fromUnixTimestamp64Milli(0, 'UTC')) AS tz
FROM system.numbers
WHERE number < 3

в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ

    ) AS tz

FROM system.numbers
WHERE number < 3

description:
Introduced in: v24.5
Restricts a value to be within the specified minimum and maximum bounds.
If the value is less than the minimum, returns the minimum. If the value is greater than the maximum, returns the maximum. Otherwise, returns the value itself.
All arguments must be of comparable types. The result type is the largest compatible type among all arguments.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Introduced in: v24.5
Restricts a value to be within the specified minimum and maximum bounds.
If the value is less than the minimum, returns the minimum. If the value is greater than the maximum, returns the maximum. Otherwise, returns the value itself.
All arguments must be of comparable types. The result type is the largest compatible type among all arguments.<sep>[SYNTAX]: clamp(value, min, max)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - value вҖ” The value to clamp. - min вҖ” The minimum bound. - max вҖ” The maximum bound.
Returned value
Returns the value, restricted to the [min, max] range.<sep>[RETURN]: Returns the value, restricted to the [min, max] range.<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT clamp(5, 1, 10) AS result; ; в”Ңв”Җresultв”Җв”җ ; SELECT clamp(-3, 0, 7) AS result;

description:
Introduced in: v1.1
Returns the greatest value among the arguments.
- For arrays, returns the lexicographically greatest array.
- For DateTime types, the result type is promoted to the largest type (e.g., DateTime64 if mixed with DateTime32).
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Introduced in: v1.1
Returns the greatest value among the arguments.
- For arrays, returns the lexicographically greatest array.
- For DateTime types, the result type is promoted to the largest type (e.g., DateTime64 if mixed with DateTime32).<sep>[SYNTAX]: greatest(x1[, x2, ..., xN])<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - x1[, x2, ..., xN] вҖ” One or multiple values to compare. All arguments must be of comparable types.
Returned value
The greatest value among the arguments, promoted to the largest compatible type.<sep>[RETURN]: The greatest value among the arguments, promoted to the largest compatible type.<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT greatest(1, 2, toUInt8(3), 3.) AS result, toTypeName(result) AS type; ; в”Ңв”Җresultв”Җв”¬в”Җtypeв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ ; SELECT greatest(['hello'], ['there'], ['world']);
example:
SELECT greatest(1, 2, toUInt8(3), 3.) AS result, toTypeName(result) AS type;
-- The type returned is a Float64 as the UInt8 must be promoted to 64 bit for the comparison.

SELECT greatest(toDateTime32(now() + toIntervalDay(1)), toDateTime64(now(), 3));
-- The type returned is a DateTime64 as the DateTime32 must be promoted to 64 bit for the comparison.

description:
Introduced in: v1.1
Performs conditional branching.
- If the condition cond evaluates to a non-zero value, the function returns the result of the expression then .
- If cond evaluates to zero or NULL, the result of the else expression is returned.
The setting short_circuit_function_evaluation controls whether short-circuit evaluation is used.
If enabled, the then expression is evaluated only on rows where cond is true and the else expression where cond is false.
For example, with short-circuit evaluation, no division-by-zero exception is thrown when executing the following query:
then and else must be of a similar type.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Introduced in: v1.1
Performs conditional branching.
- If the condition cond evaluates to a non-zero value, the function returns the result of the expression then .
- If cond evaluates to zero or NULL, the result of the else expression is returned.
The setting short_circuit_function_evaluation controls whether short-circuit evaluation is used.
If enabled, the then expression is evaluated only on rows where cond is true and the else expression where cond is false.
For example, with short-circuit evaluation, no division-by-zero exception is thrown when executing the following query:
then and else must be of a similar type.<sep>[SYNTAX]: if(cond, then, else)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - cond вҖ” The evaluated condition. UInt8 or Nullable(UInt8) or NULL
- then вҖ” The expression returned if cond is true. - else вҖ” The expression returned if cond is false or NULL .
Returned value
The result of either the then or else expressions, depending on condition cond .<sep>[RETURN]: The result of either the then or else expressions, depending on condition cond .<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT if(number = 0, 0, intDiv(42, number)) FROM numbers(10) ; SELECT if(1, 2 + 2, 2 + 6) AS res; ; в”Ңв”Җresв”Җв”җ
example: SELECT if(number = 0, 0, intDiv(42, number)) FROM numbers(10)

description:
Introduced in: v1.1
Allows writing the CASE operator more compactly in the query. Evaluates each condition in order. For the first condition that is true (non-zero and not NULL ), returns the corresponding branch value. If none of the conditions are true, returns the else value.
Setting short_circuit_function_evaluation controls whether short-circuit evaluation is used. If enabled, the then_i expression is evaluated only on rows where ((NOT cond_1) AND ... AND (NOT cond_{i-1}) AND cond_i) is true.
For example, with short-circuit evaluation, no division-by-zero exception is thrown when executing the following query:
All branch and else expressions must have a common supertype. NULL conditions are treated as false.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Introduced in: v1.1
Allows writing the CASE operator more compactly in the query. Evaluates each condition in order. For the first condition that is true (non-zero and not NULL ), returns the corresponding branch value. If none of the conditions are true, returns the else value.
Setting short_circuit_function_evaluation controls whether short-circuit evaluation is used. If enabled, the then_i expression is evaluated only on rows where ((NOT cond_1) AND ... AND (NOT cond_{i-1}) AND cond_i) is true.
For example, with short-circuit evaluation, no division-by-zero exception is thrown when executing the following query:
All branch and else expressions must have a common supertype. NULL conditions are treated as false.<sep>[SYNTAX]: multiIf(cond_1, then_1, cond_2, then_2, ..., else)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - cond_N вҖ” The N-th evaluated condition which controls if then_N is returned. UInt8 or Nullable(UInt8) or NULL
- then_N вҖ” The result of the function when cond_N is true. - else вҖ” The result of the function if none of the conditions is true.
Returned value
Returns the result of then_N for matching cond_N , otherwise returns the else condition.<sep>[RETURN]: Returns the result of then_N for matching cond_N , otherwise returns the else condition.<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT multiIf(number = 2, intDiv(1, number), number = 5) FROM numbers(10) ; в”Ңв”Җleftв”Җв”¬в”Җrightв”Җв”¬в”Җresultв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
example: SELECT multiIf(number = 2, intDiv(1, number), number = 5) FROM numbers(10)

в”Ңв”Җleftв”Җв”¬в”Җrightв”Җв”¬в”Җresultв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
в”Ӯ бҙәбөҒбҙёбҙё в”Ӯ     4 в”Ӯ Null value      в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ    1 в”Ӯ     3 в”Ӯ left is smaller в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ    2 в”Ӯ     2 в”Ӯ Both equal      в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ    3 в”Ӯ     1 в”Ӯ left is greater в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ    4 в”Ӯ  бҙәбөҒбҙёбҙё в”Ӯ Null value      в”Ӯ
в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ

Returns datetime plus num quarters Date or Date32 or DateTime or DateTime64<sep>[RETURN]: Returns datetime plus num quarters Date or Date32 or DateTime or DateTime64<sep>[DEMO]: WITH ; в”Ңв”Җadd_quarters_with_dateв”Җв”¬в”Җadd_quarters_with_date_timeв”Җв”¬в”Җadd_quarters_with_date_time_stringв”Җв”җ ; SELECT dateAdd('1998-06-16'::Date, INTERVAL 10 quarter)
example:

description:
Introduced in: v23.1
Returns the unit component of the difference between startdate and enddate . The difference is calculated using a precision of 1 nanosecond.
For example, the difference between 2021-12-29 and 2022-01-01 is 3 days for the day unit, 0 months for the month unit, and 0 years for the year unit.
For an alternative to age, see function timeDiff .
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Introduced in: v23.1
Returns the unit component of the difference between startdate and enddate . The difference is calculated using a precision of 1 nanosecond.
For example, the difference between 2021-12-29 and 2022-01-01 is 3 days for the day unit, 0 months for the month unit, and 0 years for the year unit.
For an alternative to age, see function timeDiff .<sep>[SYNTAX]: age('unit', startdate, enddate, [timezone])<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - unit вҖ” The type of interval for result.
- startdate вҖ” The first time value to subtract (the subtrahend). Date or Date32 or DateTime or DateTime64
- enddate вҖ” The second time value to subtract from (the minuend). Date or Date32 or DateTime or DateTime64
- timezone вҖ” Optional. Timezone name. If specified, it is applied to both startdate and enddate. If not specified, timezones of startdate and enddate are used. If they are not the same, the result is unspecified. String
Returned value
Returns the difference between enddate and startdate expressed in unit. Int32<sep>[RETURN]: Returns the difference between enddate and startdate expressed in unit. Int32<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT age('hour', toDateTime('2018-01-01 22:30:00'), toDateTime('2018-01-02 23:00:00')) ; в”Ңв”Җage('hour', toDateTime('2018-01-01 22:30:00'), toDateTime('2018-01-02 23:00:00'))в”Җв”җ ; SELECT
example:
SELECT
    toDate('2022-01-01') AS e,
    toDate('2021-12-29') AS s,
    age('day', s, e) AS day_age,
    age('month', s, e) AS month_age,
    age('year', s, e) AS year_age

в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җeв”Җв”¬в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җsв”Җв”¬в”Җday_ageв”Җв”¬в”Җmonth_ageв”Җв”¬в”Җyear_ageв”Җв”җ
в”Ӯ 2022-01-01 в”Ӯ 2021-12-29 в”Ӯ       3 в”Ӯ         0 в”Ӯ        0 в”Ӯ
в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ

detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Introduced in: v20.8
Truncates a date and time value to the specified part of the date.<sep>[SYNTAX]: dateTrunc(unit, datetime[, timezone])<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - unit вҖ” The type of interval to truncate the result. unit argument is case-insensitive. | Unit | Compatibility | |--------------|---------------------------------| | nanosecond | Compatible only with DateTime64 | | microsecond | Compatible only with DateTime64 | | millisecond | Compatible only with DateTime64 | | second | | | minute | | | hour | | | day | | | week | | | month | | | quarter | | | year | | String
- datetime вҖ” Date and time. Date or Date32 or DateTime or DateTime64
- timezone вҖ” Optional. Timezone name for the returned datetime. If not specified, the function uses the timezone of the datetime parameter. String
Returned value
Returns the truncated date and time value.<sep>[RETURN]: Returns the truncated date and time value.<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT now(), dateTrunc('hour', now()); ; в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җnow()в”Җв”¬в”ҖdateTrunc('hour', now())в”Җв”Җв”җ ; SELECT now(), dateTrunc('hour', now(), 'Asia/Istanbul');

description:
Introduced in: v1.1
Formats a date or date with time according to the given format string. format is a constant expression, so you cannot have multiple formats for a single result column.
formatDateTime uses MySQL datetime format style, refer to the mysql docs .
The opposite operation of this function is parseDateTime .
Using replacement fields, you can define a pattern for the resulting string. The example column in the table below shows formatting result for 2018-01-02 22:33:44 .
Replacement fields:
- In ClickHouse versions earlier than v23.4, %f prints a single zero (0) if the formatted value is a Date, Date32 or DateTime (which have no fractional seconds) or a DateTime64 with a precision of 0.
- In ClickHouse versions earlier than v25.1, %f prints as many digits as specified by the scale of the DateTime64 instead of fixed 6 digits.
- In ClickHouse versions earlier than v23.4, %M prints the minute (00-59) instead of the full month name (January-December).
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Introduced in: v1.1
Formats a date or date with time according to the given format string. format is a constant expression, so you cannot have multiple formats for a single result column.
formatDateTime uses MySQL datetime format style, refer to the mysql docs .
The opposite operation of this function is parseDateTime .
Using replacement fields, you can define a pattern for the resulting string. The example column in the table below shows formatting result for 2018-01-02 22:33:44 .
Replacement fields:
- In ClickHouse versions earlier than v23.4, %f prints a single zero (0) if the formatted value is a Date, Date32 or DateTime (which have no fractional seconds) or a DateTime64 with a precision of 0.
- In ClickHouse versions earlier than v25.1, %f prints as many digits as specified by the scale of the DateTime64 instead of fixed 6 digits.
- In ClickHouse versions earlier than v23.4, %M prints the minute (00-59) instead of the full month name (January-December).<sep>[SYNTAX]: formatDateTime(datetime, format[, timezone])<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - datetime вҖ” A date or date time to format. Date or Date32 or DateTime or DateTime64
- format вҖ” Format string with replacement fields. String
- timezone вҖ” Optional. Timezone name for the formatted time. String
Returned value
Returns time and date values according to the determined format. String<sep>[RETURN]: Returns time and date values according to the determined format. String<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT formatDateTime(toDate('2010-01-04'), '%g') ; в”Ңв”ҖformatDateTime(toDate('2010-01-04'), '%g')в”Җв”җ ; SELECT formatDateTime(toDateTime64('2010-01-04 12:34:56.123456', 7), '%f')
example:
SELECT
    now() AS ts,
    time_zone,
    formatDateTime(ts, '%T', time_zone) AS str_tz_time
FROM system.time_zones
WHERE time_zone LIKE 'Europe%'
LIMIT 10

в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җtsв”Җв”¬в”Җtime_zoneв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җstr_tz_timeв”Җв”җ

в”Ӯ 2023-09-08 19:13:40 в”Ӯ Europe/Belfast    в”Ӯ 20:13:40    в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ 2023-09-08 19:13:40 в”Ӯ Europe/Belgrade   в”Ӯ 21:13:40    в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ 2023-09-08 19:13:40 в”Ӯ Europe/Berlin     в”Ӯ 21:13:40    в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ 2023-09-08 19:13:40 в”Ӯ Europe/Bratislava в”Ӯ 21:13:40    в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ 2023-09-08 19:13:40 в”Ӯ Europe/Brussels   в”Ӯ 21:13:40    в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ 2023-09-08 19:13:40 в”Ӯ Europe/Bucharest  в”Ӯ 22:13:40    в”Ӯ
в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ

description:
Introduced in: v23.11
For a given number of days elapsed since 1 January 0000 , returns the corresponding date in the proleptic Gregorian calendar defined by ISO 8601 .
The calculation is the same as in MySQL's FROM_DAYS() function. The result is undefined if it cannot be represented within the bounds of the Date type.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Introduced in: v23.11
For a given number of days elapsed since 1 January 0000 , returns the corresponding date in the proleptic Gregorian calendar defined by ISO 8601 .
The calculation is the same as in MySQL's FROM_DAYS() function. The result is undefined if it cannot be represented within the bounds of the Date type.<sep>[SYNTAX]: fromDaysSinceYearZero(days)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - days вҖ” The number of days passed since year zero. UInt32
Returned value
Returns the date corresponding to the number of days passed since year zero. Date<sep>[RETURN]: Returns the date corresponding to the number of days passed since year zero. Date<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT ; в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җdate1в”Җв”¬в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җdate2в”Җв”җ
example:
SELECT
fromDaysSinceYearZero(739136) AS date1,
fromDaysSinceYearZero(toDaysSinceYearZero(toDate('2023-09-08'))) AS date2

UInt32
Returned value
Returns the date corresponding to the number of days passed since year zero. Date32<sep>[RETURN]: Returns the date corresponding to the number of days passed since year zero. Date32<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT ; в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җdate1в”Җв”¬в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җdate2в”Җв”җ
example:
SELECT
fromDaysSinceYearZero32(739136) AS date1,
fromDaysSinceYearZero32(toDaysSinceYearZero(toDate('2023-09-08'))) AS date2

description:
Introduced in: v21.1
Converts a Modified Julian Day number to a Proleptic Gregorian calendar date in text form YYYY-MM-DD . This function supports day number from -678941 to 2973483 (which represent 0000-01-01 and 9999-12-31 respectively). It raises an exception if the day number is outside of the supported range.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Introduced in: v21.1
Converts a Modified Julian Day number to a Proleptic Gregorian calendar date in text form YYYY-MM-DD . This function supports day number from -678941 to 2973483 (which represent 0000-01-01 and 9999-12-31 respectively). It raises an exception if the day number is outside of the supported range.<sep>[SYNTAX]: fromModifiedJulianDay(day)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - day вҖ” Modified Julian Day number. (U)Int*
Returned value
Returns date in text form. String<sep>[RETURN]: Returns date in text form. String<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT fromModifiedJulianDay(58849) ; в”Ңв”ҖfromModifiedJulianDay(58849)в”Җв”җ

description:

Returned value
Returns date in text form for valid day argument, otherwise null . Nullable(String)<sep>[RETURN]: Returns date in text form for valid day argument, otherwise null . Nullable(String)<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT fromModifiedJulianDayOrNull(58849); ; в”Ңв”ҖfromModifiedвӢҜNull(58849)в”Җв”җ

SELECT fromModifiedJulianDayOrNull(58849);
SELECT fromModifiedJulianDayOrNull(60000000); -- invalid argument, returns NULL

description:

description:
Introduced in: v20.8
This function converts a Unix timestamp to a calendar date and a time of a day.
It can be called in two ways:
- When given a single argument of type Integer , it returns a value of type DateTime , i.e. behaves like toDateTime .
- When given two or three arguments where the first argument is a value of type Integer , Date , Date32 , DateTime or DateTime64 , the second argument is a constant format string and the third argument is an optional constant time zone string, the function returns a value of type String , i.e. it behaves like formatDateTime . In this case, MySQL's datetime format style is used.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Introduced in: v20.8
This function converts a Unix timestamp to a calendar date and a time of a day.
It can be called in two ways:
- When given a single argument of type Integer , it returns a value of type DateTime , i.e. behaves like toDateTime .
- When given two or three arguments where the first argument is a value of type Integer , Date , Date32 , DateTime or DateTime64 , the second argument is a constant format string and the third argument is an optional constant time zone string, the function returns a value of type String , i.e. it behaves like formatDateTime . In this case, MySQL's datetime format style is used.<sep>[SYNTAX]: fromUnixTimestamp(timestamp)
fromUnixTimestamp(timestamp[, format[, timezone]])<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - timestamp вҖ” Unix timestamp or date/date with time value. (U)Int* or Date or Date32 or DateTime or DateTime64
- format вҖ” Optional. Constant format string for output formatting. String
- timezone вҖ” Optional. Constant time zone string. String
Returned value
Returns DateTime of the timestamp when called with one argument, or a String when called with two or three arguments. DateTime or String<sep>[RETURN]: Returns DateTime of the timestamp when called with one argument, or a String when called with two or three arguments. DateTime or String<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT fromUnixTimestamp(423543535) ; в”Ңв”ҖfromUnixTimestamp(423543535)в”Җв”җ ; SELECT fromUnixTimestamp(1234334543, '%Y-%m-%d %R:%S') AS DateTime

description:
Introduced in: v23.1
This function converts a Unix timestamp to a calendar date and a time of a day.
It can be called in two ways:
When given a single argument of type Integer , it returns a value of type DateTime , i.e. behaves like toDateTime .
When given two or three arguments where the first argument is a value of type Integer , Date , Date32 , DateTime or DateTime64 , the second argument is a constant format string and the third argument is an optional constant time zone string, the function returns a value of type String , i.e. it behaves like formatDateTimeInJodaSyntax . In this case, Joda datetime format style is used.
detail:

This function converts a Unix timestamp to a calendar date and a time of a day.
It can be called in two ways:

When given two or three arguments where the first argument is a value of type Integer , Date , Date32 , DateTime or DateTime64 , the second argument is a constant format string and the third argument is an optional constant time zone string, the function returns a value of type String , i.e. it behaves like formatDateTimeInJodaSyntax . description:
Introduced in: v22.8
Returns the current date and time at the moment of processing of each block of data. In contrast to the function now , it is not a constant expression, and the returned value will be different in different blocks for long-running queries.
It makes sense to use this function to generate the current time in long-running INSERT SELECT queries.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Introduced in: v22.8
Returns the current date and time at the moment of processing of each block of data. In contrast to the function now , it is not a constant expression, and the returned value will be different in different blocks for long-running queries.
It makes sense to use this function to generate the current time in long-running INSERT SELECT queries.<sep>[SYNTAX]: nowInBlock([timezone])<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - timezone вҖ” Optional. Timezone name for the returned value. String
Returned value
Returns the current date and time at the moment of processing of each block of data. DateTime<sep>[RETURN]: Returns the current date and time at the moment of processing of each block of data. DateTime<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT ; в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җnow()в”Җв”¬в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҖnowInBlock()в”Җв”¬в”Җsleep(1)в”Җв”җ
example:
SELECT
    now(),
    nowInBlock(),
    sleep(1)
FROM numbers(3)
SETTINGS max_block_size = 1
FORMAT PrettyCompactMonoBlock

description:

It makes sense to use this function to generate the current time in long-running INSERT SELECT queries.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Introduced in: v25.8
Returns the current date and time at the moment of processing of each block of data in milliseconds. In contrast to the function now64 , it is not a constant expression, and the returned value will be different in different blocks for long-running queries.
It makes sense to use this function to generate the current time in long-running INSERT SELECT queries.<sep>[SYNTAX]: nowInBlock([scale[, timezone]])<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - scale вҖ” Optional. Tick size (precision): 10^-precision seconds. Valid range: [0 : 9]. Typically, are used - 3 (default) (milliseconds), 6 (microseconds), 9 (nanoseconds). UInt8
- timezone вҖ” Optional. Timezone name for the returned value. String
Returned value
Returns the current date and time at the moment of processing of each block of data with sub-second precision. DateTime64<sep>[RETURN]: Returns the current date and time at the moment of processing of each block of data with sub-second precision. DateTime64<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT ; в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җnow64()в”Җв”¬в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҖnowInBlock64()в”Җв”¬в”Җsleep(1)в”Җв”җ

description:
Introduced in: v1.1
Subtracts a specified number of days from a date, a date with time or a string-encoded date or date with time.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Introduced in: v1.1
Subtracts a specified number of days from a date, a date with time or a string-encoded date or date with time.<sep>[SYNTAX]: subtractDays(datetime, num)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - datetime вҖ” Date or date with time to subtract specified number of days from. Date or Date32 or DateTime or DateTime64 or String
- num вҖ” Number of days to subtract. (U)Int* or Float*
Returned value
Returns datetime minus num days Date or Date32 or DateTime or DateTime64<sep>[RETURN]: Returns datetime minus num days Date or Date32 or DateTime or DateTime64<sep>[DEMO]: WITH ; в”Ңв”Җsubtract_days_with_dateв”Җв”¬в”Җsubtract_days_with_date_timeв”Җв”¬в”Җsubtract_days_with_date_time_stringв”Җв”җ ; SELECT dateSub('1998-06-16'::Date, INTERVAL 10 day)
example:
WITH
    toDate('2024-01-01') AS date,
    toDateTime('2024-01-01 00:00:00') AS date_time,
    '2024-01-01 00:00:00' AS date_time_string
SELECT
    subtractDays(date, 31) AS subtract_days_with_date,
    subtractDays(date_time, 31) AS subtract_days_with_date_time,
    subtractDays(date_time_string, 31) AS subtract_days_with_date_time_string

description:

description:
Introduced in: v23.4
Returns the count of the specified unit boundaries crossed between the startdate and the enddate . The difference is calculated using relative units. For example, the difference between 2021-12-29 and 2022-01-01 is 3 days for unit day (see toRelativeDayNum ), 1 month for unit month (see toRelativeMonthNum ) and 1 year for unit year (see toRelativeYearNum ).
If the unit week was specified, then timeDiff assumes that weeks start on Monday. Note that this behavior is different from that of function toWeek() in which weeks start by default on Sunday.
For an alternative to timeDiff , see function age .
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Introduced in: v23.4
Returns the count of the specified unit boundaries crossed between the startdate and the enddate . The difference is calculated using relative units. For example, the difference between 2021-12-29 and 2022-01-01 is 3 days for unit day (see toRelativeDayNum ), 1 month for unit month (see toRelativeMonthNum ) and 1 year for unit year (see toRelativeYearNum ).
If the unit week was specified, then timeDiff assumes that weeks start on Monday. Note that this behavior is different from that of function toWeek() in which weeks start by default on Sunday.
For an alternative to timeDiff , see function age .<sep>[SYNTAX]: date_diff(unit, startdate, enddate, [timezone])<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - unit вҖ” The type of interval for result.
- startdate вҖ” The first time value to subtract (the subtrahend). Date or Date32 or DateTime or DateTime64
- enddate вҖ” The second time value to subtract from (the minuend). Date or Date32 or DateTime or DateTime64
- timezone вҖ” Optional. Timezone name. If specified, it is applied to both startdate and enddate . If not specified, timezones of startdate and enddate are used. If they are not the same, the result is unspecified. String
Returned value
Returns the difference between enddate and startdate expressed in unit . Int64<sep>[RETURN]: Returns the difference between enddate and startdate expressed in unit . Int64<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT timeDiff('hour', toDateTime('2018-01-01 22:00:00'), toDateTime('2018-01-02 23:00:00')) AS res ; в”Ңв”Җresв”Җв”җ ; SELECT
example: SELECT timeDiff('hour', toDateTime('2018-01-01 22:00:00'), toDateTime('2018-01-02 23:00:00')) AS res

SELECT
    toDate('2022-01-01') AS e,
    toDate('2021-12-29') AS s,
    timeDiff('day', s, e) AS day_diff,
    timeDiff('month', s, e) AS month_diff,
    timeDiff('year', s, e) AS year_diff

в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җeв”Җв”¬в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җsв”Җв”¬в”Җday_diffв”Җв”¬в”Җmonth_diffв”Җв”¬в”Җyear_diffв”Җв”җ
в”Ӯ 2022-01-01 в”Ӯ 2021-12-29 в”Ӯ        3 в”Ӯ          1 в”Ӯ         1 в”Ӯ
в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ

description:
Introduced in: v1.1
For a time interval starting at StartTime and continuing for Duration seconds, it returns an array of moments in time, consisting of points from this interval rounded down to the Size in seconds. Size is an optional parameter set to 1800 (30 minutes) by default.
This is necessary, for example, when searching for pageviews in the corresponding session.
For DateTime64 , the return value's scale can differ from the scale of StartTime . The highest scale among all given arguments is taken.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Introduced in: v1.1
For a time interval starting at StartTime and continuing for Duration seconds, it returns an array of moments in time, consisting of points from this interval rounded down to the Size in seconds. Size is an optional parameter set to 1800 (30 minutes) by default.
This is necessary, for example, when searching for pageviews in the corresponding session.
For DateTime64 , the return value's scale can differ from the scale of StartTime . The highest scale among all given arguments is taken.<sep>[SYNTAX]: timeSlots(StartTime, Duration[, Size])<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - StartTime вҖ” Starting time for the interval. DateTime or DateTime64
- Duration вҖ” Duration of the interval in seconds. UInt32 or DateTime64
- Size вҖ” Optional. Size of time slots in seconds. Default is 1800 (30 minutes). UInt32 or DateTime64
Returned value
Returns an array of DateTime/DateTime64 (return type matches the type of StartTime ). For DateTime64, the return value's scale can differ from the scale of StartTime - the highest scale among all given arguments is taken. Array(DateTime) or Array(DateTime64)<sep>[RETURN]: Returns an array of DateTime/DateTime64 (return type matches the type of StartTime ). For DateTime64, the return value's scale can differ from the scale of StartTime - the highest scale among all given arguments is taken. Array(DateTime) or Array(DateTime64)<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT timeSlots(toDateTime('2012-01-01 12:20:00'), toUInt32(600)); ; в”Ңв”ҖtimeSlots(toDateTime('2012-01-01 12:20:00'), toUInt32(600))в”Җв”җ
example:
в”Ңв”ҖtimeSlots(toDateTime('2012-01-01 12:20:00'), toUInt32(600))в”Җв”җ
в”Ӯ ['2012-01-01 12:00:00','2012-01-01 12:30:00']               в”Ӯ
в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
в”Ңв”ҖtimeSlots(toDateTime('1980-12-12 21:01:02', 'UTC'), toUInt32(600), 299)в”Җв”җ
в”Ӯ ['1980-12-12 20:56:13','1980-12-12 21:01:12','1980-12-12 21:06:11']     в”Ӯ
в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
в”Ңв”ҖtimeSlots(toDateTime64('1980-12-12 21:01:02.1234', 4, 'UTC'), toDecimal64(600.1, 1), toDecimal64(299, 0))в”Җв”җ
в”Ӯ ['1980-12-12 20:56:13.0000','1980-12-12 21:01:12.0000','1980-12-12 21:06:11.0000']                        в”Ӯ
в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ

description:
Introduced in: v23.9
Converts the first argument expr to type DateTime64(6) . If a second argument expr_time is provided, it adds the specified time to the converted value.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Introduced in: v23.9
Converts the first argument expr to type DateTime64(6) . If a second argument expr_time is provided, it adds the specified time to the converted value.<sep>[SYNTAX]: timestamp(expr[, expr_time])<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - expr вҖ” Date or date with time. String
- expr_time вҖ” Optional. Time to add to the converted value. String
Returned value
Returns the converted value of expr , or expr with added time DateTime64(6)<sep>[RETURN]: Returns the converted value of expr , or expr with added time DateTime64(6)<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT timestamp('2023-12-31') AS ts; ; в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җtsв”Җв”җ ; SELECT timestamp('2023-12-31 12:00:00', '12:00:00.11') AS ts;

detail:

description:
Introduced in: v21.4
Returns the timezone name of a DateTime or DateTime64 value.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Introduced in: v21.4
Returns the timezone name of a DateTime or DateTime64 value.<sep>[SYNTAX]: timeZoneOf(datetime)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - datetime вҖ” A value of type. DateTime or DateTime64
- timezone вҖ” Optional. Timezone name to convert the datetime value's timezone to. String
Returned value
Returns the timezone name for datetime String<sep>[RETURN]: Returns the timezone name for datetime String<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT timezoneOf(now()); ; в”Ңв”ҖtimezoneOf(now())в”Җв”җ

description:
Introduced in: v21.6
Returns the timezone offset in seconds from UTC . The function takes daylight saving time and historical timezone changes at the specified date and time into account.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Introduced in: v21.6
Returns the timezone offset in seconds from UTC . The function takes daylight saving time and historical timezone changes at the specified date and time into account.<sep>[SYNTAX]: timeZoneOffset(datetime)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - datetime вҖ” DateTime value to get the timezone offset for. DateTime or DateTime64
Returned value
Returns the offset from UTC in seconds Int32<sep>[RETURN]: Returns the offset from UTC in seconds Int32<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT toDateTime('2021-04-21 10:20:30', 'America/New_York') AS Time, ; в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҖTimeв”Җв”¬в”ҖTypeв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”ҖOffset_in_secondsв”Җв”¬в”ҖOffset_in_hoursв”Җв”җ
example:
SELECT toDateTime('2021-04-21 10:20:30', 'America/New_York') AS Time,
toTypeName(Time) AS Type,
timeZoneOffset(Time) AS Offset_in_seconds,
(Offset_in_seconds / 3600) AS Offset_in_hours;

в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҖTimeв”Җв”¬в”ҖTypeв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”ҖOffset_in_secondsв”Җв”¬в”ҖOffset_in_hoursв”Җв”җ
в”Ӯ 2021-04-21 10:20:30 в”Ӯ DateTime('America/New_York') в”Ӯ            -14400 в”Ӯ              -4 в”Ӯ
в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ

description:
Introduced in: v1.1

detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Introduced in: v1.1
Returns the day of the month (1-31) of a Date or DateTime .<sep>[SYNTAX]: toDayOfMonth(datetime)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - datetime вҖ” Date or date with time to get the day of month from. Date or Date32 or DateTime or DateTime64
Returned value
Returns the day of the month of the given date/time UInt8<sep>[RETURN]: Returns the day of the month of the given date/time UInt8<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT toDayOfMonth(toDateTime('2023-04-21 10:20:30')) ; в”Ңв”ҖtoDayOfMonth(toDateTime('2023-04-21 10:20:30'))в”Җв”җ

description:
Introduced in: v1.1
Returns the number of the day within the week of a Date or DateTime value.
The two-argument form of toDayOfWeek() enables you to specify whether the week starts on Monday or Sunday, and whether the return value should be in the range from 0 to 6 or 1 to 7.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Introduced in: v1.1
Returns the number of the day within the week of a Date or DateTime value.
The two-argument form of toDayOfWeek() enables you to specify whether the week starts on Monday or Sunday, and whether the return value should be in the range from 0 to 6 or 1 to 7.<sep>[SYNTAX]: toDayOfWeek(datetime[, mode[, timezone]])<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - datetime вҖ” Date or date with time to get the day of week from. Date or Date32 or DateTime or DateTime64
- mode вҖ” Optional. Integer specifying the week mode (0-3). Defaults to 0 if omitted. UInt8
- timezone вҖ” Optional. Timezone to use for the conversion. String
Returned value
Returns the day of the week for the given Date or DateTime UInt8<sep>[RETURN]: Returns the day of the week for the given Date or DateTime UInt8<sep>[DEMO]: в”Ңв”ҖtoDayOfWeek(toDateTime('2023-04-21'))в”Җв”¬в”ҖtoDayOfWeek(toDateTime('2023-04-21'), 1)в”Җв”җ

Returned value
Returns the day of the year of the given Date or DateTime UInt16<sep>[RETURN]: Returns the day of the year of the given Date or DateTime UInt16<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT toDayOfYear(toDateTime('2023-04-21 10:20:30')) ; в”Ңв”ҖtoDayOfYear(toDateTime('2023-04-21 10:20:30'))в”Җв”җ

description:
Introduced in: v23.9
For a given date, returns the number of days which have passed since 1 January 0000 in the proleptic Gregorian calendar defined by ISO 8601 .
The calculation is the same as in MySQL's TO_DAYS function.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Introduced in: v23.9
For a given date, returns the number of days which have passed since 1 January 0000 in the proleptic Gregorian calendar defined by ISO 8601 .
The calculation is the same as in MySQL's TO_DAYS function.<sep>[SYNTAX]: toDaysSinceYearZero(date[, time_zone])<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - date вҖ” The date or date with time for which to calculate the number of days since year zero from. Date or Date32 or DateTime or DateTime64
- time_zone вҖ” Time zone. String
Returned value
Returns the number of days passed since date 0000-01-01 . UInt32<sep>[RETURN]: Returns the number of days passed since date 0000-01-01 . UInt32<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT toDaysSinceYearZero(toDate('2023-09-08')) ; в”Ңв”ҖtoDaysSinceYearZero(toDate('2023-09-08')))в”Җв”җ

Returned value
Returns the input value converted to an ISO year number. UInt16<sep>[RETURN]: Returns the input value converted to an ISO year number. UInt16<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT ; в”Ңв”Җweek1в”Җв”¬в”Җweek2в”Җв”җ
example:
SELECT
toISOYear(toDate('2024/10/02')) as year1,

Returned value
Returns the date of the last day of the month for the given date or date with time. Date<sep>[RETURN]: Returns the date of the last day of the month for the given date or date with time. Date<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT toLastDayOfMonth(toDateTime('2023-04-21 10:20:30')) ; в”Ңв”ҖtoLastDayOfMonth(toDateTime('2023-04-21 10:20:30'))в”Җв”җ

description:
Introduced in: v23.5
Rounds a date or date with time up to the nearest Saturday or Sunday.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Introduced in: v23.5
Rounds a date or date with time up to the nearest Saturday or Sunday.<sep>[SYNTAX]: toLastDayOfWeek(datetime[, mode[, timezone]])<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - datetime вҖ” A date or date with time to convert. Date or DateTime or Date32 or DateTime64
- mode вҖ” Determines the first day of the week as described in the toWeek() function. Default 0 . UInt8
- timezone вҖ” Optional. The timezone to use for the conversion. If not specified, the server's timezone is used. String
Returned value
Returns the date of the nearest Saturday or Sunday, on or after the given date, depending on the mode Date or Date32 or DateTime or DateTime64<sep>[RETURN]: Returns the date of the nearest Saturday or Sunday, on or after the given date, depending on the mode Date or Date32 or DateTime or DateTime64<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT ; Row 1:
example:
SELECT
    toLastDayOfWeek(toDateTime('2023-04-21 10:20:30')), /* a Friday */
    toLastDayOfWeek(toDateTime('2023-04-21 10:20:30'), 1), /* a Friday */
    toLastDayOfWeek(toDate('2023-04-23')), /* a Sunday */
    toLastDayOfWeek(toDate('2023-04-23'), 1) /* a Sunday */
FORMAT Vertical

Row 1:
в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ
toLastDayOfWeek(toDateTime('2023-04-21 10:20:30')):      2023-04-23
toLastDayOfWeek(toDateTime('2023-04-21 10:20:30'), 1):   2023-04-22
toLastDayOfWeek(toDate('2023-04-23')):                   2023-04-23
toLastDayOfWeek(toDate('2023-04-23'), 1):                2023-04-23

description:
Introduced in: v21.1
Converts a Proleptic Gregorian calendar date in text form YYYY-MM-DD to a Modified Julian Day number in Int32 . This function supports date from 0000-01-01 to 9999-12-31 . It raises an exception if the argument cannot be parsed as a date, or the date is invalid.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Introduced in: v21.1
Converts a Proleptic Gregorian calendar date in text form YYYY-MM-DD to a Modified Julian Day number in Int32 . This function supports date from 0000-01-01 to 9999-12-31 . It raises an exception if the argument cannot be parsed as a date, or the date is invalid.<sep>[SYNTAX]: toModifiedJulianDay(date)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - date вҖ” The date in String form. String or FixedString
Returned value
Returns Modified Julian Day number. Int32<sep>[RETURN]: Returns Modified Julian Day number. Int32<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT toModifiedJulianDay('2020-01-01') ; в”Ңв”ҖtoModifiedJulianDay('2020-01-01')в”Җв”җ

description:
Introduced in: v21.1

detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Introduced in: v21.1
Similar to toModifiedJulianDay() , but instead of raising exceptions it returns NULL .<sep>[SYNTAX]: toModifiedJulianDayOrNull(date)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - date вҖ” Date in text form. String or FixedString
Returned value
Returns the modified Julian day number for valid date , otherwise null . Nullable(Int32)<sep>[RETURN]: Returns the modified Julian day number for valid date , otherwise null . Nullable(Int32)<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT toModifiedJulianDayOrNull('2020-01-01'); ; в”Ңв”ҖtoModifiedJuвӢҜ020-01-01')в”Җв”җ
example:
SELECT toModifiedJulianDayOrNull('2020-01-01');
SELECT toModifiedJulianDayOrNull('0000-00-00'); -- invalid date, returns NULL

в”Ңв”ҖtoModifiedJuвӢҜ020-01-01')в”Җв”җ
в”Ӯ                    58849 в”Ӯ
в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
в”Ңв”ҖtoModifiedJuвӢҜ000-00-00')в”Җв”җ
в”Ӯ                     бҙәбөҒбҙёбҙё в”Ӯ
в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ

description:
Introduced in: v1.1
Converts a date or date with time to the number of days elapsed since a certain fixed point in the past. The exact point in time is an implementation detail, and therefore this function is not intended to be used standalone. The main purpose of the function is to calculate the difference in days between two dates or dates with time, e.g., toRelativeDayNum(dt1) - toRelativeDayNum(dt2) .
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Introduced in: v1.1
Converts a date or date with time to the number of days elapsed since a certain fixed point in the past. The exact point in time is an implementation detail, and therefore this function is not intended to be used standalone. The main purpose of the function is to calculate the difference in days between two dates or dates with time, e.g., toRelativeDayNum(dt1) - toRelativeDayNum(dt2) .<sep>[SYNTAX]: toRelativeDayNum(date)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - date вҖ” Date or date with time. Date or DateTime or DateTime64
Returned value
Returns the number of days from a fixed reference point in the past. UInt32<sep>[RETURN]: Returns the number of days from a fixed reference point in the past. UInt32<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT toRelativeDayNum(toDate('2023-04-01')) - toRelativeDayNum(toDate('2023-01-01')) ; в”Ңв”Җminus(toRelaвӢҜ3-01-01')))в”Җв”җ

description:
Introduced in: v1.1
Converts a date or date with time to the number of minutes elapsed since a certain fixed point in the past. The exact point in time is an implementation detail, and therefore this function is not intended to be used standalone. The main purpose of the function is to calculate the difference in minutes between two dates or dates with time, e.g., toRelativeMinuteNum(dt1) - toRelativeMinuteNum(dt2) .

tree:
toStartOfInterval(value, INTERVAL x unit[, time_zone])
toStartOfInterval(value, INTERVAL x unit[, origin[, time_zone]])
description:
Introduced in: v20.1
This function generalizes other toStartOf*() functions with toStartOfInterval(date_or_date_with_time, INTERVAL x unit [, time_zone]) syntax.
For example,
- toStartOfInterval(t, INTERVAL 1 YEAR) returns the same as toStartOfYear(t) ,
- toStartOfInterval(t, INTERVAL 1 MONTH) returns the same as toStartOfMonth(t) ,
- toStartOfInterval(t, INTERVAL 1 DAY) returns the same as toStartOfDay(t) ,
- toStartOfInterval(t, INTERVAL 15 MINUTE) returns the same as toStartOfFifteenMinutes(t) .
The calculation is performed relative to specific points in time:
If unit WEEK was specified, toStartOfInterval assumes that weeks start on Monday. Note that this behavior is different from that of function toStartOfWeek in which weeks start by default on Sunday.
The second overload emulates TimescaleDB's time_bucket() function, respectively PostgreSQL's date_bin() function.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Introduced in: v20.1
This function generalizes other toStartOf*() functions with toStartOfInterval(date_or_date_with_time, INTERVAL x unit [, time_zone]) syntax.
For example,
- toStartOfInterval(t, INTERVAL 1 YEAR) returns the same as toStartOfYear(t) ,
- toStartOfInterval(t, INTERVAL 1 MONTH) returns the same as toStartOfMonth(t) ,
- toStartOfInterval(t, INTERVAL 1 DAY) returns the same as toStartOfDay(t) ,
- toStartOfInterval(t, INTERVAL 15 MINUTE) returns the same as toStartOfFifteenMinutes(t) .
The calculation is performed relative to specific points in time:
If unit WEEK was specified, toStartOfInterval assumes that weeks start on Monday. Note that this behavior is different from that of function toStartOfWeek in which weeks start by default on Sunday.
The second overload emulates TimescaleDB's time_bucket() function, respectively PostgreSQL's date_bin() function.<sep>[SYNTAX]: toStartOfInterval(value, INTERVAL x unit[, time_zone])
toStartOfInterval(value, INTERVAL x unit[, origin[, time_zone]])<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - value вҖ” Date or date with time value to round down. Date or DateTime or DateTime64
- x вҖ” Interval length number. - unit вҖ” Interval unit: YEAR, QUARTER, MONTH, WEEK, DAY, HOUR, MINUTE, SECOND, MILLISECOND, MICROSECOND, NANOSECOND. - time_zone вҖ” Optional. Time zone name as a string. - origin вҖ” Optional. Origin point for calculation (second overload only).
Returned value
Returns the start of the interval containing the input value. DateTime<sep>[RETURN]: Returns the start of the interval containing the input value. DateTime<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT toStartOfInterval(toDateTime('2023-01-15 14:30:00'), INTERVAL 1 MONTH) ; в”Ңв”ҖtoStartOfIntвӢҜalMonth(1))в”Җв”җ ; SELECT toStartOfInterval(toDateTime('2023-01-01 14:45:00'), INTERVAL 1 MINUTE, toDateTime('2023-01-01 14:35:30'))

description:
Introduced in: v22.6
Rounds down a date with time to the start of the microseconds.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Introduced in: v22.6
Rounds down a date with time to the start of the microseconds.<sep>[SYNTAX]: toStartOfMicrosecond(datetime, [timezone])<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - datetime вҖ” Date and time. DateTime64
- timezone вҖ” Optional. Timezone for the returned value. If not specified, the function uses the timezone of the value parameter. String
Returned value
Input value with sub-microseconds DateTime64<sep>[RETURN]: Input value with sub-microseconds DateTime64<sep>[DEMO]: WITH toDateTime64('2020-01-01 10:20:30.999999999', 9) AS dt64 ; в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҖtoStartOfMicrosecond(dt64)в”Җв”җ ; WITH toDateTime64('2020-01-01 10:20:30.999999999', 9) AS dt64
example:
WITH toDateTime64('2020-01-01 10:20:30.999999999', 9) AS dt64
SELECT toStartOfMicrosecond(dt64);

WITH toDateTime64('2020-01-01 10:20:30.999999999', 9) AS dt64
SELECT toStartOfMicrosecond(dt64, 'Asia/Istanbul');

description:
Introduced in: v20.1
Rounds a date or date with time down to the nearest Sunday or Monday.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Introduced in: v20.1
Rounds a date or date with time down to the nearest Sunday or Monday.<sep>[SYNTAX]: toStartOfWeek(datetime[, mode[, timezone]])<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - datetime вҖ” A date or date with time to convert. Date or DateTime or Date32 or DateTime64
- mode вҖ” Determines the first day of the week as described in the toWeek() function. Default 0 . UInt8
- timezone вҖ” The timezone to use for the conversion. If not specified, the server's timezone is used. String
Returned value
Returns the date of the nearest Sunday or Monday on, or prior to, the given date, depending on the mode Date or Date32 or DateTime or DateTime64<sep>[RETURN]: Returns the date of the nearest Sunday or Monday on, or prior to, the given date, depending on the mode Date or Date32 or DateTime or DateTime64<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT ; Row 1:
example:
SELECT
        toStartOfWeek(toDateTime('2023-04-21 10:20:30')), /* a Friday */
        toStartOfWeek(toDateTime('2023-04-21 10:20:30'), 1), /* a Friday */
        toStartOfWeek(toDate('2023-04-24')), /* a Monday */
        toStartOfWeek(toDate('2023-04-24'), 1) /* a Monday */
    FORMAT Vertical

Row 1:
    в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ
    toStartOfWeek(toDateTime('2023-04-21 10:20:30')):      2023-04-17
    toStartOfWeek(toDateTime('2023-04-21 10:20:30'), 1):   2023-04-17
    toStartOfWeek(toDate('2023-04-24')):                   2023-04-24
    toStartOfWeek(toDate('2023-04-24'), 1):                2023-04-24

description:
Introduced in: v1.1
Extracts the time component of a date or date with time. The returned result is an offset to a fixed point in time, currently 1970-01-02 , but the exact point in time is an implementation detail which may change in future.
toTime should therefore not be used standalone. The main purpose of the function is to calculate the time difference between two dates or dates with time, e.g., toTime(dt1) - toTime(dt2) .
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Introduced in: v1.1
Extracts the time component of a date or date with time. The returned result is an offset to a fixed point in time, currently 1970-01-02 , but the exact point in time is an implementation detail which may change in future.
toTime should therefore not be used standalone. The main purpose of the function is to calculate the time difference between two dates or dates with time, e.g., toTime(dt1) - toTime(dt2) .<sep>[SYNTAX]: toTime(date[, timezone])<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - date вҖ” Date to convert to a time. Date or DateTime or DateTime64
- timezone вҖ” Optional. Timezone for the returned value. String
Returned value
Returns the time component of a date or date with time in the form of an offset to a fixed point in time (selected as 1970-01-02, currently). DateTime<sep>[RETURN]: Returns the time component of a date or date with time in the form of an offset to a fixed point in time (selected as 1970-01-02, currently). DateTime<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT toTime('2025-06-15 12:00:00'::DateTime) - toTime('2024-05-10 11:00:00'::DateTime) AS result, toTypeName(result) ; в”Ңв”Җresultв”Җв”¬в”ҖtoTypeName(result)в”Җв”җ
example: SELECT toTime('2025-06-15 12:00:00'::DateTime) - toTime('2024-05-10 11:00:00'::DateTime) AS result, toTypeName(result)

description:
Introduced in: v1.1
Converts a DateTime or DateTime64 to the specified time zone. The internal value (number of unix seconds) of the data doesn't change. Only the value's time zone attribute and the value's string representation changes.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Introduced in: v1.1
Converts a DateTime or DateTime64 to the specified time zone. The internal value (number of unix seconds) of the data doesn't change. Only the value's time zone attribute and the value's string representation changes.<sep>[SYNTAX]: toTimeZone(datetime, timezone)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - date вҖ” The value to convert. DateTime or DateTime64
- timezone вҖ” The target time zone name. String
Returned value

description:
Introduced in: v20.1
This function returns the week number for date or datetime. The two-argument form of toWeek() enables you to specify whether the week starts on Sunday or Monday and whether the return value should be in the range from 0 to 53 or from 1 to 53 .
toISOWeek() is a compatibility function that is equivalent to toWeek(date,3) .
The following table describes how the mode argument works.
For mode values with a meaning of "with 4 or more days this year," weeks are numbered according to ISO 8601:1988:
- If the week containing January 1 has 4 or more days in the new year, it is week 1.
- Otherwise, it is the last week of the previous year, and the next week is week 1.
For mode values with a meaning of "contains January 1", the week contains January 1 is week 1. It does not matter how many days in the new year the week contained, even if it contained only one day. I.e. if the last week of December contains January 1 of the next year, it will be week 1 of the next year.
The first argument can also be specified as String in a format supported by parseDateTime64BestEffort() . Support for string arguments exists only for reasons of compatibility with MySQL which is expected by certain 3rd party tools. As string argument support may in future be made dependent on new MySQL-compatibility settings and because string parsing is generally slow, it is recommended to not use it.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Introduced in: v20.1
This function returns the week number for date or datetime. The two-argument form of toWeek() enables you to specify whether the week starts on Sunday or Monday and whether the return value should be in the range from 0 to 53 or from 1 to 53 .
toISOWeek() is a compatibility function that is equivalent to toWeek(date,3) .
The following table describes how the mode argument works.
For mode values with a meaning of "with 4 or more days this year," weeks are numbered according to ISO 8601:1988:
- If the week containing January 1 has 4 or more days in the new year, it is week 1.
- Otherwise, it is the last week of the previous year, and the next week is week 1.
For mode values with a meaning of "contains January 1", the week contains January 1 is week 1. It does not matter how many days in the new year the week contained, even if it contained only one day. I.e. if the last week of December contains January 1 of the next year, it will be week 1 of the next year.
The first argument can also be specified as String in a format supported by parseDateTime64BestEffort() . Support for string arguments exists only for reasons of compatibility with MySQL which is expected by certain 3rd party tools. As string argument support may in future be made dependent on new MySQL-compatibility settings and because string parsing is generally slow, it is recommended to not use it.<sep>[SYNTAX]: toWeek(datetime[, mode[, time_zone]])<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - datetime вҖ” Date or date with time to get the week number from. Date or DateTime
- mode вҖ” Optional. A mode 0 to 9 determines the first day of the week and the range of the week number. Default 0 . - time_zone вҖ” Optional. Time zone. String
Returned value
Returns the week number according to the specified mode. UInt32<sep>[RETURN]: Returns the week number according to the specified mode. UInt32<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT toDate('2016-12-27') AS date, toWeek(date) AS week0, toWeek(date,1) AS week1, toWeek(date,9) AS week9 ; в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җdateв”Җв”¬в”Җweek0в”Җв”¬в”Җweek1в”Җв”¬в”Җweek9в”Җв”җ
example: SELECT toDate('2016-12-27') AS date, toWeek(date) AS week0, toWeek(date,1) AS week1, toWeek(date,9) AS week9

description:
Introduced in: v1.1
Converts a date or date with time to a UInt32 number containing the year and month number (YYYY * 100 + MM). Accepts a second optional timezone argument. If provided, the timezone must be a string constant.
This function is the opposite of function YYYYMMDDToDate() .

This function is the opposite of function YYYYMMDDToDate() .<sep>[SYNTAX]: toYYYYMM(datetime[, timezone])<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - datetime вҖ” A date or date with time to convert. Date or Date32 or DateTime or DateTime64
- timezone вҖ” Optional. Timezone for the conversion. If provided, the timezone must be a string constant. String

description:
Introduced in: v20.1
Returns the year and week for a date. The year in the result may be different from the year in the date argument for the first and the last week of the year.
The mode argument works like the mode argument of toWeek() .
Warning: The week number returned by toYearWeek() can be different from what the toWeek() returns. toWeek() always returns week number in the context of the given year, and in case toWeek() returns 0 , toYearWeek() returns the value corresponding to the last week of previous year. See prev_yearWeek in example below.
The first argument can also be specified as String in a format supported by parseDateTime64BestEffort() . Support for string arguments exists only for reasons of compatibility with MySQL which is expected by certain 3rd party tools. As string argument support may in future be made dependent on new MySQL-compatibility settings and because string parsing is generally slow, it is recommended to not use it.
