Calculates the distance between two points (the elements of the vectors are the coordinates) in L_{inf} space ( maximum norm ).<sep>[SYNTAX]: LinfDistance(vector1, vector2)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - vector1 вҖ” First vector. Tuple(T) or Array(T)
- vector2 вҖ” Second vector. Tuple(T) or Array(T)
Returned value
Returns the Infinity-norm distance. Float64<sep>[RETURN]: Returns the Infinity-norm distance. Float64<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT LinfDistance((1, 2), (2, 3)) ; в”Ңв”ҖLinfDistance((1, 2), (2, 3))в”Җв”җ

description:
Introduced in: v21.11
Calculates the maximum of absolute elements of a vector.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Introduced in: v21.11
Calculates the maximum of absolute elements of a vector.<sep>[SYNTAX]: LinfNorm(vector)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - vector вҖ” Vector or tuple of numeric values. Array(T) or Tuple(T)
Returned value
Returns the Linf-norm or the maximum absolute value. Float64<sep>[RETURN]: Returns the Linf-norm or the maximum absolute value. Float64<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT LinfNorm((1, -2)) ; в”Ңв”ҖLinfNorm((1, -2))в”Җв”җ

description:
Introduced in: v21.11
Calculates the unit vector of a given vector (the elements of the tuple are the coordinates) in L_{inf} space (using maximum norm ).
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Introduced in: v21.11
Calculates the unit vector of a given vector (the elements of the tuple are the coordinates) in L_{inf} space (using maximum norm ).<sep>[SYNTAX]: LinfNormalize(tuple)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - tuple вҖ” A tuple of numeric values. Tuple(T)
Returned value
Returns the unit vector. Tuple(Float64)<sep>[RETURN]: Returns the unit vector. Tuple(Float64)<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT LinfNormalize((3, 4)) ; в”Ңв”ҖLinfNormalize((3, 4))в”Җв”җ

description:
Introduced in: v21.11
Calculates the distance between two points (the elements of the vectors are the coordinates) in Lp space ( p-norm distance ).
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Introduced in: v21.11
Calculates the distance between two points (the elements of the vectors are the coordinates) in Lp space ( p-norm distance ).<sep>[SYNTAX]: LpDistance(vector1, vector2, p)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - vector1 вҖ” First vector. Tuple(T) or Array(T)
- vector2 вҖ” Second vector. Tuple(T) or Array(T)
- p вҖ” The power. Possible values: real number from [1; inf) . UInt* or Float*
Returned value
Returns the p-norm distance. Float64<sep>[RETURN]: Returns the p-norm distance. Float64<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT LpDistance((1, 2), (2, 3), 3) ; в”Ңв”ҖLpDistance((1, 2), (2, 3), 3)в”Җв”җ

description:
Introduced in: v21.11
Calculates the p-norm of a vector, which is the p-th root of the sum of the p-th powers of the absolute elements of its elements.
Special cases:
- When p=1, it's equivalent to L1Norm (Manhattan distance).
- When p=2, it's equivalent to L2Norm (Euclidean distance).
- When p=вҲһ, it's equivalent to LinfNorm (maximum norm).
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Introduced in: v21.11
Calculates the p-norm of a vector, which is the p-th root of the sum of the p-th powers of the absolute elements of its elements.
Special cases:
- When p=1, it's equivalent to L1Norm (Manhattan distance).
- When p=2, it's equivalent to L2Norm (Euclidean distance).
- When p=вҲһ, it's equivalent to LinfNorm (maximum norm).<sep>[SYNTAX]: LpNorm(vector, p)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - vector вҖ” Vector or tuple of numeric values. Tuple(T) or Array(T)
- p вҖ” The power. Possible values are real numbers in the range [1; inf) . UInt* or Float*
Returned value
Returns the Lp-norm . Float64<sep>[RETURN]: Returns the Lp-norm . Float64<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT LpNorm((1, -2), 2) ; в”Ңв”ҖLpNorm((1, -2), 2)в”Җв”җ

description:
Introduced in: v21.11
Calculates the unit vector of a given vector (the elements of the tuple are the coordinates) in Lp space (using p-norm ).
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Introduced in: v21.11
Calculates the unit vector of a given vector (the elements of the tuple are the coordinates) in Lp space (using p-norm ).<sep>[SYNTAX]: LpNormalize(tuple, p)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - tuple вҖ” A tuple of numeric values. Tuple(T)
- p вҖ” The power. Possible values are any number in the range range from [1; inf) . UInt* or Float*
Returned value
Returns the unit vector. Tuple(Float64)<sep>[RETURN]: Returns the unit vector. Tuple(Float64)<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT LpNormalize((3, 4), 5) ; в”Ңв”ҖLpNormalize((3, 4), 5)в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ

description:
Introduced in: v1.1
Calculates the cosine distance between two vectors (the elements of the tuples are the coordinates). The smaller the returned value is, the more similar are the vectors.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Introduced in: v1.1
Calculates the cosine distance between two vectors (the elements of the tuples are the coordinates). The smaller the returned value is, the more similar are the vectors.<sep>[SYNTAX]: cosineDistance(vector1, vector2)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - vector1 вҖ” First tuple. Tuple(T) or Array(T)
- vector2 вҖ” Second tuple. Tuple(T) or Array(T)
Returned value
Returns the cosine of the angle between two vectors subtracted from one. Float64<sep>[RETURN]: Returns the cosine of the angle between two vectors subtracted from one. Float64<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT cosineDistance((1, 2), (2, 3)); ; в”Ңв”ҖcosineDistance((1, 2), (2, 3))в”Җв”җ

description: Accepts a region ID and geobase and returns a string of the name of the region in the corresponding language. If the region with the specified ID does not exist, an empty string is returned.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Accepts a region ID and geobase and returns a string of the name of the region in the corresponding language. If the region with the specified ID does not exist, an empty string is returned.<sep>[SYNTAX]: regionToName(id\[, lang\])<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - id вҖ” Region ID from the geobase. UInt32 .
- geobase вҖ” Dictionary key. See Multiple Geobases . String . Optional.
Returned value
- Name of the region in the corresponding language specified by geobase . String .
- Otherwise, an empty string.<sep>[RETURN]: - Name of the region in the corresponding language specified by geobase . String .
- Otherwise, an empty string.<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT regionToName(number::UInt32,'en') FROM numbers(0,5); ; в”Ңв”ҖregionToName(CAST(number, 'UInt32'), 'en')в”Җв”җ
example:
в”Ңв”ҖregionToName(CAST(number, 'UInt32'), 'en')в”Җв”җ
в”Ӯ                                            в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ World                                      в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ USA                                        в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ Colorado                                   в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ Boulder County                             в”Ӯ
в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ

description: Accepts a region ID from the geobase. If this region is a city or part of a city, it returns the region ID for the appropriate city. Otherwise, returns 0.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Accepts a region ID from the geobase. If this region is a city or part of a city, it returns the region ID for the appropriate city. Otherwise, returns 0.<sep>[SYNTAX]: regionToCity(id [, geobase])<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - id вҖ” Region ID from the geobase. UInt32 .
- geobase вҖ” Dictionary key. See Multiple Geobases . String . Optional.
Returned value
- Region ID for the appropriate city, if it exists. UInt32 .
- 0, if there is none.<sep>[RETURN]: - Region ID for the appropriate city, if it exists. UInt32 .
- 0, if there is none.<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT regionToName(number::UInt32, 'en'), regionToCity(number::UInt32) AS id, regionToName(id, 'en') FROM numbers(13); ; в”Ңв”ҖregionToName(CAST(number, 'UInt32'), 'en')в”Җв”¬в”Җidв”Җв”¬в”ҖregionToName(regionToCity(CAST(number, 'UInt32')), 'en')в”Җв”җ

description: Converts a region to an area (type 5 in the geobase). In every other way, this function is the same as 'regionToCity' .

[DESCRIPTION]: Converts a region to an area (type 5 in the geobase). In every other way, this function is the same as 'regionToCity' .<sep>[SYNTAX]: regionToArea(id [, geobase])<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - id вҖ” Region ID from the geobase. UInt32 .

description: Finds the highest continent in the hierarchy for the region.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Finds the highest continent in the hierarchy for the region.<sep>[SYNTAX]: regionToTopContinent(id[, geobase])<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - id вҖ” Region ID from the geobase. UInt32 .
- geobase вҖ” Dictionary key. See Multiple Geobases . String . Optional.
Returned value
- Identifier of the top level continent (the latter when you climb the hierarchy of regions). UInt32 .
- 0, if there is none.<sep>[RETURN]: - Identifier of the top level continent (the latter when you climb the hierarchy of regions). UInt32 .
- 0, if there is none.<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT regionToName(number::UInt32, 'en'), regionToTopContinent(number::UInt32) AS id, regionToName(id, 'en') FROM numbers(13); ; в”Ңв”ҖregionToName(CAST(number, 'UInt32'), 'en')в”Җв”¬в”Җidв”Җв”¬в”ҖregionToName(regionToTopContinent(CAST(number, 'UInt32')), 'en')в”Җв”җ
example: SELECT regionToName(number::UInt32, 'en'), regionToTopContinent(number::UInt32) AS id, regionToName(id, 'en') FROM numbers(13);

в”Ңв”ҖregionToName(CAST(number, 'UInt32'), 'en')в”Җв”¬в”Җidв”Җв”¬в”ҖregionToName(regionToTopContinent(CAST(number, 'UInt32')), 'en')в”Җв”җ

description: Gets the population for a region. The population can be recorded in files with the geobase. See the section "Dictionaries" . If the population is not recorded for the region, it returns 0. In the geobase, the population might be recorded for child regions, but not for parent regions.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Gets the population for a region. The population can be recorded in files with the geobase. See the section "Dictionaries" . If the population is not recorded for the region, it returns 0. In the geobase, the population might be recorded for child regions, but not for parent regions.<sep>[SYNTAX]: regionToPopulation(id[, geobase])<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - id вҖ” Region ID from the geobase. UInt32 .
- geobase вҖ” Dictionary key. See Multiple Geobases . String . Optional.
Returned value
- Population for the region. UInt32 .
- 0, if there is none.<sep>[RETURN]: - Population for the region. UInt32 .
- 0, if there is none.<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT regionToName(number::UInt32, 'en'), regionToPopulation(number::UInt32) AS id, regionToName(id, 'en') FROM numbers(13); ; в”Ңв”ҖregionToName(CAST(number, 'UInt32'), 'en')в”Җв”¬в”Җpopulationв”Җв”җ

description: Checks whether a lhs region belongs to a rhs region. Returns a UInt8 number equal to 1 if it belongs, or 0 if it does not belong.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Checks whether a lhs region belongs to a rhs region. Returns a UInt8 number equal to 1 if it belongs, or 0 if it does not belong.<sep>[SYNTAX]: regionIn(lhs, rhs\[, geobase\])<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - lhs вҖ” Lhs region ID from the geobase. UInt32 .
- rhs вҖ” Rhs region ID from the geobase. UInt32 .
- geobase вҖ” Dictionary key. See Multiple Geobases . String . Optional.
Returned value
- 1, if it belongs. UInt8 .
- 0, if it doesn't belong.
Implementation details
The relationship is reflexive вҖ“ any region also belongs to itself.<sep>[RETURN]: - 1, if it belongs. UInt8 .
- 0, if it doesn't belong.
Implementation details
The relationship is reflexive вҖ“ any region also belongs to itself.<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT regionToName(n1.number::UInt32, 'en') || (regionIn(n1.number::UInt32, n2.number::UInt32) ? ' is in ' : ' is not in ') || regionToName(n2.number::UInt32, 'en') FROM numbers(1,2) AS n1 CROSS JOIN numbers(1,5) AS n2; ; World is in World
example: SELECT regionToName(n1.number::UInt32, 'en') || (regionIn(n1.number::UInt32, n2.number::UInt32) ? ' is in ' : ' is not in ') || regionToName(n2.number::UInt32, 'en') FROM numbers(1,2) AS n1 CROSS JOIN numbers(1,5) AS n2;

World is in World
World is not in USA
World is not in Colorado
World is not in Boulder County
World is not in Boulder
USA is in World
USA is in USA
USA is not in Colorado
USA is not in Boulder County
USA is not in Boulder

description: Accepts a UInt32 number вҖ“ the region ID from the geobase. Returns an array of region IDs consisting of the passed region and all parents along the chain.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Accepts a UInt32 number вҖ“ the region ID from the geobase. Returns an array of region IDs consisting of the passed region and all parents along the chain.<sep>[SYNTAX]: regionHierarchy(id\[, geobase\])<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - id вҖ” Region ID from the geobase. UInt32 .
- geobase вҖ” Dictionary key. See Multiple Geobases . String . Optional.
Returned value
- Array of region IDs consisting of the passed region and all parents along the chain. Array ( UInt32 ).<sep>[RETURN]: - Array of region IDs consisting of the passed region and all parents along the chain. Array ( UInt32 ).<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT regionHierarchy(number::UInt32) AS arr, arrayMap(id -> regionToName(id, 'en'), arr) FROM numbers(5); ; в”Ңв”Җarrв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”ҖarrayMap(lambda(tuple(id), regionToName(id, 'en')), regionHierarchy(CAST(number, 'UInt32')))в”Җв”җ

description:
Returns a string containing the argument's hexadecimal representation.
Alias: HEX .
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Returns a string containing the argument's hexadecimal representation.
Alias: HEX .<sep>[SYNTAX]: hex(arg)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - arg вҖ” A value to convert to hexadecimal. Types: String , UInt , Float , Decimal , Date or DateTime .
Returned value
- A string with the hexadecimal representation of the argument. String .<sep>[RETURN]: - A string with the hexadecimal representation of the argument. String .<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT hex(1); ; 01 ; SELECT hex(toFloat32(number)) AS hex_presentation FROM numbers(15, 2);
example: SELECT hex(toFloat32(number)) AS hex_presentation FROM numbers(15, 2);
SELECT hex(toFloat64(number)) AS hex_presentation FROM numbers(15, 2);

description:
Performs the opposite operation of hex . It interprets each pair of hexadecimal digits (in the argument) as a number and converts it to the byte represented by the number. The return value is a binary string (BLOB).
If you want to convert the result to a number, you can use the reverse and reinterpretAs<Type> functions.
Alias: UNHEX .
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Performs the opposite operation of hex . It interprets each pair of hexadecimal digits (in the argument) as a number and converts it to the byte represented by the number. The return value is a binary string (BLOB).
If you want to convert the result to a number, you can use the reverse and reinterpretAs<Type> functions.
Alias: UNHEX .<sep>[SYNTAX]: unhex(arg)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - arg вҖ” A string containing any number of hexadecimal digits. String , FixedString .
Supports both uppercase and lowercase letters A-F . The number of hexadecimal digits does not have to be even. If it is odd, the last digit is interpreted as the least significant half of the 00-0F byte. If the argument string contains anything other than hexadecimal digits, some implementation-defined result is returned (an exception isn't thrown). For a numeric argument the inverse of hex(N) is not performed by unhex().
Returned value
- A binary string (BLOB). String .<sep>[RETURN]: - A binary string (BLOB). String .<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT unhex('303132'), UNHEX('4D7953514C'); ; в”Ңв”Җunhex('303132')в”Җв”¬в”Җunhex('4D7953514C')в”Җв”җ ; SELECT reinterpretAsUInt64(reverse(unhex('FFF'))) AS num;

description: Returns a string containing the argument's binary representation.
example: SELECT bin(toFloat32(number)) AS bin_presentation FROM numbers(15, 2);
SELECT bin(toFloat64(number)) AS bin_presentation FROM numbers(15, 2);

description: Interprets each pair of binary digits (in the argument) as a number and converts it to the byte represented by the number. The functions performs the opposite operation to bin .
Accepts an integer. Returns a string containing the list of powers of two that total the source number when summed. They are comma-separated without spaces in text format, in ascending order.
detail: [DESCRIPTION]: Accepts an integer. Returns a string containing the list of powers of two that total the source number when summed. They are comma-separated without spaces in text format, in ascending order.

description: Accepts an integer. Returns an array of UInt64 numbers containing the list of powers of two that total the source number when summed. Numbers in the array are in ascending order.
detail: [DESCRIPTION]: Accepts an integer. Returns an array of UInt64 numbers containing the list of powers of two that total the source number when summed. Numbers in the array are in ascending order.

description: Accepts an integer and converts it to an unsigned integer. Returns an array of UInt64 numbers containing the list of positions of bits of arg that equal 1 , in ascending order.
Accepts up to 8 unsigned integers as arguments and produces a UInt64 code.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Accepts up to 8 unsigned integers as arguments and produces a UInt64 code.<sep>[SYNTAX]: mortonEncode(args)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - args : up to 8 unsigned integers or columns of the aforementioned type.
Returned value
- A UInt64 code. UInt64<sep>[RETURN]: - A UInt64 code. UInt64<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT mortonEncode(1, 2, 3); ; 53

description:
Accepts a range mask ( tuple ) as a first argument and up to 8 unsigned integers as other arguments.
Each number in the mask configures the amount of range expansion: 1 - no expansion 2 - 2x expansion 3 - 3x expansion ... Up to 8x expansion.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Accepts a range mask ( tuple ) as a first argument and up to 8 unsigned integers as other arguments.
Each number in the mask configures the amount of range expansion: 1 - no expansion 2 - 2x expansion 3 - 3x expansion ... Up to 8x expansion.<sep>[SYNTAX]: mortonEncode(range_mask, args)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - range_mask : 1-8.
- args : up to 8 unsigned integers or columns of the aforementioned type.
Note: when using columns for args the provided range_mask tuple should still be a constant.
Returned value
- A UInt64 code. UInt64<sep>[RETURN]: - A UInt64 code. UInt64<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT mortonEncode((1,2), 1024, 16); ; 1572864

description: Encodes a binary data string, along with a human-readable part (HRP), using the Bech32 or Bech32m algorithms.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Encodes a binary data string, along with a human-readable part (HRP), using the Bech32 or Bech32m algorithms.<sep>[SYNTAX]: bech32Encode(hrp, data[, witver])<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - hrp вҖ” String of 1 - 83 lowercase characters specifying the "human-readable part" of the code. Usually 'bc' or 'tb'. String or FixedString .
- data вҖ” String of binary data to encode. String or FixedString .
- witver - Witness version. Optional, default = 1. An UInt* specifying the version of the algorithm to run. 0 for Bech32 and 1 or greater for Bech32m.
Returned value
- A Bech32 address string, consisting of the human-readable part, a separator character which is always '1', and a data part. The length of the string will never exceed 90 characters. If the algorithm cannot generate a valid address from the input, it will return an empty string.
Type: String .<sep>[RETURN]: - A Bech32 address string, consisting of the human-readable part, a separator character which is always '1', and a data part. The length of the string will never exceed 90 characters. If the algorithm cannot generate a valid address from the input, it will return an empty string.
Type: String .<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT bech32Encode('bc', unhex('751e76e8199196d454941c45d1b3a323f1433bd6')); ; bc1w508d6qejxtdg4y5r3zarvary0c5xw7k8zcwmq ; SELECT bech32Encode('bc', unhex('751e76e8199196d454941c45d1b3a323f1433bd6'), 0);

description: Decodes a Bech32 address string generated by either the bech32 or bech32m algorithms.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Decodes a Bech32 address string generated by either the bech32 or bech32m algorithms.<sep>[SYNTAX]: bech32Decode(address)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - address вҖ” Bech32 string to decode. String or FixedString .
Returned value
- A tuple consisting of the (hrp, data) that was used to encode the string. The data is in binary format.
Type: ( String , String ).<sep>[RETURN]: - A tuple consisting of the (hrp, data) that was used to encode the string. The data is in binary format.
Type: ( String , String ).<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT tup.1 AS hrp, hex(tup.2) AS data FROM (SELECT bech32Decode('bc1w508d6qejxtdg4y5r3zarvary0c5xw7kj7gz7z') AS tup); ; bc   751E76E8199196D454941C45D1B3A323F1433BD6 ; SELECT tup.1 AS hrp, hex(tup.2) AS data FROM (SELECT bech32Decode('tb1w508d6qejxtdg4y5r3zarvary0c5xw7kzp034v') AS tup);
example: SELECT tup.1 AS hrp, hex(tup.2) AS data FROM (SELECT bech32Decode('bc1w508d6qejxtdg4y5r3zarvary0c5xw7kj7gz7z') AS tup);
SELECT tup.1 AS hrp, hex(tup.2) AS data FROM (SELECT bech32Decode('tb1w508d6qejxtdg4y5r3zarvary0c5xw7kzp034v') AS tup);

description:
This function encrypts data using these modes:
- aes-128-ecb, aes-192-ecb, aes-256-ecb
- aes-128-cbc, aes-192-cbc, aes-256-cbc
- aes-128-ofb, aes-192-ofb, aes-256-ofb
- aes-128-gcm, aes-192-gcm, aes-256-gcm
- aes-128-ctr, aes-192-ctr, aes-256-ctr
- aes-128-cfb, aes-128-cfb1, aes-128-cfb8
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: This function encrypts data using these modes:
- aes-128-ecb, aes-192-ecb, aes-256-ecb
- aes-128-cbc, aes-192-cbc, aes-256-cbc
- aes-128-ofb, aes-192-ofb, aes-256-ofb
- aes-128-gcm, aes-192-gcm, aes-256-gcm
- aes-128-ctr, aes-192-ctr, aes-256-ctr
- aes-128-cfb, aes-128-cfb1, aes-128-cfb8<sep>[SYNTAX]: encrypt('mode', 'plaintext', 'key' [, iv, aad])<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - mode вҖ” Encryption mode. String .
- plaintext вҖ” Text that need to be encrypted. String .
- key вҖ” Encryption key. String .
- iv вҖ” Initialization vector. Required for -gcm modes, optional for others. String .
- aad вҖ” Additional authenticated data. It isn't encrypted, but it affects decryption. Works only in -gcm modes, for others would throw an exception. String .
Returned value
- Ciphertext binary string. String .<sep>[RETURN]: - Ciphertext binary string. String .<sep>[DEMO]: CREATE TABLE encryption_test ; INSERT INTO encryption_test VALUES('aes-256-ofb no IV', encrypt('aes-256-ofb', 'Secret', '12345678910121314151617181920212')),\ ; SELECT comment, hex(secret) FROM encryption_test;
example:
INSERT INTO encryption_test VALUES('aes-256-ofb no IV', encrypt('aes-256-ofb', 'Secret', '12345678910121314151617181920212')),\
('aes-256-ofb no IV, different key', encrypt('aes-256-ofb', 'Secret', 'keykeykeykeykeykeykeykeykeykeyke')),\
('aes-256-ofb with IV', encrypt('aes-256-ofb', 'Secret', '12345678910121314151617181920212', 'iviviviviviviviv')),\
('aes-256-cbc no IV', encrypt('aes-256-cbc', 'Secret', '12345678910121314151617181920212'));

description:
Compatible with mysql encryption and resulting ciphertext can be decrypted with AES_DECRYPT function.
Will produce the same ciphertext as encrypt on equal inputs. But when key or iv are longer than they should normally be, aes_encrypt_mysql will stick to what MySQL's aes_encrypt does: 'fold' key and ignore excess bits of iv .
Supported encryption modes:
- aes-128-ecb, aes-192-ecb, aes-256-ecb
- aes-128-cbc, aes-192-cbc, aes-256-cbc
- aes-128-ofb, aes-192-ofb, aes-256-ofb
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Compatible with mysql encryption and resulting ciphertext can be decrypted with AES_DECRYPT function.
Will produce the same ciphertext as encrypt on equal inputs. But when key or iv are longer than they should normally be, aes_encrypt_mysql will stick to what MySQL's aes_encrypt does: 'fold' key and ignore excess bits of iv .
Supported encryption modes:
- aes-128-ecb, aes-192-ecb, aes-256-ecb
- aes-128-cbc, aes-192-cbc, aes-256-cbc
- aes-128-ofb, aes-192-ofb, aes-256-ofb<sep>[SYNTAX]: aes_encrypt_mysql('mode', 'plaintext', 'key' [, iv])<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - mode вҖ” Encryption mode. String .
- plaintext вҖ” Text that needs to be encrypted. String .
- key вҖ” Encryption key. If key is longer than required by mode, MySQL-specific key folding is performed. String .
- iv вҖ” Initialization vector. Optional, only first 16 bytes are taken into account String .
Returned value
- Ciphertext binary string. String .<sep>[RETURN]: - Ciphertext binary string. String .<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT encrypt('aes-256-ofb', 'Secret', '12345678910121314151617181920212', 'iviviviviviviviv') = aes_encrypt_mysql('aes-256-ofb', 'Secret', '12345678910121314151617181920212', 'iviviviviviviviv') AS ciphertexts_equal; ; в”Ңв”Җciphertexts_equalв”Җв”җ ; SELECT encrypt('aes-256-ofb', 'Secret', '123456789101213141516171819202122', 'iviviviviviviviv123');

description:
This function decrypts ciphertext into a plaintext using these modes:
- aes-128-ecb, aes-192-ecb, aes-256-ecb
- aes-128-cbc, aes-192-cbc, aes-256-cbc
- aes-128-ofb, aes-192-ofb, aes-256-ofb
- aes-128-gcm, aes-192-gcm, aes-256-gcm
- aes-128-ctr, aes-192-ctr, aes-256-ctr
- aes-128-cfb, aes-128-cfb1, aes-128-cfb8
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: This function decrypts ciphertext into a plaintext using these modes:
- aes-128-ecb, aes-192-ecb, aes-256-ecb
- aes-128-cbc, aes-192-cbc, aes-256-cbc
- aes-128-ofb, aes-192-ofb, aes-256-ofb
- aes-128-gcm, aes-192-gcm, aes-256-gcm
- aes-128-ctr, aes-192-ctr, aes-256-ctr
- aes-128-cfb, aes-128-cfb1, aes-128-cfb8<sep>[SYNTAX]: decrypt('mode', 'ciphertext', 'key' [, iv, aad])<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - mode вҖ” Decryption mode. String .
- ciphertext вҖ” Encrypted text that needs to be decrypted. String .
- key вҖ” Decryption key. String .
- iv вҖ” Initialization vector. Required for -gcm modes, Optional for others. String .
- aad вҖ” Additional authenticated data. Won't decrypt if this value is incorrect. Works only in -gcm modes, for others would throw an exception. String .
Returned value
- Decrypted String. String .<sep>[RETURN]: - Decrypted String. String .<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT comment, hex(secret) FROM encryption_test; ; в”Ңв”Җcommentв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җhex(secret)в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ ; SELECT comment, decrypt('aes-256-cfb128', secret, '12345678910121314151617181920212') AS plaintext FROM encryption_test

description: Similar to decrypt , but returns NULL if decryption fails because of using the wrong key.
detail: [DESCRIPTION]: Similar to decrypt , but returns NULL if decryption fails because of using the wrong key.<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT ; в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җdtв”Җв”¬в”Җuser_idв”Җв”¬в”Җvalueв”Җв”Җв”җ

    user_id,

ORDER BY user_id ASC

description:
Compatible with mysql encryption and decrypts data encrypted with AES_ENCRYPT function.
Will produce same plaintext as decrypt on equal inputs. But when key or iv are longer than they should normally be, aes_decrypt_mysql will stick to what MySQL's aes_decrypt does: 'fold' key and ignore excess bits of IV .
Supported decryption modes:
- aes-128-ecb, aes-192-ecb, aes-256-ecb
- aes-128-cbc, aes-192-cbc, aes-256-cbc
- aes-128-cfb128
- aes-128-ofb, aes-192-ofb, aes-256-ofb
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Compatible with mysql encryption and decrypts data encrypted with AES_ENCRYPT function.
Will produce same plaintext as decrypt on equal inputs. But when key or iv are longer than they should normally be, aes_decrypt_mysql will stick to what MySQL's aes_decrypt does: 'fold' key and ignore excess bits of IV .
Supported decryption modes:
- aes-128-ecb, aes-192-ecb, aes-256-ecb
- aes-128-cbc, aes-192-cbc, aes-256-cbc
- aes-128-cfb128
- aes-128-ofb, aes-192-ofb, aes-256-ofb<sep>[SYNTAX]: aes_decrypt_mysql('mode', 'ciphertext', 'key' [, iv])<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - mode вҖ” Decryption mode. String .
- ciphertext вҖ” Encrypted text that needs to be decrypted. String .
- key вҖ” Decryption key. String .
- iv вҖ” Initialization vector. Optional. String .
Returned value

description: Retrieves values from a dictionary.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Retrieves values from a dictionary.<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - dict_name вҖ” Name of the dictionary. String literal .
- attr_names вҖ” Name of the column of the dictionary, String literal , or tuple of column names, Tuple ( String literal .
- id_expr вҖ” Key value. Expression returning dictionary key-type value or Tuple -type value depending on the dictionary configuration.
- default_value_expr вҖ” Values returned if the dictionary does not contain a row with the id_expr key. Expression or Tuple ( Expression ), returning the value (or values) in the data types configured for the attr_names attribute.
Returned value
- If ClickHouse parses the attribute successfully in the attribute's data type , functions return the value of the dictionary attribute that corresponds to id_expr .
- If there is no the key, corresponding to id_expr , in the dictionary, then: dictGet returns the content of the <null_value> element specified for the attribute in the dictionary configuration. dictGetOrDefault returns the value passed as the default_value_expr parameter. dictGetOrNull returns NULL in case key was not found in dictionary.
ClickHouse throws an exception if it cannot parse the value of the attribute or the value does not match the attribute data type.
Example for simple key dictionary
Create a text file ext-dict-test.csv containing the following:
1,1
2,2
The first column is id , the second column is c1 .
Configure the dictionary:
<clickhouse>
    <dictionary>
        <name>ext-dict-test</name>
        <source>
            <file>
                <path>/path-to/ext-dict-test.csv</path>
                <format>CSV</format>
            </file>
        </source>
        <layout>
            <flat />
        </layout>
        <structure>
            <id>
                <name>id</name>
            </id>
            <attribute>
                <name>c1</name>
                <type>UInt32</type>
                <null_value></null_value>
            </attribute>
        </structure>
        <lifetime>0</lifetime>
    </dictionary>
</clickhouse>
Perform the query:
SELECT
    dictGetOrDefault('ext-dict-test', 'c1', number + 1, toUInt32(number * 10)) AS val,
    toTypeName(val) AS type
FROM system.numbers
LIMIT 3;
в”Ңв”Җvalв”Җв”¬в”Җtypeв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
в”Ӯ   1 в”Ӯ UInt32 в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ   2 в”Ӯ UInt32 в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  20 в”Ӯ UInt32 в”Ӯ
в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
Example for complex key dictionary
Create a text file ext-dict-mult.csv containing the following:
1,1,'1'
2,2,'2'
3,3,'3'
The first column is id , the second is c1 , the third is c2 .
Configure the dictionary:
<clickhouse>
    <dictionary>
        <name>ext-dict-mult</name>
        <source>
            <file>
                <path>/path-to/ext-dict-mult.csv</path>
                <format>CSV</format>
            </file>
        </source>
        <layout>
            <flat />
        </layout>
        <structure>
            <id>
                <name>id</name>
            </id>
            <attribute>
                <name>c1</name>
                <type>UInt32</type>
                <null_value></null_value>
            </attribute>
            <attribute>
                <name>c2</name>
                <type>String</type>
                <null_value></null_value>
            </attribute>
        </structure>
        <lifetime>0</lifetime>
    </dictionary>
</clickhouse>
Perform the query:
SELECT
    dictGet('ext-dict-mult', ('c1','c2'), number + 1) AS val,
    toTypeName(val) AS type
FROM system.numbers
LIMIT 3;
в”Ңв”Җvalв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җtypeв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
в”Ӯ (1,'1') в”Ӯ Tuple(UInt8, String)  в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ (2,'2') в”Ӯ Tuple(UInt8, String)  в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ (3,'3') в”Ӯ Tuple(UInt8, String)  в”Ӯ
в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
Example for range key dictionary
Input table:
CREATE TABLE range_key_dictionary_source_table
(
    key UInt64,
    start_date Date,
    end_date Date,
    value String,
    value_nullable Nullable(String)
)
ENGINE = TinyLog();

INSERT INTO range_key_dictionary_source_table VALUES(1, toDate('2019-05-20'), toDate('2019-05-20'), 'First', 'First');
INSERT INTO range_key_dictionary_source_table VALUES(2, toDate('2019-05-20'), toDate('2019-05-20'), 'Second', NULL);
INSERT INTO range_key_dictionary_source_table VALUES(3, toDate('2019-05-20'), toDate('2019-05-20'), 'Third', 'Third');
Create the dictionary:
CREATE DICTIONARY range_key_dictionary
(
    key UInt64,
    start_date Date,
    end_date Date,
    value String,
    value_nullable Nullable(String)
)
PRIMARY KEY key
SOURCE(CLICKHOUSE(HOST 'localhost' PORT tcpPort() TABLE 'range_key_dictionary_source_table'))
LIFETIME(MIN 1 MAX 1000)
LAYOUT(RANGE_HASHED())
RANGE(MIN start_date MAX end_date);
Perform the query:
SELECT
    (number, toDate('2019-05-20')),
    dictHas('range_key_dictionary', number, toDate('2019-05-20')),
    dictGetOrNull('range_key_dictionary', 'value', number, toDate('2019-05-20')),
    dictGetOrNull('range_key_dictionary', 'value_nullable', number, toDate('2019-05-20')),
    dictGetOrNull('range_key_dictionary', ('value', 'value_nullable'), number, toDate('2019-05-20'))
FROM system.numbers LIMIT 5 FORMAT TabSeparated;<sep>[RETURN]: - If ClickHouse parses the attribute successfully in the attribute's data type , functions return the value of the dictionary attribute that corresponds to id_expr .
- If there is no the key, corresponding to id_expr , in the dictionary, then: dictGet returns the content of the <null_value> element specified for the attribute in the dictionary configuration. dictGetOrDefault returns the value passed as the default_value_expr parameter. dictGetOrNull returns NULL in case key was not found in dictionary.
ClickHouse throws an exception if it cannot parse the value of the attribute or the value does not match the attribute data type.

        </layout>

            <id>

            </id>

                <name>c1</name>
                <type>UInt32</type>
                <null_value></null_value>
            </attribute>
        </structure>

    </dictionary>
</clickhouse>
Perform the query:
SELECT

    toTypeName(val) AS type

LIMIT 3;
в”Ңв”Җvalв”Җв”¬в”Җtypeв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ

INSERT INTO range_key_dictionary_source_table VALUES(2, toDate('2019-05-20'), toDate('2019-05-20'), 'Second', NULL);
INSERT INTO range_key_dictionary_source_table VALUES(3, toDate('2019-05-20'), toDate('2019-05-20'), 'Third', 'Third');
Create the dictionary:
CREATE DICTIONARY range_key_dictionary
(
    key UInt64,
    start_date Date,
    end_date Date,
    value String,
    value_nullable Nullable(String)
)
PRIMARY KEY key
SOURCE(CLICKHOUSE(HOST 'localhost' PORT tcpPort() TABLE 'range_key_dictionary_source_table'))
LIFETIME(MIN 1 MAX 1000)
LAYOUT(RANGE_HASHED())
RANGE(MIN start_date MAX end_date);
Perform the query:
SELECT
    (number, toDate('2019-05-20')),
    dictHas('range_key_dictionary', number, toDate('2019-05-20')),
    dictGetOrNull('range_key_dictionary', 'value', number, toDate('2019-05-20')),
    dictGetOrNull('range_key_dictionary', 'value_nullable', number, toDate('2019-05-20')),
    dictGetOrNull('range_key_dictionary', ('value', 'value_nullable'), number, toDate('2019-05-20'))
FROM system.numbers LIMIT 5 FORMAT TabSeparated;<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT ; в”Ңв”Җvalв”Җв”¬в”Җtypeв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ ; SELECT
example:
SELECT
    dictGetOrDefault('ext-dict-test', 'c1', number + 1, toUInt32(number * 10)) AS val,
    toTypeName(val) AS type
FROM system.numbers
LIMIT 3;

в”Ңв”Җvalв”Җв”¬в”Җtypeв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
в”Ӯ   1 в”Ӯ UInt32 в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ   2 в”Ӯ UInt32 в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  20 в”Ӯ UInt32 в”Ӯ
в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ

SELECT
    dictGet('ext-dict-mult', ('c1','c2'), number + 1) AS val,
    toTypeName(val) AS type
FROM system.numbers
LIMIT 3;

в”Ңв”Җvalв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җtypeв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
в”Ӯ (1,'1') в”Ӯ Tuple(UInt8, String)  в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ (2,'2') в”Ӯ Tuple(UInt8, String)  в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ (3,'3') в”Ӯ Tuple(UInt8, String)  в”Ӯ
в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ

    (number, toDate('2019-05-20')),
    dictHas('range_key_dictionary', number, toDate('2019-05-20')),
    dictGetOrNull('range_key_dictionary', 'value', number, toDate('2019-05-20')),

description: Checks whether a key is present in a dictionary.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Checks whether a key is present in a dictionary.<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - dict_name вҖ” Name of the dictionary. String literal .
- id_expr вҖ” Key value. Expression returning dictionary key-type value or Tuple -type value depending on the dictionary configuration.
Returned value
- 0, if there is no key. UInt8 .
- 1, if there is a key. UInt8 .<sep>[RETURN]: - 0, if there is no key. UInt8 .
- 1, if there is a key. UInt8 .<sep>[DEMO]: dictHas('dict_name', id_expr)

description: Creates an array, containing all the parents of a key in the hierarchical dictionary .
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Creates an array, containing all the parents of a key in the hierarchical dictionary .<sep>[SYNTAX]: dictGetHierarchy('dict_name', key)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - dict_name вҖ” Name of the dictionary. String literal .
- key вҖ” Key value. Expression returning a UInt64 -type value.
Returned value
- Parents for the key. Array(UInt64) .<sep>[RETURN]: - Parents for the key. Array(UInt64) .

description: Checks the ancestor of a key through the whole hierarchical chain in the dictionary.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Checks the ancestor of a key through the whole hierarchical chain in the dictionary.<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - dict_name вҖ” Name of the dictionary. String literal .
- child_id_expr вҖ” Key to be checked. Expression returning a UInt64 -type value.
- ancestor_id_expr вҖ” Alleged ancestor of the child_id_expr key. Expression returning a UInt64 -type value.
Returned value
- 0, if child_id_expr is not a child of ancestor_id_expr . UInt8 .
- 1, if child_id_expr is a child of ancestor_id_expr or if child_id_expr is an ancestor_id_expr . UInt8 .<sep>[RETURN]: - 0, if child_id_expr is not a child of ancestor_id_expr . UInt8 .
- 1, if child_id_expr is a child of ancestor_id_expr or if child_id_expr is an ancestor_id_expr . UInt8 .

description: Returns first-level children as an array of indexes. It is the inverse transformation for dictGetHierarchy .
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Returns first-level children as an array of indexes. It is the inverse transformation for dictGetHierarchy .<sep>[SYNTAX]: dictGetChildren(dict_name, key)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - dict_name вҖ” Name of the dictionary. String literal .
- key вҖ” Key value. Expression returning a UInt64 -type value.
Returned values
- First-level descendants for the key. Array ( UInt64 ).<sep>[RETURN]: - First-level descendants for the key. Array ( UInt64 ).<sep>[DEMO]: в”Ңв”Җidв”Җв”¬в”Җparent_idв”Җв”җ ; SELECT dictGetChildren('hierarchy_flat_dictionary', number) FROM system.numbers LIMIT 4; ; в”Ңв”ҖdictGetChildren('hierarchy_flat_dictionary', number)в”Җв”җ

description: Returns all descendants as if dictGetChildren function was applied level times recursively.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Returns all descendants as if dictGetChildren function was applied level times recursively.<sep>[SYNTAX]: dictGetDescendants(dict_name, key, level)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - dict_name вҖ” Name of the dictionary. String literal .
- key вҖ” Key value. Expression returning a UInt64 -type value.
- level вҖ” Hierarchy level. If level = 0 returns all descendants to the end. UInt8 .
Returned values
- Descendants for the key. Array ( UInt64 ).<sep>[RETURN]: - Descendants for the key. Array ( UInt64 ).<sep>[DEMO]: в”Ңв”Җidв”Җв”¬в”Җparent_idв”Җв”җ ; SELECT dictGetDescendants('hierarchy_flat_dictionary', number) FROM system.numbers LIMIT 4; ; в”Ңв”ҖdictGetDescendants('hierarchy_flat_dictionary', number)в”Җв”җ

description:
Retrieves the attribute values of all nodes that matched each key in a regular expression tree dictionary .
Besides returning values of type Array(T) instead of T , this function behaves similarly to dictGet .
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Retrieves the attribute values of all nodes that matched each key in a regular expression tree dictionary .
Besides returning values of type Array(T) instead of T , this function behaves similarly to dictGet .<sep>[SYNTAX]: dictGetAll('dict_name', attr_names, id_expr[, limit])<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - dict_name вҖ” Name of the dictionary. String literal .
- attr_names вҖ” Name of the column of the dictionary, String literal , or tuple of column names, Tuple ( String literal ).
- id_expr вҖ” Key value. Expression returning array of dictionary key-type value or Tuple -type value depending on the dictionary configuration.
- limit - Maximum length for each value array returned. When truncating, child nodes are given precedence over parent nodes, and otherwise the defined list order for the regexp tree dictionary is respected. If unspecified, array length is unlimited.
Returned value
- If ClickHouse parses the attribute successfully in the attribute's data type as defined in the dictionary, returns an array of dictionary attribute values that correspond to id_expr for each attribute specified by attr_names .
- If there is no key corresponding to id_expr in the dictionary, then an empty array is returned.
ClickHouse throws an exception if it cannot parse the value of the attribute or the value does not match the attribute data type.<sep>[RETURN]: - If ClickHouse parses the attribute successfully in the attribute's data type as defined in the dictionary, returns an array of dictionary attribute values that correspond to id_expr for each attribute specified by attr_names .
- If there is no key corresponding to id_expr in the dictionary, then an empty array is returned.
ClickHouse throws an exception if it cannot parse the value of the attribute or the value does not match the attribute data type.<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT dictGetAll('regexp_dict', 'tag', 'foobarbaz'); ; в”Ңв”ҖdictGetAll('regexp_dict', 'tag', 'foobarbaz')в”Җв”җ ; SELECT dictGetAll('regexp_dict', 'tag', 'foobarbaz', 2);

description:
ClickHouse supports specialized functions that convert dictionary attribute values to a specific data type regardless of the dictionary configuration.
Functions:
- dictGetInt8 , dictGetInt16 , dictGetInt32 , dictGetInt64
- dictGetUInt8 , dictGetUInt16 , dictGetUInt32 , dictGetUInt64
- dictGetFloat32 , dictGetFloat64
- dictGetDate
- dictGetDateTime
- dictGetUUID
- dictGetString
- dictGetIPv4 , dictGetIPv6
All these functions have the OrDefault modification. For example, dictGetDateOrDefault .
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: ClickHouse supports specialized functions that convert dictionary attribute values to a specific data type regardless of the dictionary configuration.
Functions:
- dictGetInt8 , dictGetInt16 , dictGetInt32 , dictGetInt64
- dictGetUInt8 , dictGetUInt16 , dictGetUInt32 , dictGetUInt64
- dictGetFloat32 , dictGetFloat64
- dictGetDate
- dictGetDateTime
- dictGetUUID
- dictGetString
- dictGetIPv4 , dictGetIPv6
All these functions have the OrDefault modification. For example, dictGetDateOrDefault .<sep>[SYNTAX]: dictGet[Type]('dict_name', 'attr_name', id_expr)
dictGet[Type]OrDefault('dict_name', 'attr_name', id_expr, default_value_expr)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - dict_name вҖ” Name of the dictionary. String literal .
- attr_name вҖ” Name of the column of the dictionary. String literal .
- id_expr вҖ” Key value. Expression returning a UInt64 or Tuple -type value depending on the dictionary configuration.
- default_value_expr вҖ” Value returned if the dictionary does not contain a row with the id_expr key. Expression returning the value in the data type configured for the attr_name attribute.
Returned value
- If ClickHouse parses the attribute successfully in the attribute's data type , functions return the value of the dictionary attribute that corresponds to id_expr .
- If there is no requested id_expr in the dictionary then: dictGet[Type] returns the content of the <null_value> element specified for the attribute in the dictionary configuration. dictGet[Type]OrDefault returns the value passed as the default_value_expr parameter.
ClickHouse throws an exception if it cannot parse the value of the attribute or the value does not match the attribute data type.<sep>[RETURN]: - If ClickHouse parses the attribute successfully in the attribute's data type , functions return the value of the dictionary attribute that corresponds to id_expr .
- If there is no requested id_expr in the dictionary then: dictGet[Type] returns the content of the <null_value> element specified for the attribute in the dictionary configuration. dictGet[Type]OrDefault returns the value passed as the default_value_expr parameter.
ClickHouse throws an exception if it cannot parse the value of the attribute or the value does not match the attribute data type.

description:
Reads a file as string and loads the data into the specified column. The file content is not interpreted.
Also see table function file .
example: INSERT INTO table SELECT file('a.txt'), file('b.txt');

Calculates the Internal Rate of Return (IRR) for a series of cash flows occurring at regular intervals. IRR is the discount rate at which the Net Present Value (NPV) equals zero.
IRR attempts to solve the following equation:
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Introduced in: v25.7
Calculates the Internal Rate of Return (IRR) for a series of cash flows occurring at regular intervals. IRR is the discount rate at which the Net Present Value (NPV) equals zero.
IRR attempts to solve the following equation:<sep>[SYNTAX]: financialInternalRateOfReturn(cashflows[, guess])<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - cashflows вҖ” Array of cash flows. Each value represents a payment (negative value) or income (positive value). Array(Int8/16/32/64) or Array(Float*)
- [, guess] вҖ” Optional initial guess (constant value) for the internal rate of return (default 0.1). Float*
Returned value
Returns the internal rate of return or NaN if the calculation cannot converge, input array is empty or has only one element, all cash flows are zero, or other calculation errors occur. Float64<sep>[RETURN]: Returns the internal rate of return or NaN if the calculation cannot converge, input array is empty or has only one element, all cash flows are zero, or other calculation errors occur. Float64<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT financialInternalRateOfReturn([-100, 39, 59, 55, 20]) ; 0.2809484211599611 ; SELECT financialInternalRateOfReturn([-100, 39, 59, 55, 20], 0.1)

description:
Introduced in: v25.7
Calculates the Extended Internal Rate of Return (XIRR) for a series of cash flows occurring at irregular intervals. XIRR is the discount rate at which the net present value (NPV) of all cash flows equals zero.
XIRR attempts to solve the following equation (example for ACT_365F ):
Arrays should be sorted by date in ascending order. Dates need to be unique.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Introduced in: v25.7
Calculates the Extended Internal Rate of Return (XIRR) for a series of cash flows occurring at irregular intervals. XIRR is the discount rate at which the net present value (NPV) of all cash flows equals zero.
XIRR attempts to solve the following equation (example for ACT_365F ):
Arrays should be sorted by date in ascending order. Dates need to be unique.<sep>[SYNTAX]: financialInternalRateOfReturnExtended(cashflow, date [, guess, daycount])<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - cashflow вҖ” An array of cash flows corresponding to the dates in second param. Array(Int8/16/32/64) or Array(Float*)
- date вҖ” A sorted array of unique dates corresponding to the cash flows. Array(Date) or Array(Date32)
- [, guess] вҖ” Optional. Initial guess (constant value) for the XIRR calculation. Float*
- [, daycount] вҖ” Optional day count convention (default 'ACT_365F'). Supported values:
- 'ACT_365F' - Actual/365 Fixed: Uses actual number of days between dates divided by 365
- 'ACT_365_25' - Actual/365.25: Uses actual number of days between dates divided by 365.25 String
Returned value
Returns the XIRR value. If the calculation cannot be performed, it returns NaN. Float64<sep>[RETURN]: Returns the XIRR value. If the calculation cannot be performed, it returns NaN. Float64<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT financialInternalRateOfReturnExtended([-10000, 5750, 4250, 3250], [toDate('2020-01-01'), toDate('2020-03-01'), toDate('2020-10-30'), toDate('2021-02-15')]) ; 0.6342972615260243 ; SELECT financialInternalRateOfReturnExtended([-10000, 5750, 4250, 3250], [toDate('2020-01-01'), toDate('2020-03-01'), toDate('2020-10-30'), toDate('2021-02-15')], 0.5)

Default variant ( start_from_zero = true):
Excel-compatible variant ( start_from_zero = false):
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Introduced in: v25.7
Calculates the Net Present Value (NPV) of a series of cash flows assuming equal time intervals between each cash flow.
Default variant ( start_from_zero = true):
Excel-compatible variant ( start_from_zero = false):<sep>[SYNTAX]: financialNetPresentValue(rate, cashflows[, start_from_zero])<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - rate вҖ” The discount rate to apply. Float*
- cashflows вҖ” Array of cash flows. Each value represents a payment (negative value) or income (positive value). Array(Int8/16/32/64) or Array(Float*)
- [, start_from_zero] вҖ” Optional boolean parameter indicating whether to start the NPV calculation from period 0 (true) or period 1 (false, Excel-compatible). Default: true. Bool
Returned value
Returns the net present value as a Float64 value. Float64<sep>[RETURN]: Returns the net present value as a Float64 value. Float64<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT financialNetPresentValue(0.08, [-40000., 5000., 8000., 12000., 30000.]) ; 3065.2226681795255 ; SELECT financialNetPresentValue(0.08, [-40000., 5000., 8000., 12000., 30000.], false)

description:
Introduced in: v25.7
Calculates the Extended Net Present Value (XNPV) for a series of cash flows occurring at irregular intervals. XNPV considers the specific timing of each cash flow when calculating present value.
XNPV equation for ACT_365F :
Arrays should be sorted by date in ascending order. Dates need to be unique.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Introduced in: v25.7
Calculates the Extended Net Present Value (XNPV) for a series of cash flows occurring at irregular intervals. XNPV considers the specific timing of each cash flow when calculating present value.
XNPV equation for ACT_365F :
Arrays should be sorted by date in ascending order. Dates need to be unique.<sep>[SYNTAX]: financialNetPresentValueExtended(rate, cashflows, dates[, daycount])<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - rate вҖ” The discount rate to apply. Float*
- cashflows вҖ” Array of cash flows. Each value represents a payment (negative value) or income (positive value). Must contain at least one positive and one negative value. Array(Int8/16/32/64) or Array(Float*)
- dates вҖ” Array of dates corresponding to each cash flow. Must have the same size as cashflows array. Array(Date) or Array(Date32)
- [, daycount] вҖ” Optional day count convention. Supported values: 'ACT_365F' (default) вҖ” Actual/365 Fixed, 'ACT_365_25' вҖ” Actual/365.25. String
Returned value
Returns the net present value as a Float64 value. Float64<sep>[RETURN]: Returns the net present value as a Float64 value. Float64<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT financialNetPresentValueExtended(0.1, [-10000., 5750., 4250., 3250.], [toDate('2020-01-01'), toDate('2020-03-01'), toDate('2020-10-30'), toDate('2021-02-15')]) ; 2506.579458169746 ; SELECT financialNetPresentValueExtended(0.1, [-10000., 5750., 4250., 3250.], [toDate('2020-01-01'), toDate('2020-03-01'), toDate('2020-10-30'), toDate('2021-02-15')], 'ACT_365_25')

description:
Introduced in: v1.1
Returns the corresponding non- Nullable value for a value of type Nullable . If the original value is NULL , an arbitrary result can be returned.
See also: functions ifNull and coalesce .
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Introduced in: v1.1
Returns the corresponding non- Nullable value for a value of type Nullable . If the original value is NULL , an arbitrary result can be returned.
See also: functions ifNull and coalesce .<sep>[SYNTAX]: assumeNotNull(x)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - x вҖ” The original value of any nullable type. Nullable(T)
Returned value
Returns the non-nullable value, if the original value was not NULL , otherwise an arbitrary value, if the input value is NULL . Any<sep>[RETURN]: Returns the non-nullable value, if the original value was not NULL , otherwise an arbitrary value, if the input value is NULL . Any<sep>[DEMO]: в”Ңв”ҖassumeNotNull(y)в”Җв”җ
example:
в”Ңв”ҖassumeNotNull(y)в”Җв”җ
в”Ӯ                0 в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ                3 в”Ӯ

description:
Introduced in: v1.1
Returns the leftmost non- NULL argument.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Introduced in: v1.1
Returns the leftmost non- NULL argument.<sep>[SYNTAX]: coalesce(x[, y, ...])<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - x[, y, ...] вҖ” Any number of parameters of non-compound type. All parameters must be of mutually compatible data types. Any
Returned value
Returns the first non- NULL argument, otherwise NULL , if all arguments are NULL . Any or NULL<sep>[RETURN]: Returns the first non- NULL argument, otherwise NULL , if all arguments are NULL . Any or NULL<sep>[DEMO]: в”Ңв”Җnameв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җcoalesce(mail, phone, CAST(telegram, 'Nullable(String)'))в”Җв”җ
example:
в”Ңв”Җnameв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җcoalesce(mail, phone, CAST(telegram, 'Nullable(String)'))в”Җв”җ

description:
Introduced in: v23.8
Performs a null-safe comparison between two JOIN keys. This function will consider two NULL values as identical and will return true , which is distinct from the usual equals behavior where comparing two NULL values would return NULL .
For a complete example see: NULL values in JOIN keys .

description:
Interprets all the input parameters as strings and calculates the MD5 hash value for each of them. Then combines hashes, takes the first 8 bytes of the hash of the resulting string, and interprets them as UInt64 in big-endian byte order.
The function is relatively slow (5 million short strings per second per processor core). Consider using the sipHash64 function instead.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Interprets all the input parameters as strings and calculates the MD5 hash value for each of them. Then combines hashes, takes the first 8 bytes of the hash of the resulting string, and interprets them as UInt64 in big-endian byte order.
The function is relatively slow (5 million short strings per second per processor core). Consider using the sipHash64 function instead.<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: The function takes a variable number of input parameters. Arguments can be any of the supported data types . For some data types calculated value of hash function may be the same for the same values even if types of arguments differ (integers of different size, named and unnamed Tuple with the same data, Map and the corresponding Array(Tuple(key, value)) type with the same data).
Returned Value
A UInt64 data type hash value.<sep>[RETURN]: A UInt64 data type hash value.<sep>[DEMO]: halfMD5(par1, ...) ; SELECT halfMD5(array('e','x','a'), 'mple', 10, toDateTime('2019-06-15 23:00:00')) AS halfMD5hash, toTypeName(halfMD5hash) AS type; ; в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҖhalfMD5hashв”Җв”¬в”Җtypeв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
example: SELECT halfMD5(array('e','x','a'), 'mple', 10, toDateTime('2019-06-15 23:00:00')) AS halfMD5hash, toTypeName(halfMD5hash) AS type;

description: Calculates the MD4 from a string and returns the resulting set of bytes as FixedString(16).
detail: [DESCRIPTION]: Calculates the MD4 from a string and returns the resulting set of bytes as FixedString(16).

description: Calculates the MD5 from a string and returns the resulting set of bytes as FixedString(16). If you do not need MD5 in particular, but you need a decent cryptographic 128-bit hash, use the 'sipHash128' function instead. If you want to get the same result as output by the md5sum utility, use lower(hex(MD5(s))).
detail: [DESCRIPTION]: Calculates the MD5 from a string and returns the resulting set of bytes as FixedString(16). If you do not need MD5 in particular, but you need a decent cryptographic 128-bit hash, use the 'sipHash128' function instead. If you want to get the same result as output by the md5sum utility, use lower(hex(MD5(s))).

description: Produces RIPEMD-160 hash value.
example: SELECT HEX(RIPEMD160('The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog'));

description:
Produces a 64-bit SipHash hash value.
This is a cryptographic hash function. It works at least three times faster than the MD5 hash function.
The function interprets all the input parameters as strings and calculates the hash value for each of them. It then combines the hashes by the following algorithm:
- The first and the second hash value are concatenated to an array which is hashed.
- The previously calculated hash value and the hash of the third input parameter are hashed in a similar way.
- This calculation is repeated for all remaining hash values of the original input.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Produces a 64-bit SipHash hash value.
This is a cryptographic hash function. It works at least three times faster than the MD5 hash function.
The function interprets all the input parameters as strings and calculates the hash value for each of them. It then combines the hashes by the following algorithm:
- The first and the second hash value are concatenated to an array which is hashed.
- The previously calculated hash value and the hash of the third input parameter are hashed in a similar way.
- This calculation is repeated for all remaining hash values of the original input.<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: The function takes a variable number of input parameters of any of the supported data types .
Returned Value
A UInt64 data type hash value.
Note that the calculated hash values may be equal for the same input values of different argument types. This affects for example integer types of different size, named and unnamed Tuple with the same data, Map and the corresponding Array(Tuple(key, value)) type with the same data.<sep>[RETURN]: A UInt64 data type hash value.
Note that the calculated hash values may be equal for the same input values of different argument types. This affects for example integer types of different size, named and unnamed Tuple with the same data, Map and the corresponding Array(Tuple(key, value)) type with the same data.<sep>[DEMO]: sipHash64(par1,...) ; SELECT sipHash64(array('e','x','a'), 'mple', 10, toDateTime('2019-06-15 23:00:00')) AS SipHash, toTypeName(SipHash) AS type; ; в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҖSipHashв”Җв”¬в”Җtypeв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
example: SELECT sipHash64(array('e','x','a'), 'mple', 10, toDateTime('2019-06-15 23:00:00')) AS SipHash, toTypeName(SipHash) AS type;

description: Same as sipHash64 but additionally takes an explicit key argument instead of using a fixed key.
example: SELECT sipHash64Keyed((506097522914230528, 1084818905618843912), array('e','x','a'), 'mple', 10, toDateTime('2019-06-15 23:00:00')) AS SipHash, toTypeName(SipHash) AS type;

description: Like sipHash64 but produces a 128-bit hash value, i.e. the final xor-folding state is done up to 128 bits.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Like sipHash64 but produces a 128-bit hash value, i.e. the final xor-folding state is done up to 128 bits.<sep>[SYNTAX]: sipHash128(par1,...)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: Same as for sipHash64 .
Returned value
A 128-bit SipHash hash value of type FixedString(16) .<sep>[RETURN]: A 128-bit SipHash hash value of type FixedString(16) .<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT hex(sipHash128('foo', '\x01', 3)); ; в”Ңв”Җhex(sipHash128('foo', '', 3))в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ

description: Same as sipHash128 but additionally takes an explicit key argument instead of using a fixed key.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Same as sipHash128 but additionally takes an explicit key argument instead of using a fixed key.<sep>[SYNTAX]: sipHash128Keyed((k0, k1), par1,...)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: Same as sipHash128 , but the first argument is a tuple of two UInt64 values representing the key.
Returned value
A 128-bit SipHash hash value of type FixedString(16) .<sep>[RETURN]: A 128-bit SipHash hash value of type FixedString(16) .<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT hex(sipHash128Keyed((506097522914230528, 1084818905618843912),'foo', '\x01', 3)); ; в”Ңв”Җhex(sipHash128Keyed((506097522914230528, 1084818905618843912), 'foo', '', 3))в”Җв”җ

description: Like sipHash128 but implements the 128-bit algorithm from the original authors of SipHash.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Like sipHash128 but implements the 128-bit algorithm from the original authors of SipHash.<sep>[SYNTAX]: sipHash128Reference(par1,...)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: Same as for sipHash128 .
Returned value
A 128-bit SipHash hash value of type FixedString(16) .<sep>[RETURN]: A 128-bit SipHash hash value of type FixedString(16) .<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT hex(sipHash128Reference('foo', '\x01', 3)); ; в”Ңв”Җhex(sipHash128Reference('foo', '', 3))в”Җв”җ

description: Same as sipHash128Reference but additionally takes an explicit key argument instead of using a fixed key.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Same as sipHash128Reference but additionally takes an explicit key argument instead of using a fixed key.<sep>[SYNTAX]: sipHash128ReferenceKeyed((k0, k1), par1,...)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: Same as sipHash128Reference , but the first argument is a tuple of two UInt64 values representing the key.
Returned value
A 128-bit SipHash hash value of type FixedString(16) .<sep>[RETURN]: A 128-bit SipHash hash value of type FixedString(16) .<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT hex(sipHash128ReferenceKeyed((506097522914230528, 1084818905618843912),'foo', '\x01', 3)); ; в”Ңв”Җhex(sipHash128ReferenceKeyed((506097522914230528, 1084818905618843912), 'foo', '', 3))в”Җв”җ

description:
Produces a 64-bit CityHash hash value.
This is a fast non-cryptographic hash function. It uses the CityHash algorithm for string parameters and implementation-specific fast non-cryptographic hash function for parameters with other data types. The function uses the CityHash combinator to get the final results.
Note that Google changed the algorithm of CityHash after it has been added to ClickHouse. In other words, ClickHouse's cityHash64 and Google's upstream CityHash now produce different results. ClickHouse cityHash64 corresponds to CityHash v1.0.2.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Produces a 64-bit CityHash hash value.
This is a fast non-cryptographic hash function. It uses the CityHash algorithm for string parameters and implementation-specific fast non-cryptographic hash function for parameters with other data types. The function uses the CityHash combinator to get the final results.
Note that Google changed the algorithm of CityHash after it has been added to ClickHouse. In other words, ClickHouse's cityHash64 and Google's upstream CityHash now produce different results. ClickHouse cityHash64 corresponds to CityHash v1.0.2.<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: The function takes a variable number of input parameters. Arguments can be any of the supported data types . For some data types calculated value of hash function may be the same for the same values even if types of arguments differ (integers of different size, named and unnamed Tuple with the same data, Map and the corresponding Array(Tuple(key, value)) type with the same data).
Returned Value
A UInt64 data type hash value.<sep>[RETURN]: A UInt64 data type hash value.<sep>[DEMO]: cityHash64(par1,...) ; SELECT cityHash64(array('e','x','a'), 'mple', 10, toDateTime('2019-06-15 23:00:00')) AS CityHash, toTypeName(CityHash) AS type; ; в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҖCityHashв”Җв”¬в”Җtypeв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ

Returned value

description: Calculates a 64-bit hash code from any type of integer. This is a relatively fast non-cryptographic hash function of average quality for numbers. It works faster than intHash32 .

description: Calculates SHA-1, SHA-224, SHA-256, SHA-512, SHA-512-256 hash from a string and returns the resulting set of bytes as FixedString .
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Calculates SHA-1, SHA-224, SHA-256, SHA-512, SHA-512-256 hash from a string and returns the resulting set of bytes as FixedString .<sep>[SYNTAX]: SHA1('s')
...
SHA512('s')<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - s вҖ” Input string for SHA hash calculation. String .
Returned value
- SHA hash as a hex-unencoded FixedString. SHA-1 returns as FixedString(20), SHA-224 as FixedString(28), SHA-256 вҖ” FixedString(32), SHA-512 вҖ” FixedString(64). FixedString .<sep>[RETURN]: - SHA hash as a hex-unencoded FixedString. SHA-1 returns as FixedString(20), SHA-224 as FixedString(28), SHA-256 вҖ” FixedString(32), SHA-512 вҖ” FixedString(64). FixedString .<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT hex(SHA1('abc')); ; в”Ңв”Җhex(SHA1('abc'))в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ

description: A fast, decent-quality non-cryptographic hash function for a string obtained from a URL using some type of normalization. URLHash(s) вҖ“ Calculates a hash from a string without one of the trailing symbols / , ? or # at the end, if present. URLHash(s, N) вҖ“ Calculates a hash from a string up to the N level in the URL hierarchy, without one of the trailing symbols / , ? or # at the end, if present. Levels are the same as in URLHierarchy.

Levels are the same as in URLHierarchy.

description:
Calculates JavaHash from a string , Byte , Short , Integer , Long . This hash function is neither fast nor having a good quality. The only reason to use it is when this algorithm is already used in another system and you have to calculate exactly the same result.
Note that Java only support calculating signed integers hash, so if you want to calculate unsigned integers hash you must cast it to proper signed ClickHouse types.
detail:

description:
Calculates HiveHash from a string.
This is just JavaHash with zeroed out sign bit. This function is used in Apache Hive for versions before 3.0. This hash function is neither fast nor having a good quality. The only reason to use it is when this algorithm is already used in another system and you have to calculate exactly the same result.
Returned value
- hiveHash hash value. Int32 .

- hiveHash hash value. Int32 .<sep>[RETURN]: - hiveHash hash value. Int32 .<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT hiveHash('Hello, world!'); ; в”Ңв”ҖhiveHash('Hello, world!')в”Җв”җ

description: Produces a 64-bit MetroHash hash value.
detail:

A UInt64 data type hash value.<sep>[RETURN]: A UInt64 data type hash value.<sep>[DEMO]: metroHash64(par1, ...) ; SELECT metroHash64(array('e','x','a'), 'mple', 10, toDateTime('2019-06-15 23:00:00')) AS MetroHash, toTypeName(MetroHash) AS type; ; в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҖMetroHashв”Җв”¬в”Җtypeв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ

description: An O(1) time and space consistent hash algorithm by Konstantin 'kostik' Oblakov. Previously yandexConsistentHash .
detail:

- n : Number of buckets. UInt16 .
Returned value
- A UInt16 data type hash value.
Implementation details
It is efficient only if n <= 32768.<sep>[RETURN]: - A UInt16 data type hash value.
Implementation details
It is efficient only if n <= 32768.<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT kostikConsistentHash(16045690984833335023, 2);

description:
Splits a ASCII string into n-grams of ngramsize symbols and returns the n-gram simhash . Is case sensitive.
Can be used for detection of semi-duplicate strings with bitHammingDistance . The smaller is the Hamming Distance of the calculated simhashes of two strings, the more likely these strings are the same.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Splits a ASCII string into n-grams of ngramsize symbols and returns the n-gram simhash . Is case sensitive.
Can be used for detection of semi-duplicate strings with bitHammingDistance . The smaller is the Hamming Distance of the calculated simhashes of two strings, the more likely these strings are the same.<sep>[SYNTAX]: ngramSimHash(string[, ngramsize])<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - string вҖ” String. String .
- ngramsize вҖ” The size of an n-gram. Optional. Possible values: any number from 1 to 25 . Default value: 3 . UInt8 .
Returned value
- Hash value. UInt64 .<sep>[RETURN]: - Hash value. UInt64 .<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT ngramSimHash('ClickHouse') AS Hash; ; в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҖHashв”Җв”җ

description:
Splits a ASCII string into n-grams of ngramsize symbols and calculates hash values for each n-gram. Uses hashnum minimum hashes to calculate the minimum hash and hashnum maximum hashes to calculate the maximum hash. Returns a tuple with these hashes. Is case sensitive.
Can be used for detection of semi-duplicate strings with tupleHammingDistance . For two strings: if one of the returned hashes is the same for both strings, we think that those strings are the same.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Splits a ASCII string into n-grams of ngramsize symbols and calculates hash values for each n-gram. Uses hashnum minimum hashes to calculate the minimum hash and hashnum maximum hashes to calculate the maximum hash. Returns a tuple with these hashes. Is case sensitive.
Can be used for detection of semi-duplicate strings with tupleHammingDistance . For two strings: if one of the returned hashes is the same for both strings, we think that those strings are the same.<sep>[SYNTAX]: ngramMinHash(string[, ngramsize, hashnum])<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - string вҖ” String. String .
- ngramsize вҖ” The size of an n-gram. Optional. Possible values: any number from 1 to 25 . Default value: 3 . UInt8 .
- hashnum вҖ” The number of minimum and maximum hashes used to calculate the result. Optional. Possible values: any number from 1 to 25 . Default value: 6 . UInt8 .
Returned value
- Tuple with two hashes вҖ” the minimum and the maximum. Tuple ( UInt64 , UInt64 ).<sep>[RETURN]: - Tuple with two hashes вҖ” the minimum and the maximum. Tuple ( UInt64 , UInt64 ).<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT ngramMinHash('ClickHouse') AS Tuple; ; в”Ңв”ҖTupleв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ

description:
Splits a ASCII string into parts (shingles) of shinglesize words and calculates hash values for each word shingle. Uses hashnum minimum hashes to calculate the minimum hash and hashnum maximum hashes to calculate the maximum hash. Returns a tuple with these hashes. Is case sensitive.
Can be used for detection of semi-duplicate strings with tupleHammingDistance . For two strings: if one of the returned hashes is the same for both strings, we think that those strings are the same.

Tuple ( Tuple ( String ), Tuple ( String )).<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT wordShingleMinHashArgCaseInsensitiveUTF8('ClickHouseВ® is a column-oriented database management system (DBMS) for online analytical processing of queries (OLAP).', 1, 3) AS Tuple; ; в”Ңв”ҖTupleв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ

description: Encodes numbers as a Sqid which is a YouTube-like ID string. The output alphabet is abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyzABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZ0123456789 . Do not use this function for hashing - the generated IDs can be decoded back into the original numbers.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Encodes numbers as a Sqid which is a YouTube-like ID string. The output alphabet is abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyzABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZ0123456789 . Do not use this function for hashing - the generated IDs can be decoded back into the original numbers.<sep>[SYNTAX]: sqidEncode(number1, ...)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - A variable number of UInt8, UInt16, UInt32 or UInt64 numbers.
Returned Value
A sqid String .<sep>[RETURN]: A sqid String .<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT sqidEncode(1, 2, 3, 4, 5); ; в”Ңв”ҖsqidEncode(1, 2, 3, 4, 5)в”Җв”җ

description:
Converts virtual memory address inside ClickHouse server process to the filename and the line number in ClickHouse source code.
If you use official ClickHouse packages, you need to install the clickhouse-common-static-dbg package.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Converts virtual memory address inside ClickHouse server process to the filename and the line number in ClickHouse source code.
If you use official ClickHouse packages, you need to install the clickhouse-common-static-dbg package.<sep>[SYNTAX]: addressToLine(address_of_binary_instruction)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - address_of_binary_instruction ( UInt64 ) вҖ” Address of instruction in a running process.
Returned value
- Source code filename and the line number in this file delimited by colon. For example, /build/obj-x86_64-linux-gnu/../src/Common/ThreadPool.cpp:199 , where 199 is a line number.
- Name of a binary, if the function couldn't find the debug information.
- Empty string, if the address is not valid.
Type: String .<sep>[RETURN]: - Source code filename and the line number in this file delimited by colon. For example, /build/obj-x86_64-linux-gnu/../src/Common/ThreadPool.cpp:199 , where 199 is a line number.
- Name of a binary, if the function couldn't find the debug information.
- Empty string, if the address is not valid.
Type: String .<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT * FROM system.trace_log LIMIT 1 \G; ; Row 1: ; SELECT addressToLine(94784076370703) \G;
example:
SELECT
    arrayStringConcat(arrayMap(x -> addressToLine(x), trace), '\n') AS trace_source_code_lines
FROM system.trace_log
LIMIT 1
\G

description: Similar to addressToLine , but returns an Array with all inline functions. As a result of this, it is slower than addressToLine .
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Similar to addressToLine , but returns an Array with all inline functions. As a result of this, it is slower than addressToLine .<sep>[SYNTAX]: addressToLineWithInlines(address_of_binary_instruction)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - address_of_binary_instruction ( UInt64 ) вҖ” Address of instruction in a running process.
Returned value
- An array whose first element is the source code filename and line number in the file delimited by a colon. From the second element onwards, inline functions' source code filenames, line numbers and function names are listed. If the function couldn't find the debug information, then an array with a single element equal to the name of the binary is returned, otherwise an empty array is returned if the address is not valid. Array(String) .<sep>[RETURN]: - An array whose first element is the source code filename and line number in the file delimited by a colon. From the second element onwards, inline functions' source code filenames, line numbers and function names are listed. If the function couldn't find the debug information, then an array with a single element equal to the name of the binary is returned, otherwise an empty array is returned if the address is not valid. Array(String) .<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT addressToLineWithInlines(531055181::UInt64); ; в”Ңв”ҖaddressToLineWithInlines(CAST('531055181', 'UInt64'))в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ ; SELECT
example:
SELECT
    ta, addressToLineWithInlines(arrayJoin(trace) AS ta)
FROM system.trace_log
WHERE
    query_id = '5e173544-2020-45de-b645-5deebe2aae54';

- covering_part ( String ) вҖ” Name of expected covering part.
Returned value
- 1 if it covers, 0 otherwise<sep>[RETURN]: - 1 if it covers, 0 otherwise<sep>[DEMO]: WITH 'all_12_25_7_4' AS lhs, 'all_7_100_10_20' AS rhs SELECT isMergeTreePartCoveredBy(rhs, lhs), isMergeTreePartCoveredBy(lhs, rhs); ; в”Ңв”ҖisMergeTreePвӢҜy(rhs, lhs)в”Җв”¬в”ҖisMergeTreePвӢҜy(lhs, rhs)в”Җв”җ

description:
Accepts a FixedString(16) value containing the IPv6 address in binary format. Returns a string containing this address in text format. IPv6-mapped IPv4 addresses are output in the format ::ffff:111.222.33.44.
Alias: INET6_NTOA .
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Accepts a FixedString(16) value containing the IPv6 address in binary format. Returns a string containing this address in text format. IPv6-mapped IPv4 addresses are output in the format ::ffff:111.222.33.44.
Alias: INET6_NTOA .<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT IPv6NumToString(toFixedString(unhex('2A0206B8000000000000000000000011'), 16)) AS addr; ; в”Ңв”Җaddrв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ ; SELECT
example:
SELECT
    IPv6NumToString(ClientIP6 AS k),
    count() AS c
FROM hits_all
WHERE EventDate = today() AND substring(ClientIP6, 1, 12) != unhex('00000000000000000000FFFF')
GROUP BY k
ORDER BY c DESC
LIMIT 10

в”Ңв”ҖIPv6NumToString(ClientIP6)в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җcв”Җв”җ

в”Ӯ 2a02:810b:8888:888:8888:8888:8888:8888  в”Ӯ 14345 в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ 2a02:6b8:0:444:4444:4444:4444:4444      в”Ӯ 14279 в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ 2a01:7e00::ffff:ffff:ffff:ffff          в”Ӯ 13880 в”Ӯ
в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ

description:
The reverse function of IPv6NumToString . If the IPv6 address has an invalid format, it throws exception.
If the input string contains a valid IPv4 address, returns its IPv6 equivalent. HEX can be uppercase or lowercase.
Alias: INET6_ATON .
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: The reverse function of IPv6NumToString . If the IPv6 address has an invalid format, it throws exception.
If the input string contains a valid IPv4 address, returns its IPv6 equivalent. HEX can be uppercase or lowercase.
Alias: INET6_ATON .<sep>[SYNTAX]: IPv6StringToNum(string)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - string вҖ” IP address. String .
Returned value
- IPv6 address in binary format. FixedString(16) .<sep>[RETURN]: - IPv6 address in binary format. FixedString(16) .<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT addr, cutIPv6(IPv6StringToNum(addr), 0, 0) FROM (SELECT ['notaddress', '127.0.0.1', '1111::ffff'] AS addr) ARRAY JOIN addr; ; в”Ңв”Җaddrв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”ҖcutIPv6(IPv6StringToNum(addr), 0, 0)в”Җв”җ
example: SELECT addr, cutIPv6(IPv6StringToNum(addr), 0, 0) FROM (SELECT ['notaddress', '127.0.0.1', '1111::ffff'] AS addr) ARRAY JOIN addr;

в”Ңв”Җaddrв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”ҖcutIPv6(IPv6StringToNum(addr), 0, 0)в”Җв”җ
в”Ӯ notaddress в”Ӯ ::                                   в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ 127.0.0.1  в”Ӯ ::ffff:127.0.0.1                     в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ 1111::ffff в”Ӯ 1111::ffff                           в”Ӯ
в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ

For example:<sep>[DEMO]: WITH ; в”Ңв”ҖcutIPv6(ipv6, 2, 0)в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”ҖcutIPv6(ipv4, 0, 2)в”Җв”җ

SELECT
