description: Converts a string or a UInt32 form of IPv4 address to IPv4 type. Similar to IPv4StringToNum and IPv4NumToString functions but it supports both string and unsigned integer data types as input arguments.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Converts a string or a UInt32 form of IPv4 address to IPv4 type. Similar to IPv4StringToNum and IPv4NumToString functions but it supports both string and unsigned integer data types as input arguments.<sep>[SYNTAX]: toIPv4(x)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - x вҖ” IPv4 address. String , UInt8/16/32 .
Returned value
- IPv4 address. IPv4 .<sep>[RETURN]: - IPv4 address. IPv4 .<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT toIPv4('171.225.130.45'); ; в”Ңв”ҖtoIPv4('171.225.130.45')в”Җв”җ ; WITH
example:
WITH
    '171.225.130.45' AS IPv4_string
SELECT
    hex(IPv4StringToNum(IPv4_string)),
    hex(toIPv4(IPv4_string))

description: Same as toIPv4 , but if the IPv4 address has an invalid format, it returns 0.0.0.0 (0 IPv4), or the provided IPv4 default.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Same as toIPv4 , but if the IPv4 address has an invalid format, it returns 0.0.0.0 (0 IPv4), or the provided IPv4 default.<sep>[SYNTAX]: toIPv4OrDefault(string[, default])<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - value вҖ” IP address. String .
- default (optional) вҖ” The value to return if string has an invalid format. IPv4 .
Returned value
- string converted to the current IPv4 address. String .<sep>[RETURN]: - string converted to the current IPv4 address. String .<sep>[DEMO]: WITH ; в”Ңв”Җvalidв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җdefaultв”Җв”¬в”Җprovided_defaultв”Җв”җ
example:
WITH
    '::ffff:127.0.0.1' AS valid_IPv6_string,
    'fe80:2030:31:24' AS invalid_IPv6_string
SELECT
    toIPv4OrDefault(valid_IPv6_string) AS valid,
    toIPv4OrDefault(invalid_IPv6_string) AS default,
    toIPv4OrDefault(invalid_IPv6_string, toIPv4('1.1.1.1')) AS provided_default;

description: Same as toIPv4 , but if the IPv4 address has an invalid format, it returns null.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Same as toIPv4 , but if the IPv4 address has an invalid format, it returns null.<sep>[SYNTAX]: toIPv4OrNull(string)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - string вҖ” IP address. String .

- string converted to the current IPv4 address, or null if string is an invalid address. String .<sep>[RETURN]: - string converted to the current IPv4 address, or null if string is an invalid address. String .<sep>[DEMO]: WITH 'fe80:2030:31:24' AS invalid_IPv6_string ; в”Ңв”ҖtoIPv4OrNull(invalid_IPv6_string)в”Җв”җ

description: Same as toIPv4 , but if the IPv4 address has an invalid format, it returns 0.0.0.0 .
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Same as toIPv4 , but if the IPv4 address has an invalid format, it returns 0.0.0.0 .<sep>[SYNTAX]: toIPv4OrZero(string)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - string вҖ” IP address. String .
Returned value
- string converted to the current IPv4 address, or 0.0.0.0 if string is an invalid address. String .<sep>[RETURN]: - string converted to the current IPv4 address, or 0.0.0.0 if string is an invalid address. String .<sep>[DEMO]: WITH 'Not an IP address' AS invalid_IPv6_string ; в”Ңв”ҖtoIPv4OrZero(invalid_IPv6_string)в”Җв”җ
example:
WITH 'Not an IP address' AS invalid_IPv6_string
SELECT toIPv4OrZero(invalid_IPv6_string);

description:
Converts a string or a UInt128 form of IPv6 address to IPv6 type. For strings, if the IPv6 address has an invalid format, returns an empty value. Similar to IPv6StringToNum and IPv6NumToString functions, which convert IPv6 address to and from binary format (i.e. FixedString(16) ).
If the input string contains a valid IPv4 address, then the IPv6 equivalent of the IPv4 address is returned.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Converts a string or a UInt128 form of IPv6 address to IPv6 type. For strings, if the IPv6 address has an invalid format, returns an empty value. Similar to IPv6StringToNum and IPv6NumToString functions, which convert IPv6 address to and from binary format (i.e. FixedString(16) ).
If the input string contains a valid IPv4 address, then the IPv6 equivalent of the IPv4 address is returned.<sep>[SYNTAX]: toIPv6(string)
toIPv6(UInt128)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - x вҖ” IP address. String or UInt128 .
Returned value
- IP address. IPv6 .<sep>[RETURN]: - IP address. IPv6 .<sep>[DEMO]: WITH '2001:438:ffff::407d:1bc1' AS IPv6_string ; в”Ңв”Җhex(IPv6StringToNum(IPv6_string))в”Җв”¬в”Җhex(toIPv6(IPv6_string))в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ ; SELECT toIPv6('127.0.0.1');
example:
WITH '2001:438:ffff::407d:1bc1' AS IPv6_string
SELECT
    hex(IPv6StringToNum(IPv6_string)),
    hex(toIPv6(IPv6_string));

description: Same as toIPv6 , but if the IPv6 address has an invalid format, it returns :: (0 IPv6) or the provided IPv6 default.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Same as toIPv6 , but if the IPv6 address has an invalid format, it returns :: (0 IPv6) or the provided IPv6 default.<sep>[SYNTAX]: toIPv6OrDefault(string[, default])<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - string вҖ” IP address. String .
- default (optional) вҖ” The value to return if string has an invalid format. IPv6 .
Returned value
- IPv6 address IPv6 , otherwise :: or the provided optional default if string has an invalid format.<sep>[RETURN]: - IPv6 address IPv6 , otherwise :: or the provided optional default if string has an invalid format.<sep>[DEMO]: WITH ; в”Ңв”Җvalidв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җdefaultв”Җв”¬в”Җprovided_defaultв”Җв”җ
example:
WITH
    '127.0.0.1' AS valid_IPv4_string,
    '127.0.0.1.6' AS invalid_IPv4_string
SELECT
    toIPv6OrDefault(valid_IPv4_string) AS valid,
    toIPv6OrDefault(invalid_IPv4_string) AS default,
    toIPv6OrDefault(invalid_IPv4_string, toIPv6('1.1.1.1')) AS provided_default

description: Determines whether the input string is an IPv4 address or not. If string is IPv6 address returns 0 .
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Determines whether the input string is an IPv4 address or not. If string is IPv6 address returns 0 .<sep>[SYNTAX]: isIPv4String(string)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - string вҖ” IP address. String .
Returned value
- 1 if string is IPv4 address, 0 otherwise. UInt8 .<sep>[RETURN]: - 1 if string is IPv4 address, 0 otherwise. UInt8 .<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT addr, isIPv4String(addr) FROM ( SELECT ['0.0.0.0', '127.0.0.1', '::ffff:127.0.0.1'] AS addr ) ARRAY JOIN addr; ; в”Ңв”Җaddrв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”ҖisIPv4String(addr)в”Җв”җ

description: Parses a true/false value from the value of the field named field_name . The result is UInt8 .
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Parses a true/false value from the value of the field named field_name . The result is UInt8 .<sep>[SYNTAX]: simpleJSONExtractBool(json, field_name)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - json вҖ” The JSON in which the field is searched for. String
- field_name вҖ” The name of the field to search for. String literal
Returned value
It returns 1 if the value of the field is true , 0 otherwise. This means this function will return 0 including (and not only) in the following cases:
- If the field doesn't exists.
- If the field contains true as a string, e.g.: {"field":"true"} .
- If the field contains 1 as a numerical value.<sep>[RETURN]: It returns 1 if the value of the field is true , 0 otherwise. This means this function will return 0 including (and not only) in the following cases:
- If the field doesn't exists.
- If the field contains true as a string, e.g.: {"field":"true"} .
- If the field contains 1 as a numerical value.<sep>[DEMO]: CREATE TABLE jsons ; 0

- field_name вҖ” The name of the field to search for. String literal
Returned value
- Returns the value of the field as a string, including separators if the field exists, or an empty string otherwise. String<sep>[RETURN]: - Returns the value of the field as a string, including separators if the field exists, or an empty string otherwise. String<sep>[DEMO]: CREATE TABLE jsons ; "-4e3"

description: Parses String in double quotes from the value of the field named field_name .
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Parses String in double quotes from the value of the field named field_name .<sep>[SYNTAX]: simpleJSONExtractString(json, field_name)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - json вҖ” The JSON in which the field is searched for. String
- field_name вҖ” The name of the field to search for. String literal
Returned value
- Returns the unescaped value of a field as a string, including separators. An empty string is returned if the field doesn't contain a double quoted string, if unescaping fails or if the field doesn't exist. String .
Implementation details
There is currently no support for code points in the format \uXXXX\uYYYY that are not from the basic multilingual plane (they are converted to CESU-8 instead of UTF-8).<sep>[RETURN]: - Returns the unescaped value of a field as a string, including separators. An empty string is returned if the field doesn't contain a double quoted string, if unescaping fails or if the field doesn't exist. String .
Implementation details
There is currently no support for code points in the format \uXXXX\uYYYY that are not from the basic multilingual plane (they are converted to CESU-8 instead of UTF-8).<sep>[DEMO]: CREATE TABLE jsons ; \n\0

description: If the value exists in the JSON document, 1 will be returned. If the value does not exist, 0 will be returned.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: If the value exists in the JSON document, 1 will be returned. If the value does not exist, 0 will be returned.<sep>[SYNTAX]: JSONHas(json [, indices_or_keys]...)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - json вҖ” JSON string to parse. String .
- indices_or_keys вҖ” A list of zero or more arguments, each of which can be either string or integer. String , Int* .
indices_or_keys type:
- String = access object member by key.
- Positive integer = access the n-th member/key from the beginning.
- Negative integer = access the n-th member/key from the end.
Returned value
- Returns 1 if the value exists in json , otherwise 0 . UInt8 .<sep>[RETURN]: - Returns 1 if the value exists in json , otherwise 0 . UInt8 .<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT JSONHas('{"a": "hello", "b": [-100, 200.0, 300]}', 'b') = 1

- indices_or_keys вҖ” A list of zero or more arguments, each of which can be either string or integer. String , Int* .
indices_or_keys type:

- Positive integer = access the n-th member/key from the beginning.
- Negative integer = access the n-th member/key from the end.
Returned value
- Returns the length of the JSON array or JSON object. Returns 0 if the value does not exist or has the wrong type. UInt64 .<sep>[RETURN]: - Returns the length of the JSON array or JSON object. Returns 0 if the value does not exist or has the wrong type. UInt64 .<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT JSONLength('{"a": "hello", "b": [-100, 200.0, 300]}', 'b') = 3

description: Return the type of a JSON value. If the value does not exist, Null=0 will be returned (not usual Null , but Null=0 of Enum8('Null' = 0, 'String' = 34,... ). .
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Return the type of a JSON value. If the value does not exist, Null=0 will be returned (not usual Null , but Null=0 of Enum8('Null' = 0, 'String' = 34,... ). .<sep>[SYNTAX]: JSONType(json [, indices_or_keys]...)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - json вҖ” JSON string to parse. String .
- indices_or_keys вҖ” A list of zero or more arguments, each of which can be either string or integer. String , Int* .
indices_or_keys type:
- String = access object member by key.
- Positive integer = access the n-th member/key from the beginning.
- Negative integer = access the n-th member/key from the end.

Enum .<sep>[RETURN]: - Returns the type of a JSON value as a string, otherwise if the value doesn't exists it returns Null=0 . example:

SELECT JSONType('{"a": "hello", "b": [-100, 200.0, 300]}', 'a') = 'String'
SELECT JSONType('{"a": "hello", "b": [-100, 200.0, 300]}', 'b') = 'Array'

description: Parses JSON and extracts a string. This function is similar to visitParamExtractString functions. If the value does not exist or has a wrong type, an empty string will be returned.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Parses JSON and extracts a string. This function is similar to visitParamExtractString functions. If the value does not exist or has a wrong type, an empty string will be returned.<sep>[SYNTAX]: JSONExtractString(json [, indices_or_keys]...)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - json вҖ” JSON string to parse. String .
- indices_or_keys вҖ” A list of zero or more arguments, each of which can be either string or integer. String , Int* .
indices_or_keys type:
- String = access object member by key.
- Positive integer = access the n-th member/key from the beginning.
- Negative integer = access the n-th member/key from the end.
Returned value
- Returns an unescaped string from json . If unescaping failed, if the value does not exist or if it has a wrong type then it returns an empty string. String .<sep>[RETURN]: - Returns an unescaped string from json . If unescaping failed, if the value does not exist or if it has a wrong type then it returns an empty string. String .<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT JSONExtractString('{"a": "hello", "b": [-100, 200.0, 300]}', 'a') = 'hello'
example:
SELECT JSONExtractString('{"a": "hello", "b": [-100, 200.0, 300]}', 'a') = 'hello'
SELECT JSONExtractString('{"abc":"\\n\\u0000"}', 'abc') = '\n\0'
SELECT JSONExtractString('{"abc":"\\u263a"}', 'abc') = 'вҳә'
SELECT JSONExtractString('{"abc":"\\u263"}', 'abc') = ''
SELECT JSONExtractString('{"abc":"hello}', 'abc') = ''

description:
Parses JSON and extracts a value of the given ClickHouse data type. This function is a generalized version of the previous JSONExtract<type> functions. Meaning:
JSONExtract(..., 'String') returns exactly the same as JSONExtractString() , JSONExtract(..., 'Float64') returns exactly the same as JSONExtractFloat() .
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Parses JSON and extracts a value of the given ClickHouse data type. This function is a generalized version of the previous JSONExtract<type> functions. Meaning:
JSONExtract(..., 'String') returns exactly the same as JSONExtractString() , JSONExtract(..., 'Float64') returns exactly the same as JSONExtractFloat() .<sep>[SYNTAX]: JSONExtract(json [, indices_or_keys...], return_type)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - json вҖ” JSON string to parse. String .
- indices_or_keys вҖ” A list of zero or more arguments, each of which can be either string or integer. String , Int* .
- return_type вҖ” A string specifying the type of the value to extract. String .
indices_or_keys type:
- String = access object member by key.
- Positive integer = access the n-th member/key from the beginning.
- Negative integer = access the n-th member/key from the end.
Returned value
- Returns a value if it exists of the specified return type, otherwise it returns 0 , Null , or an empty-string depending on the specified return type. UInt64 , Int64 , Float64 , Bool or String .<sep>[RETURN]: - Returns a value if it exists of the specified return type, otherwise it returns 0 , Null , or an empty-string depending on the specified return type. UInt64 , Int64 , Float64 , Bool or String .<sep>[DEMO]: в”Ңв”Җvalв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”ҖtoTypeName(val)в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”ҖarrayElement(val, 'd')в”Җв”җ

в”Ңв”Җvalв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”ҖtoTypeName(val)в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”ҖarrayElement(val, 'd')в”Җв”җ

description: Parses key-value pairs from JSON where the values are of the given ClickHouse data type.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Parses key-value pairs from JSON where the values are of the given ClickHouse data type.<sep>[SYNTAX]: JSONExtractKeysAndValues(json [, indices_or_keys...], value_type)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - json вҖ” JSON string to parse. String .
- indices_or_keys вҖ” A list of zero or more arguments, each of which can be either string or integer. String , Int* .
- value_type вҖ” A string specifying the type of the value to extract. String .
indices_or_keys type:
- String = access object member by key.
- Positive integer = access the n-th member/key from the beginning.
- Negative integer = access the n-th member/key from the end.
Returned value
- Returns an array of parsed key-value pairs. Array ( Tuple ( value_type )).<sep>[RETURN]: - Returns an array of parsed key-value pairs. Array ( Tuple ( value_type )).<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT JSONExtractKeysAndValues('{"x": {"a": 5, "b": 7, "c": 11}}', 'x', 'Int8') = [('a',5),('b',7),('c',11)];

description: Parses a JSON string and extracts the keys.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Parses a JSON string and extracts the keys.<sep>[SYNTAX]: JSONExtractKeys(json[, a, b, c...])<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - json вҖ” String with valid JSON.
- a, b, c... вҖ” Comma-separated indices or keys that specify the path to the inner field in a nested JSON object. Each argument can be either a String to get the field by the key or an Integer to get the N-th field (indexed from 1, negative integers count from the end). If not set, the whole JSON is parsed as the top-level object. Optional parameter.
Returned value
- Returns an array with the keys of the JSON. Array ( String ).<sep>[RETURN]: - Returns an array with the keys of the JSON. Array ( String ).<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT JSONExtractKeys('{"a": "hello", "b": [-100, 200.0, 300]}'); ; в”Ңв”ҖJSONExtractKeys('{"a": "hello", "b": [-100, 200.0, 300]}')в”Җв”җ

description: Returns part of the JSON as an unparsed string. If the part does not exist or has the wrong type, an empty string will be returned.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Returns part of the JSON as an unparsed string. If the part does not exist or has the wrong type, an empty string will be returned.<sep>[SYNTAX]: JSONExtractRaw(json [, indices_or_keys]...)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - json вҖ” JSON string to parse. String .
- indices_or_keys вҖ” A list of zero or more arguments, each of which can be either string or integer. String , Int* .
indices_or_keys type:
- String = access object member by key.
- Positive integer = access the n-th member/key from the beginning.
- Negative integer = access the n-th member/key from the end.
Returned value
- Returns part of the JSON as an unparsed string. If the part does not exist or has the wrong type, an empty string is returned. String .<sep>[RETURN]: - Returns part of the JSON as an unparsed string. If the part does not exist or has the wrong type, an empty string is returned. String .<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT JSONExtractRaw('{"a": "hello", "b": [-100, 200.0, 300]}', 'b') = '[-100, 200.0, 300]';

description: The following functions perform ASCII case-insensitive key matching when extracting values from JSON objects. They work identically to their case-sensitive counterparts, except that object keys are matched without regard to case. When multiple keys match with different cases, the first match is returned.
detail: [DESCRIPTION]: The following functions perform ASCII case-insensitive key matching when extracting values from JSON objects. They work identically to their case-sensitive counterparts, except that object keys are matched without regard to case. When multiple keys match with different cases, the first match is returned.

description: Parses JSON and extracts a value of Int type using case-insensitive key matching. This function is similar to JSONExtractInt .
detail: [DESCRIPTION]: Parses JSON and extracts a value of Int type using case-insensitive key matching. This function is similar to JSONExtractInt .<sep>[SYNTAX]: JSONExtractIntCaseInsensitive(json [, indices_or_keys]...)<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT JSONExtractIntCaseInsensitive('{"Value": 123}', 'value') = 123;

description: Parses JSON and extracts a value of UInt type using case-insensitive key matching. This function is similar to JSONExtractUInt .
detail: [DESCRIPTION]: Parses JSON and extracts a value of UInt type using case-insensitive key matching. This function is similar to JSONExtractUInt .<sep>[SYNTAX]: JSONExtractUIntCaseInsensitive(json [, indices_or_keys]...)<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT JSONExtractUIntCaseInsensitive('{"COUNT": 789}', 'count') = 789;

description: Parses JSON and extracts a value of Float type using case-insensitive key matching. This function is similar to JSONExtractFloat .
detail: [DESCRIPTION]: Parses JSON and extracts a value of Float type using case-insensitive key matching. This function is similar to JSONExtractFloat .<sep>[SYNTAX]: JSONExtractFloatCaseInsensitive(json [, indices_or_keys]...)<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT JSONExtractFloatCaseInsensitive('{"Price": 12.34}', 'PRICE') = 12.34;

description: Parses JSON and extracts a boolean value using case-insensitive key matching. This function is similar to JSONExtractBool .
detail: [DESCRIPTION]: Parses JSON and extracts a boolean value using case-insensitive key matching. This function is similar to JSONExtractBool .<sep>[SYNTAX]: JSONExtractBoolCaseInsensitive(json [, indices_or_keys]...)<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT JSONExtractBoolCaseInsensitive('{"IsActive": true}', 'isactive') = 1;

description: Parses JSON and extracts a string using case-insensitive key matching. This function is similar to JSONExtractString .
detail: [DESCRIPTION]: Parses JSON and extracts a string using case-insensitive key matching. This function is similar to JSONExtractString .<sep>[SYNTAX]: JSONExtractStringCaseInsensitive(json [, indices_or_keys]...)<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT JSONExtractStringCaseInsensitive('{"ABC": "def"}', 'abc') = 'def';

description: Parses JSON and extracts a value of the given ClickHouse data type using case-insensitive key matching. This function is similar to JSONExtract .
detail: [DESCRIPTION]: Parses JSON and extracts a value of the given ClickHouse data type using case-insensitive key matching. This function is similar to JSONExtract .<sep>[SYNTAX]: JSONExtractCaseInsensitive(json [, indices_or_keys...], return_type)<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT JSONExtractCaseInsensitive('{"Number": 123}', 'number', 'Int32') = 123;

description: Parses key-value pairs from JSON using case-insensitive key matching. This function is similar to JSONExtractKeysAndValues .
detail: [DESCRIPTION]: Parses key-value pairs from JSON using case-insensitive key matching. This function is similar to JSONExtractKeysAndValues .<sep>[SYNTAX]: JSONExtractKeysAndValuesCaseInsensitive(json [, indices_or_keys...], value_type)<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT JSONExtractKeysAndValuesCaseInsensitive('{"Name": "Alice", "AGE": 30}', 'String')[1] = ('Name', 'Alice');

description: Returns part of the JSON as an unparsed string using case-insensitive key matching. This function is similar to JSONExtractRaw .
detail: [DESCRIPTION]: Returns part of the JSON as an unparsed string using case-insensitive key matching. This function is similar to JSONExtractRaw .<sep>[SYNTAX]: JSONExtractRawCaseInsensitive(json [, indices_or_keys]...)<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT JSONExtractRawCaseInsensitive('{"Object": {"key": "value"}}', 'OBJECT') = '{"key":"value"}';

description: Returns an array with elements of JSON array, each represented as unparsed string, using case-insensitive key matching. This function is similar to JSONExtractArrayRaw .
detail: [DESCRIPTION]: Returns an array with elements of JSON array, each represented as unparsed string, using case-insensitive key matching. This function is similar to JSONExtractArrayRaw .<sep>[SYNTAX]: JSONExtractArrayRawCaseInsensitive(json [, indices_or_keys]...)<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT JSONExtractArrayRawCaseInsensitive('{"Items": [1, 2, 3]}', 'ITEMS') = ['1', '2', '3'];

description: Extracts raw key-value pairs from JSON using case-insensitive key matching. This function is similar to JSONExtractKeysAndValuesRaw .
detail: [DESCRIPTION]: Extracts raw key-value pairs from JSON using case-insensitive key matching. This function is similar to JSONExtractKeysAndValuesRaw .<sep>[SYNTAX]: JSONExtractKeysAndValuesRawCaseInsensitive(json [, indices_or_keys]...)<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT JSONExtractKeysAndValuesRawCaseInsensitive('{"Name": "Alice", "AGE": 30}')[1] = ('Name', '"Alice"');

description: Parses a JSON string and extracts the keys using case-insensitive key matching to navigate to nested objects. This function is similar to JSONExtractKeys .
detail: [DESCRIPTION]: Parses a JSON string and extracts the keys using case-insensitive key matching to navigate to nested objects. This function is similar to JSONExtractKeys .<sep>[SYNTAX]: JSONExtractKeysCaseInsensitive(json [, indices_or_keys]...)<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT JSONExtractKeysCaseInsensitive('{"Name": "Alice", "AGE": 30}') = ['Name', 'AGE'];

description: Returns an array with elements of JSON array, each represented as unparsed string. If the part does not exist or isn't an array, then an empty array will be returned.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Returns an array with elements of JSON array, each represented as unparsed string. If the part does not exist or isn't an array, then an empty array will be returned.<sep>[SYNTAX]: JSONExtractArrayRaw(json [, indices_or_keys...])<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - json вҖ” JSON string to parse. String .
- indices_or_keys вҖ” A list of zero or more arguments, each of which can be either string or integer. String , Int* .
indices_or_keys type:
- String = access object member by key.
- Positive integer = access the n-th member/key from the beginning.
- Negative integer = access the n-th member/key from the end.
Returned value
- Returns an array with elements of JSON array, each represented as unparsed string. Otherwise, an empty array is returned if the part does not exist or is not an array. Array ( String ).<sep>[RETURN]: - Returns an array with elements of JSON array, each represented as unparsed string. Otherwise, an empty array is returned if the part does not exist or is not an array. Array ( String ).<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT JSONExtractArrayRaw('{"a": "hello", "b": [-100, 200.0, "hello"]}', 'b') = ['-100', '200.0', '"hello"'];

description: Extracts raw data from a JSON object.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Extracts raw data from a JSON object.<sep>[SYNTAX]: JSONExtractKeysAndValuesRaw(json[, p, a, t, h])<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - json вҖ” String with valid JSON.
- p, a, t, h вҖ” Comma-separated indices or keys that specify the path to the inner field in a nested JSON object. Each argument can be either a string to get the field by the key or an integer to get the N-th field (indexed from 1, negative integers count from the end). If not set, the whole JSON is parsed as the top-level object. Optional parameter.
Returned values
- Array with ('key', 'value') tuples. Both tuple members are strings. Array ( Tuple ( String , String ).
- Empty array if the requested object does not exist, or input JSON is invalid. Array ( Tuple ( String , String ).<sep>[RETURN]: - Array with ('key', 'value') tuples. Both tuple members are strings. Array ( Tuple ( String , String ).
- Empty array if the requested object does not exist, or input JSON is invalid. Array ( Tuple ( String , String ).<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT JSONExtractKeysAndValuesRaw('{"a": [-100, 200.0], "b":{"c": {"d": "hello", "f": "world"}}}'); ; в”Ңв”ҖJSONExtractKeysAndValuesRaw('{"a": [-100, 200.0], "b":{"c": {"d": "hello", "f": "world"}}}')в”Җв”җ ; SELECT JSONExtractKeysAndValuesRaw('{"a": [-100, 200.0], "b":{"c": {"d": "hello", "f": "world"}}}', 'b');

description: If the value exists in the JSON document, 1 will be returned. If the value does not exist, 0 will be returned.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: If the value exists in the JSON document, 1 will be returned. If the value does not exist, 0 will be returned.<sep>[SYNTAX]: JSON_EXISTS(json, path)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - json вҖ” A string with valid JSON. String .
- path вҖ” A string representing the path. String .
Returned value
- Returns 1 if the value exists in the JSON document, otherwise 0 .<sep>[RETURN]: - Returns 1 if the value exists in the JSON document, otherwise 0 .<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT JSON_EXISTS('{"hello":1}', '$.hello');

description: Parses a JSON and extract a value as a JSON array or JSON object. If the value does not exist, an empty string will be returned.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Parses a JSON and extract a value as a JSON array or JSON object. If the value does not exist, an empty string will be returned.<sep>[SYNTAX]: JSON_QUERY(json, path)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - json вҖ” A string with valid JSON. String .
- path вҖ” A string representing the path. String .
Returned value
- Returns the extracted value as a JSON array or JSON object. Otherwise it returns an empty string if the value does not exist. String .<sep>[RETURN]: - Returns the extracted value as a JSON array or JSON object. Otherwise it returns an empty string if the value does not exist. String .<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT JSON_QUERY('{"hello":"world"}', '$.hello'); ; ["world"]

description:
Parses a JSON and extract a value as a JSON scalar. If the value does not exist, an empty string will be returned by default.
This function is controlled by the following settings:
- by SET function_json_value_return_type_allow_nullable = true , NULL will be returned. If the value is complex type (such as: struct, array, map), an empty string will be returned by default.
- by SET function_json_value_return_type_allow_complex = true , the complex value will be returned.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Parses a JSON and extract a value as a JSON scalar. If the value does not exist, an empty string will be returned by default.
This function is controlled by the following settings:
- by SET function_json_value_return_type_allow_nullable = true , NULL will be returned. If the value is complex type (such as: struct, array, map), an empty string will be returned by default.
- by SET function_json_value_return_type_allow_complex = true , the complex value will be returned.<sep>[SYNTAX]: JSON_VALUE(json, path)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - json вҖ” A string with valid JSON. String .
- path вҖ” A string representing the path. String .
Returned value
- Returns the extracted value as a JSON scalar if it exists, otherwise an empty string is returned. String .<sep>[RETURN]: - Returns the extracted value as a JSON scalar if it exists, otherwise an empty string is returned. String .<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT JSON_VALUE('{"hello":"world"}', '$.hello'); ; world

description: Serializes a value to its JSON representation. Various data types and nested structures are supported. 64-bit integers or bigger (like UInt64 or Int128 ) are enclosed in quotes by default. output_format_json_quote_64bit_integers controls this behavior. Special values NaN and inf are replaced with null . Enable output_format_json_quote_denormals setting to show them. When serializing an Enum value, the function outputs its name.
example:
SELECT toJSONString(map('key1', 1, 'key2', 2));
SELECT toJSONString(tuple(1.25, NULL, NaN, +inf, -inf, [])) SETTINGS output_format_json_quote_denormals = 1;

description: Returns the number of elements in the outermost JSON array. The function returns NULL if input JSON string is invalid.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Returns the number of elements in the outermost JSON array. The function returns NULL if input JSON string is invalid.<sep>[SYNTAX]: JSONArrayLength(json)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - json вҖ” String with valid JSON.
Returned value
- If json is a valid JSON array string, returns the number of array elements, otherwise returns NULL. Nullable(UInt64) .<sep>[RETURN]: - If json is a valid JSON array string, returns the number of array elements, otherwise returns NULL. Nullable(UInt64) .<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT
example:
SELECT
    JSONArrayLength(''),
    JSONArrayLength('[1,2,3]')

в”Ңв”ҖJSONArrayLength('')в”Җв”¬в”ҖJSONArrayLength('[1,2,3]')в”Җв”җ
в”Ӯ                бҙәбөҒбҙёбҙё в”Ӯ                          3 в”Ӯ
в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ

description: Returns the merged JSON object string which is formed by merging multiple JSON objects.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Returns the merged JSON object string which is formed by merging multiple JSON objects.<sep>[SYNTAX]: jsonMergePatch(json1, json2, ...)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - json вҖ” String with valid JSON.
Returned value
- If JSON object strings are valid, return the merged JSON object string. String .<sep>[RETURN]: - If JSON object strings are valid, return the merged JSON object string. String .<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT jsonMergePatch('{"a":1}', '{"name": "joey"}', '{"name": "tom"}', '{"name": "zoey"}') AS res
example:
SELECT jsonMergePatch('{"a":1}', '{"name": "joey"}', '{"name": "tom"}', '{"name": "zoey"}') AS res

в”Ңв”Җresв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
в”Ӯ {"a":1,"name":"zoey"} в”Ӯ
в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ

example:
в”Ңв”Җjsonв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”ҖJSONAllPaths(json)в”Җв”җ

Map(String, String) .<sep>[DEMO]: в”Ңв”Җjsonв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”ҖJSONAllPathsWithTypes(json)в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
example:
в”Ңв”Җjsonв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”ҖJSONAllPathsWithTypes(json)в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ

description:
Calculates the logical conjunction of two or more values.
Setting short_circuit_function_evaluation controls whether short-circuit evaluation is used. If enabled, val_i is evaluated only if (val_1 AND val_2 AND ... AND val_{i-1}) is true . For example, with short-circuit evaluation, no division-by-zero exception is thrown when executing the query SELECT and(number = 2, intDiv(1, number)) FROM numbers(5) .
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Calculates the logical conjunction of two or more values.
Setting short_circuit_function_evaluation controls whether short-circuit evaluation is used. If enabled, val_i is evaluated only if (val_1 AND val_2 AND ... AND val_{i-1}) is true . For example, with short-circuit evaluation, no division-by-zero exception is thrown when executing the query SELECT and(number = 2, intDiv(1, number)) FROM numbers(5) .<sep>[SYNTAX]: and(val1, val2...)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - val1, val2, ... вҖ” List of at least two values. Int , UInt , Float or Nullable .
Returned value
- 0 , if at least one argument evaluates to false ,
- NULL , if no argument evaluates to false and at least one argument is NULL ,
- 1 , otherwise.
Type: UInt8 or Nullable ( UInt8 ).<sep>[RETURN]: - 0 , if at least one argument evaluates to false ,
- NULL , if no argument evaluates to false and at least one argument is NULL ,
- 1 , otherwise.
Type: UInt8 or Nullable ( UInt8 ).<sep>[DEMO]: в”Ңв”Җand(0, 1, -2)в”Җв”җ ; в”Ңв”Җand(NULL, 1, 10, -2)в”Җв”җ

description:
Calculates the logical disjunction of two or more values.
Setting short_circuit_function_evaluation controls whether short-circuit evaluation is used. If enabled, val_i is evaluated only if ((NOT val_1) AND (NOT val_2) AND ... AND (NOT val_{i-1})) is true . For example, with short-circuit evaluation, no division-by-zero exception is thrown when executing the query SELECT or(number = 0, intDiv(1, number) != 0) FROM numbers(5) .
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Calculates the logical disjunction of two or more values.
Setting short_circuit_function_evaluation controls whether short-circuit evaluation is used. If enabled, val_i is evaluated only if ((NOT val_1) AND (NOT val_2) AND ... AND (NOT val_{i-1})) is true . For example, with short-circuit evaluation, no division-by-zero exception is thrown when executing the query SELECT or(number = 0, intDiv(1, number) != 0) FROM numbers(5) .<sep>[SYNTAX]: or(val1, val2...)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - val1, val2, ... вҖ” List of at least two values. Int , UInt , Float or Nullable .
Returned value
- 1 , if at least one argument evaluates to true ,
- 0 , if all arguments evaluate to false ,
- NULL , if all arguments evaluate to false and at least one argument is NULL .
Type: UInt8 or Nullable ( UInt8 ).<sep>[RETURN]: - 1 , if at least one argument evaluates to true ,
- 0 , if all arguments evaluate to false ,
- NULL , if all arguments evaluate to false and at least one argument is NULL .
Type: UInt8 or Nullable ( UInt8 ).<sep>[DEMO]: в”Ңв”Җor(1, 0, 0, 2, NULL)в”Җв”җ ; в”Ңв”Җor(0, NULL)в”Җв”җ

description: Calculates the logical exclusive disjunction of two or more values. For more than two input values, the function first xor-s the first two values, then xor-s the result with the third value etc.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Calculates the logical exclusive disjunction of two or more values. For more than two input values, the function first xor-s the first two values, then xor-s the result with the third value etc.<sep>[SYNTAX]: xor(val1, val2...)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - val1, val2, ... вҖ” List of at least two values. Int , UInt , Float or Nullable .
Returned value
- 1 , for two values: if one of the values evaluates to false and other does not,
- 0 , for two values: if both values evaluate to false or to both true ,
- NULL , if at least one of the inputs is NULL
Type: UInt8 or Nullable ( UInt8 ).<sep>[RETURN]: - 1 , for two values: if one of the values evaluates to false and other does not,
- 0 , for two values: if both values evaluate to false or to both true ,
- NULL , if at least one of the inputs is NULL
Type: UInt8 or Nullable ( UInt8 ).<sep>[DEMO]: в”Ңв”Җxor(0, 1, 1)в”Җв”җ

[DESCRIPTION]: Returns the sign of a real number.<sep>[SYNTAX]: sign(x)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - x вҖ” Values from вҲ’ вҲһ -\infty вҲ’ вҲһ to + вҲһ +\infty + вҲһ . Supports all numeric types in ClickHouse.
Returned value
- -1 for x < 0
- 0 for x = 0
- 1 for x > 0
Type: Int8 .<sep>[RETURN]: - -1 for x < 0
- 0 for x = 0
- 1 for x > 0
Type: Int8 .<sep>[DEMO]: в”Ңв”Җsign(0)в”Җв”җ ; в”Ңв”Җsign(1)в”Җв”җ ; в”Ңв”Җsign(-1)в”Җв”җ

description: Returns the sigmoid function .
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Returns the sigmoid function .<sep>[SYNTAX]: sigmoid(x)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - x вҖ” input value. Values from the interval: вҲ’ вҲһ < x < + вҲһ -\infty \lt x \lt +\infty вҲ’ вҲһ < x < + вҲһ . (U)Int* , Float* or Decimal* .
Returned value
- Corresponding value along the sigmoid curve between 0 and 1. Float64 .<sep>[RETURN]: - Corresponding value along the sigmoid curve between 0 and 1. Float64 .<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT round(sigmoid(x), 5) FROM (SELECT arrayJoin([-1, 0, 1]) AS x); ; 0.26894
example: SELECT round(sigmoid(x), 5) FROM (SELECT arrayJoin([-1, 0, 1]) AS x);

description:
Computes the factorial of an integer value. Works with any native integer type including UInt(8|16|32|64) and Int(8|16|32|64). The return type is UInt64.
The factorial of 0 is 1. Likewise, the factorial() function returns 1 for any negative value. The maximum positive value for the input argument is 20, a value of 21 or greater will cause exception throw.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Computes the factorial of an integer value. Works with any native integer type including UInt(8|16|32|64) and Int(8|16|32|64). The return type is UInt64.
The factorial of 0 is 1. Likewise, the factorial() function returns 1 for any negative value. The maximum positive value for the input argument is 20, a value of 21 or greater will cause exception throw.<sep>[SYNTAX]: factorial(n)<sep>[DEMO]: в”Ңв”Җfactorial(10)в”Җв”җ

description:
Returns the number of the bucket in which operand falls in a histogram having count equal-width buckets spanning the range low to high . Returns 0 if operand < low , and returns count+1 if operand >= high .
operand , low , high can be any native number type. count can only be unsigned native integer and its value cannot be zero.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Returns the number of the bucket in which operand falls in a histogram having count equal-width buckets spanning the range low to high . Returns 0 if operand < low , and returns count+1 if operand >= high .
operand , low , high can be any native number type. count can only be unsigned native integer and its value cannot be zero.<sep>[SYNTAX]: widthBucket(operand, low, high, count)<sep>[DEMO]: в”Ңв”ҖwidthBucket(10.15, -8.6, 23, 18)в”Җв”җ

tree: proportionsZTest(successes_x, successes_y, trials_x, trials_y, conf_level, pool_type)
description: Returns test statistics for the two proportion Z-test - a statistical test for comparing the proportions from two populations x and y .
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Returns test statistics for the two proportion Z-test - a statistical test for comparing the proportions from two populations x and y .<sep>[SYNTAX]: proportionsZTest(successes_x, successes_y, trials_x, trials_y, conf_level, pool_type)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - successes_x : Number of successes in population x . UInt64 .
- successes_y : Number of successes in population y . UInt64 .
- trials_x : Number of trials in population x . UInt64 .
- trials_y : Number of trials in population y . UInt64 .
- conf_level : Confidence level for the test. Float64 .
- pool_type : Selection of pooling (way in which the standard error is estimated). Can be either unpooled or pooled . String .
Returned value
- z_stat : Z statistic. Float64 .
- p_val : P value. Float64 .
- ci_low : The lower confidence interval. Float64 .
- ci_high : The upper confidence interval. Float64 .<sep>[RETURN]: - z_stat : Z statistic. Float64 .
- p_val : P value. Float64 .
- ci_low : The lower confidence interval. Float64 .
- ci_high : The upper confidence interval. Float64 .<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT proportionsZTest(10, 11, 100, 101, 0.95, 'unpooled'); ; в”Ңв”ҖproportionsZTest(10, 11, 100, 101, 0.95, 'unpooled')в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ

description: The detectCharset function detects the character set of the non-UTF8-encoded input string.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: The detectCharset function detects the character set of the non-UTF8-encoded input string.<sep>[SYNTAX]: detectCharset('text_to_be_analyzed')<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - text_to_be_analyzed вҖ” A collection (or sentences) of strings to analyze. String .
Returned value
- A String containing the code of the detected character set<sep>[RETURN]: - A String containing the code of the detected character set<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT detectCharset('Ich bleibe fГјr ein paar Tage.'); ; в”Ңв”ҖdetectCharset('Ich bleibe fГјr ein paar Tage.')в”Җв”җ

description:
Detects the language of the UTF8-encoded input string. The function uses the CLD2 library for detection, and it returns the 2-letter ISO language code.
The detectLanguage function works best when providing over 200 characters in the input string.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Detects the language of the UTF8-encoded input string. The function uses the CLD2 library for detection, and it returns the 2-letter ISO language code.
The detectLanguage function works best when providing over 200 characters in the input string.<sep>[SYNTAX]: detectLanguage('text_to_be_analyzed')<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - text_to_be_analyzed вҖ” A collection (or sentences) of strings to analyze. String .
Returned value
- The 2-letter ISO code of the detected language
Other possible results:
- un = unknown, can not detect any language.
- other = the detected language does not have 2 letter code.<sep>[RETURN]: - The 2-letter ISO code of the detected language
Other possible results:
- un = unknown, can not detect any language.
- other = the detected language does not have 2 letter code.<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT detectLanguage('Je pense que je ne parviendrai jamais Г  parler franГ§ais comme un natif. Where there's a will, there's a way.'); ; fr
example: SELECT detectLanguage('Je pense que je ne parviendrai jamais Г  parler franГ§ais comme un natif. Where there's a will, there's a way.');

description: Similar to the detectLanguage function, but detectLanguageMixed returns a Map of 2-letter language codes that are mapped to the percentage of the certain language in the text.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Similar to the detectLanguage function, but detectLanguageMixed returns a Map of 2-letter language codes that are mapped to the percentage of the certain language in the text.<sep>[SYNTAX]: detectLanguageMixed('text_to_be_analyzed')<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - text_to_be_analyzed вҖ” A collection (or sentences) of strings to analyze. String .
Returned value
- Map(String, Float32) : The keys are 2-letter ISO codes and the values are a percentage of text found for that language<sep>[RETURN]: - Map(String, Float32) : The keys are 2-letter ISO codes and the values are a percentage of text found for that language<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT detectLanguageMixed('дәҢе…ҺгӮ’иҝҪгҒҶиҖ…гҒҜдёҖе…ҺгӮ’гӮӮеҫ—гҒҡдәҢе…ҺгӮ’иҝҪгҒҶиҖ…гҒҜдёҖе…ҺгӮ’гӮӮеҫ—гҒҡ A vaincre sans peril, on triomphe sans gloire.'); ; в”Ңв”ҖdetectLanguageMixed()в”Җв”җ
example: SELECT detectLanguageMixed('дәҢе…ҺгӮ’иҝҪгҒҶиҖ…гҒҜдёҖе…ҺгӮ’гӮӮеҫ—гҒҡдәҢе…ҺгӮ’иҝҪгҒҶиҖ…гҒҜдёҖе…ҺгӮ’гӮӮеҫ—гҒҡ A vaincre sans peril, on triomphe sans gloire.');

description: Determines the programming language from the source code. Calculates all the unigrams and bigrams of commands in the source code. Then using a marked-up dictionary with weights of unigrams and bigrams of commands for various programming languages finds the biggest weight of the programming language and returns it.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Determines the programming language from the source code. Calculates all the unigrams and bigrams of commands in the source code. Then using a marked-up dictionary with weights of unigrams and bigrams of commands for various programming languages finds the biggest weight of the programming language and returns it.<sep>[SYNTAX]: detectProgrammingLanguage('source_code')<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - source_code вҖ” String representation of the source code to analyze. String .
Returned value
- Programming language. String .<sep>[RETURN]: - Programming language. String .<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT detectProgrammingLanguage('#include <iostream>'); ; в”Ңв”ҖdetectProgrammingLanguage('#include <iostream>')в”Җв”җ

description: Determines the sentiment of text data. Uses a marked-up sentiment dictionary, in which each word has a tonality ranging from -12 to 6 . For each text, it calculates the average sentiment value of its words and returns it in the range [-1,1] .
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Determines the sentiment of text data. Uses a marked-up sentiment dictionary, in which each word has a tonality ranging from -12 to 6 . For each text, it calculates the average sentiment value of its words and returns it in the range [-1,1] .<sep>[SYNTAX]: detectTonality(text)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - text вҖ” The text to be analyzed. String .
Returned value
- The average sentiment value of the words in text . Float32 .<sep>[RETURN]: - The average sentiment value of the words in text . Float32 .<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT detectTonality('РЁР°СҖРёРә - С…РҫСҖРҫСҲРёР№ РҝС‘СҒ'), -- Sharik is a good dog  ; в”Ңв”ҖdetectTonality('РЁР°СҖРёРә - С…РҫСҖРҫСҲРёР№ РҝС‘СҒ')в”Җв”¬в”ҖdetectTonality('РЁР°СҖРёРә - РҝС‘СҒ')в”Җв”¬в”ҖdetectTonality('РЁР°СҖРёРә - РҝР»РҫС…РҫР№ РҝС‘СҒ')в”Җв”җ
example:
SELECT detectTonality('РЁР°СҖРёРә - С…РҫСҖРҫСҲРёР№ РҝС‘СҒ'), -- Sharik is a good dog 
       detectTonality('РЁР°СҖРёРә - РҝС‘СҒ'), -- Sharik is a dog
       detectTonality('РЁР°СҖРёРә - РҝР»РҫС…РҫР№ РҝС‘СҒ'); -- Sharkik is a bad dog

в”Ңв”ҖdetectTonality('РЁР°СҖРёРә - С…РҫСҖРҫСҲРёР№ РҝС‘СҒ')в”Җв”¬в”ҖdetectTonality('РЁР°СҖРёРә - РҝС‘СҒ')в”Җв”¬в”ҖdetectTonality('РЁР°СҖРёРә - РҝР»РҫС…РҫР№ РҝС‘СҒ')в”Җв”җ

description: Performs lemmatization on a given word. Needs dictionaries to operate, which can be obtained here .
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Performs lemmatization on a given word. Needs dictionaries to operate, which can be obtained here .<sep>[SYNTAX]: lemmatize('language', word)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - language вҖ” Language which rules will be applied. String .
- word вҖ” Word that needs to be lemmatized. Must be lowercase. String .<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT lemmatize('en', 'wolves'); ; в”Ңв”Җlemmatize("wolves")в”Җв”җ

description: Performs stemming on a given word.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Performs stemming on a given word.<sep>[SYNTAX]: stem('language', word)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - language вҖ” Language which rules will be applied. Use the two letter ISO 639-1 code .
- word вҖ” word that needs to be stemmed. Must be in lowercase. String .<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT arrayMap(x -> stem('en', x), ['I', 'think', 'it', 'is', 'a', 'blessing', 'in', 'disguise']) AS res; ; в”Ңв”Җresв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
example: SELECT arrayMap(x -> stem('en', x), ['I', 'think', 'it', 'is', 'a', 'blessing', 'in', 'disguise']) AS res;

description:
Finds synonyms to a given word. There are two types of synonym extensions: plain and wordnet .
With the plain extension type we need to provide a path to a simple text file, where each line corresponds to a certain synonym set. Words in this line must be separated with space or tab characters.
With the wordnet extension type we need to provide a path to a directory with WordNet thesaurus in it. Thesaurus must contain a WordNet sense index.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Finds synonyms to a given word. There are two types of synonym extensions: plain and wordnet .
With the plain extension type we need to provide a path to a simple text file, where each line corresponds to a certain synonym set. Words in this line must be separated with space or tab characters.
With the wordnet extension type we need to provide a path to a directory with WordNet thesaurus in it. Thesaurus must contain a WordNet sense index.<sep>[SYNTAX]: synonyms('extension_name', word)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - extension_name вҖ” Name of the extension in which search will be performed. String .
- word вҖ” Word that will be searched in extension. String .<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT synonyms('list', 'important'); ; в”Ңв”Җsynonyms('list', 'important')в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ

description:
In the BSI (Bit-Sliced Index) storage method, the data is stored in Bit-Sliced Index and then compressed using Roaring Bitmap . Aggregating operations and pointwise operations are directly on the compressed data, which can significantly improve the efficiency of storage and query.
A vector contains indices and their corresponding values. The following are some characteristics and constraints of this data structure in BSI storage mode:
- The index type can be one of UInt8 , UInt16 , or UInt32 . Note: Considering the performance of 64-bit implementation of Roaring Bitmap, BSI format does not support UInt64 / Int64 .
- The value type can be one of Int8 , Int16 , Int32 , Int64 , UInt8 , UInt16 , UInt32 , UInt64 , Float32 , or Float64 . Note: The value type does not automatically expand. For example, if you use UInt8 as the value type, any sum that exceeds the capacity of UInt8 will result in an overflow rather than being promoted to a higher type; similarly, operations on integers will yield integer results (e.g., division will not automatically convert to a floating-point result). Therefore, it is important to plan and design the value type ahead of time. In real-world scenarios, floating-point types ( Float32 / Float64 ) are commonly used.
- Only two vectors with the same index type and value type can perform operations.
- The underlying storage uses Bit-Sliced Index, with bitmap storing indexes. Roaring Bitmap is used as the specific implementation of bitmap. A best practice is to concentrate the index in several Roaring Bitmap containers as much as possible to maximize compression and query performance.
- The Bit-Sliced Index mechanism converts value into binary. For floating-point types, the conversion uses fixed-point representation, which may lead to precision loss. The precision can be adjusted by customizing the number of bits used for the fractional part, default is 24 bits, which is sufficient for most scenarios. You can customize the number of integer bits and fractional bits when constructing NumericIndexedVector using aggregate function groupNumericIndexedVector with -State .
- There are three cases for indices: non-zero value, zero value and non-existent. In NumericIndexedVector, only non-zero value and zero value will be stored. In addition, in pointwise operations between two NumericIndexedVectors, the value of non-existent index will be treated as 0. In the division scenario, the result is zero when the divisor is zero.
There are two ways to create this structure: one is to use the aggregate function groupNumericIndexedVector with -State . Of course, you can continue to add suffix -if to accept an additional condition. The aggregate function will only process the rows that trigger the condition. The other is to build it from a map using numericIndexedVectorBuild . The groupNumericIndexedVectorState function allows customization of the number of integer and fractional bits through parameters, while numericIndexedVectorBuild does not.

tree:
groupNumericIndexedVectorState(col1, col2)
groupNumericIndexedVectorState(type, integer_bit_num, fraction_bit_num)(col1, col2)
description: Constructs a NumericIndexedVector from two data columns and returns the sum of all values as a Float64 type. If the suffix State is added, it returns a NumericIndexedVector object.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Constructs a NumericIndexedVector from two data columns and returns the sum of all values as a Float64 type. If the suffix State is added, it returns a NumericIndexedVector object.<sep>[SYNTAX]: groupNumericIndexedVectorState(col1, col2)
groupNumericIndexedVectorState(type, integer_bit_num, fraction_bit_num)(col1, col2)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - type : String, optional. Specifies the storage format. Currently, only 'BSI' is supported.
- integer_bit_num : UInt32 , optional. Effective under the 'BSI' storage format, this parameter indicates the number of bits used for the integer part. When the index type is an integer type, the default value corresponds to the number of bits used to store the index. For example, if the index type is UInt16, the default integer_bit_num is 16. For Float32 and Float64 index types, the default value of integer_bit_num is 40, so the integer part of the data that can be represented is in the range [-2^39, 2^39 - 1] . The legal range is [0, 64] .
- fraction_bit_num : UInt32 , optional. Effective under the 'BSI' storage format, this parameter indicates the number of bits used for the fractional part. When the value type is an integer, the default value is 0; when the value type is Float32 or Float64 types, the default value is 24. The valid range is [0, 24] .
- There is also a constraint that the valid range of integer_bit_num + fraction_bit_num is [0, 64].
- col1 : The index column. Supported types: UInt8 / UInt16 / UInt32 / Int8 / Int16 / Int32 .
- col2 : The value column. Supported types: Int8 / Int16 / Int32 / Int64 / UInt8 / UInt16 / UInt32 / UInt64 / Float32 / Float64 .
Return value
A Float64 value representing the sum of all values.<sep>[RETURN]: A Float64 value representing the sum of all values.<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT groupNumericIndexedVector(UserID, PlayTime) AS num FROM t;

description: Extracts the tail of a string following its last slash or backslash. This function if often used to extract the filename from a path.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Extracts the tail of a string following its last slash or backslash. This function if often used to extract the filename from a path.<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - expr вҖ” A value of type String . Backslashes must be escaped.
Returned Value
A string that contains:
- The tail of the input string after its last slash or backslash. If the input string ends with a slash or backslash (e.g. / or c:\ ), the function returns an empty string.
- The original string if there are no slashes or backslashes.<sep>[RETURN]: A string that contains:
- The tail of the input string after its last slash or backslash. If the input string ends with a slash or backslash (e.g. / or c:\ ), the function returns an empty string.
- The original string if there are no slashes or backslashes.<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT 'some/long/path/to/file' AS a, basename(a) ; в”Ңв”Җaв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җbasename('some\\long\\path\\to\\file')в”Җв”җ ; SELECT 'some\\long\\path\\to\\file' AS a, basename(a)

CREATE TABLE test
(
    `key` Int32,

    `i64` Int64,
    `f32` Float32,

)
ENGINE = MergeTree

description:
Converts a colour encoded in the sRGB colour space to the perceptually uniform OKLCH colour space.
If any input channel is outside [0...255] or the gamma value is non-positive, the behaviour is implementation-defined.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Converts a colour encoded in the sRGB colour space to the perceptually uniform OKLCH colour space.
If any input channel is outside [0...255] or the gamma value is non-positive, the behaviour is implementation-defined.<sep>[SYNTAX]: colorSRGBToOKLCH(tuple [, gamma])<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - tuple - Three numeric values R, G, B in the range [0...255] . Tuple .
- gamma - Optional numeric value. Exponent that is used to linearize sRGB by applying (x / 255)^gamma to each channel x . Defaults to 2.2 .
Returned values
- A tuple (L, C, H) of type Tuple(Float64, Float64, Float64) .
Implementation details
The conversion consists of three stages:
- sRGB to Linear sRGB
- Linear sRGB to OKLab
- OKLab to OKLCH.
Gamma is used at the first stage, when computing linear sRGB. For that we normalize sRGB values and take them in power of gamma. Observe, that this lacks some precision due to floating-point rounding. This design choice was made in order to be able to quickly compute values for different gammas, and since the difference does not changed the perception of the color significantly.
Two stages involve matrix multiplication and trigonometry conversions respectively. For more details on maths please see an article on OKLab color space: https://bottosson.github.io/posts/OKLab/
In order to have some references for colors in OKLCH space, and how they correspond to sRGB colors please see https://OKLCH.com/<sep>[RETURN]: - A tuple (L, C, H) of type Tuple(Float64, Float64, Float64) .
Implementation details
The conversion consists of three stages:
- sRGB to Linear sRGB
- Linear sRGB to OKLab
- OKLab to OKLCH.
Gamma is used at the first stage, when computing linear sRGB. For that we normalize sRGB values and take them in power of gamma. Observe, that this lacks some precision due to floating-point rounding. This design choice was made in order to be able to quickly compute values for different gammas, and since the difference does not changed the perception of the color significantly.
Two stages involve matrix multiplication and trigonometry conversions respectively. For more details on maths please see an article on OKLab color space: https://bottosson.github.io/posts/OKLab/
In order to have some references for colors in OKLCH space, and how they correspond to sRGB colors please see https://OKLCH.com/<sep>[DEMO]: в”Ңв”Җlchв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ

description:
Converts a colour from the OKLCH perceptual colour space to the familiar sRGB colour space.
If L is outside [0...1] , C is negative, or H is outside [0...360] , the result is implementation-defined.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Converts a colour from the OKLCH perceptual colour space to the familiar sRGB colour space.
If L is outside [0...1] , C is negative, or H is outside [0...360] , the result is implementation-defined.<sep>[SYNTAX]: colorOKLCHToSRGB(tuple [, gamma])<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - tuple - Three numeric values L , C , H , presented as tuple where L is in range [0...1] , C >= 0 and H is in range [0...360] . Tuple .
- gamma - Optional numeric value. Exponent that is used to transform linear sRGB back to sRGB by applying (x ^ (1 / gamma)) * 255 for each channel x . Defaults to 2.2 .
Returned values
- A tuple (R, G, B) of type Tuple(Float64, Float64, Float64) .
Implementation details
The conversion is inverse of colorSRGBToOKLCH :
- OKLCH to OKLab.
- OKLab to Linear sRGB
- Linear sRGB to sRGB
Second argument gamma is used at the last stage. Note, that all three channels are clipped in range [0...1] right before computing linear sRGB, and then set in power 1 / gamma . In case gamma is 0 , 1 / gamma is changed for 1'000'000 . Thus, regardless of the input we normally will have returned floats in range [0...255] .
As in case of colorSRGBToOKLCH , two other stages involve trigonometry conversions and matrix multiplication respectively. For more details on maths please see see an article on OKLab color space: https://bottosson.github.io/posts/oklab/
In order to have some references for colors in OKLCH space, and how they correspond to sRGB colors please see https://oklch.com/<sep>[RETURN]: - A tuple (R, G, B) of type Tuple(Float64, Float64, Float64) .
Implementation details
The conversion is inverse of colorSRGBToOKLCH :
- OKLCH to OKLab.
- OKLab to Linear sRGB
- Linear sRGB to sRGB
Second argument gamma is used at the last stage. Note, that all three channels are clipped in range [0...1] right before computing linear sRGB, and then set in power 1 / gamma . In case gamma is 0 , 1 / gamma is changed for 1'000'000 . Thus, regardless of the input we normally will have returned floats in range [0...255] .
As in case of colorSRGBToOKLCH , two other stages involve trigonometry conversions and matrix multiplication respectively. For more details on maths please see see an article on OKLab color space: https://bottosson.github.io/posts/oklab/
In order to have some references for colors in OKLCH space, and how they correspond to sRGB colors please see https://oklch.com/<sep>[DEMO]: в”Ңв”Җrgbв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ

description:
Returns whether the argument is a constant expression.
A constant expression is an expression whose result is known during query analysis, i.e. before execution. For example, expressions over literals are constant expressions.
This function is mostly intended for development, debugging and demonstration.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Returns whether the argument is a constant expression.
A constant expression is an expression whose result is known during query analysis, i.e. before execution. For example, expressions over literals are constant expressions.
This function is mostly intended for development, debugging and demonstration.<sep>[SYNTAX]: isConstant(x)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - x вҖ” Expression to check.
Returned values
- 1 if x is constant. UInt8 .
- 0 if x is non-constant. UInt8 .<sep>[RETURN]: - 1 if x is constant. UInt8 .
- 0 if x is non-constant. UInt8 .<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT isConstant(x + 1) FROM (SELECT 43 AS x) ; в”Ңв”ҖisConstant(plus(x, 1))в”Җв”җ ; WITH 3.14 AS pi SELECT isConstant(cos(pi))

description: Given the database name, the table name, and the column name as constant strings, returns 1 if the given column exists, otherwise 0.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Given the database name, the table name, and the column name as constant strings, returns 1 if the given column exists, otherwise 0.<sep>[SYNTAX]: hasColumnInTable(\['hostname'\[, 'username'\[, 'password'\]\],\] 'database', 'table', 'column')<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - database : name of the database. String literal
- table : name of the table. String literal
- column : name of the column. String literal
- hostname : remote server name to perform the check on. String literal
- username : username for remote server. String literal
- password : password for remote server. String literal
Returned value
- 1 if the given column exists.
- 0 , otherwise.
Implementation details
For elements in a nested data structure, the function checks for the existence of a column. For the nested data structure itself, the function returns 0.<sep>[RETURN]: - 1 if the given column exists.
- 0 , otherwise.
Implementation details
For elements in a nested data structure, the function checks for the existence of a column. For the nested data structure itself, the function returns 0.<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT hasColumnInTable('system','metrics','metric')

description: Returns whether Thread Fuzzer is effective. It can be used in tests to prevent runs from being too long.
detail: [DESCRIPTION]: Returns whether Thread Fuzzer is effective. It can be used in tests to prevent runs from being too long.<sep>[SYNTAX]: hasThreadFuzzer();

description:
Builds a bar chart.
bar(x, min, max, width) draws a band with width proportional to (x - min) and equal to width characters when x = max .
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Builds a bar chart.
bar(x, min, max, width) draws a band with width proportional to (x - min) and equal to width characters when x = max .<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - x вҖ” Size to display.
- min, max вҖ” Integer constants. The value must fit in Int64 .
- width вҖ” Constant, positive integer, can be fractional.
The band is drawn with accuracy to one eighth of a symbol.<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT ; в”Ңв”Җв”Җhв”Җв”¬в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җcв”Җв”¬в”Җbarв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
example:
SELECT
    toHour(EventTime) AS h,
    count() AS c,
    bar(c, 0, 600000, 20) AS bar
FROM test.hits
GROUP BY h
ORDER BY h ASC

description:
x вҖ“ What to transform.
array_from вҖ“ Constant array of values to convert.
array_to вҖ“ Constant array of values to convert the values in 'from' to.
default вҖ“ Which value to use if 'x' is not equal to any of the values in 'from'.
array_from and array_to must have equally many elements.
Signature:
For x equal to one of the elements in array_from , the function returns the corresponding element in array_to , i.e. the one at the same array index. Otherwise, it returns default . If multiple matching elements exist array_from , it returns the element corresponding to the first of them.
transform(T, Array(T), Array(U), U) -> U
T and U can be numeric, string, or Date or DateTime types. The same letter (T or U) means that types must be mutually compatible and not necessarily equal. For example, the first argument could have type Int64 , while the second argument could have type Array(UInt16) .
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: x вҖ“ What to transform.
array_from вҖ“ Constant array of values to convert.
array_to вҖ“ Constant array of values to convert the values in 'from' to.
default вҖ“ Which value to use if 'x' is not equal to any of the values in 'from'.
array_from and array_to must have equally many elements.
Signature:
For x equal to one of the elements in array_from , the function returns the corresponding element in array_to , i.e. the one at the same array index. Otherwise, it returns default . If multiple matching elements exist array_from , it returns the element corresponding to the first of them.
transform(T, Array(T), Array(U), U) -> U
T and U can be numeric, string, or Date or DateTime types. The same letter (T or U) means that types must be mutually compatible and not necessarily equal. For example, the first argument could have type Int64 , while the second argument could have type Array(UInt16) .<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT ; в”Ңв”Җtitleв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җcв”Җв”җ
example:
SELECT
    transform(SearchEngineID, [2, 3], ['Yandex', 'Google'], 'Other') AS title,
    count() AS c
FROM test.hits
WHERE SearchEngineID != 0
GROUP BY title
ORDER BY c DESC

в”Ңв”Җtitleв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җcв”Җв”җ

detail: [DESCRIPTION]: Similar to the other variation but has no 'default' argument. In case no match can be found, x is returned.<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT ; в”Ңв”Җsв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җcв”Җв”җ

description:
Given a size (number of bytes), this function returns a readable, rounded size with suffix (KB, MB, etc.) as string.
The opposite operations of this function are parseReadableSize , parseReadableSizeOrZero , and parseReadableSizeOrNull .
detail:

description: Given a number, this function returns a rounded number with suffix (thousand, million, billion, etc.) as string.
detail: [DESCRIPTION]: Given a number, this function returns a rounded number with suffix (thousand, million, billion, etc.) as string.<sep>[SYNTAX]: formatReadableQuantity(x)<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT ; в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җnumberв”Җв”¬в”Җnumber_for_humansв”Җв”җ

SELECT

    formatReadableQuantity(number) AS number_for_humans

description: Given a time interval (delta) in seconds, this function returns a time delta with year/month/day/hour/minute/second/millisecond/microsecond/nanosecond as string.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Given a time interval (delta) in seconds, this function returns a time delta with year/month/day/hour/minute/second/millisecond/microsecond/nanosecond as string.<sep>[SYNTAX]: formatReadableTimeDelta(column[, maximum_unit, minimum_unit])<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - column вҖ” A column with a numeric time delta.
- maximum_unit вҖ” Optional. Maximum unit to show. Acceptable values: nanoseconds , microseconds , milliseconds , seconds , minutes , hours , days , months , years . Default value: years .
- minimum_unit вҖ” Optional. Minimum unit to show. All smaller units are truncated. Acceptable values: nanoseconds , microseconds , milliseconds , seconds , minutes , hours , days , months , years . If explicitly specified value is bigger than maximum_unit , an exception will be thrown. Default value: seconds if maximum_unit is seconds or bigger, nanoseconds otherwise.<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT ; в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җelapsedв”Җв”¬в”Җtime_delta в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ ; SELECT
example:
SELECT
    arrayJoin([100, 12345, 432546534]) AS elapsed,
    formatReadableTimeDelta(elapsed) AS time_delta

в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җelapsedв”Җв”¬в”Җtime_delta в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ

в”Ӯ      12345 в”Ӯ 3 hours, 25 minutes and 45 seconds                              в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  432546534 в”Ӯ 13 years, 8 months, 17 days, 7 hours, 48 minutes and 54 seconds в”Ӯ
в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ

description:
Given a string containing a byte size and B , KiB , KB , MiB , MB , etc. as a unit (i.e. ISO/IEC 80000-13 or decimal byte unit), this function returns the corresponding number of bytes. If the function is unable to parse the input value, it throws an exception.
The inverse operations of this function are formatReadableSize and formatReadableDecimalSize .
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Given a string containing a byte size and B , KiB , KB , MiB , MB , etc. as a unit (i.e. ISO/IEC 80000-13 or decimal byte unit), this function returns the corresponding number of bytes. If the function is unable to parse the input value, it throws an exception.
The inverse operations of this function are formatReadableSize and formatReadableDecimalSize .<sep>[SYNTAX]: parseReadableSize(x)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - x : Readable size with ISO/IEC 80000-13 or decimal byte unit ( String ).
Returned value
- Number of bytes, rounded up to the nearest integer ( UInt64 ).<sep>[RETURN]: - Number of bytes, rounded up to the nearest integer ( UInt64 ).<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT ; в”Ңв”Җreadable_sizesв”Җв”¬в”Җв”Җв”Җsizesв”Җв”җ
example:
в”Ңв”Җreadable_sizesв”Җв”¬в”Җв”Җв”Җsizesв”Җв”җ
в”Ӯ 1 B            в”Ӯ       1 в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ 1 KiB          в”Ӯ    1024 в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ 3 MB           в”Ӯ 3000000 в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ 5.314 KiB      в”Ӯ    5442 в”Ӯ
в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ

description: Returns a monotonically increasing sequence number of the block containing the row. The returned block number is updated on a best-effort basis, i.e. it may not be fully accurate.
detail: [DESCRIPTION]: Returns a monotonically increasing sequence number of the block containing the row. The returned block number is updated on a best-effort basis, i.e. it may not be fully accurate.<sep>[SYNTAX]: blockNumber()<sep>[RETURN]: - Sequence number of the data block where the row is located. UInt64 .<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT blockNumber() ; в”Ңв”ҖblockNumber()в”Җв”җ

SELECT blockNumber()

description: Replaces literals, sequences of literals and complex aliases (containing whitespace, more than two digits or at least 36 bytes long such as UUIDs) with placeholder ? .
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Replaces literals, sequences of literals and complex aliases (containing whitespace, more than two digits or at least 36 bytes long such as UUIDs) with placeholder ? .<sep>[SYNTAX]: normalizeQuery(x)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - x вҖ” Sequence of characters. String .

description: Replaces literals, sequences of literals with placeholder ? but does not replace complex aliases (containing whitespace, more than two digits or at least 36 bytes long such as UUIDs). This helps better analyze complex query logs.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Replaces literals, sequences of literals with placeholder ? but does not replace complex aliases (containing whitespace, more than two digits or at least 36 bytes long such as UUIDs). This helps better analyze complex query logs.<sep>[SYNTAX]: normalizeQueryKeepNames(x)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - x вҖ” Sequence of characters. String .
Returned value

String .<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT normalizeQuery('SELECT 1 AS aComplexName123'), normalizeQueryKeepNames('SELECT 1 AS aComplexName123'); ; в”Ңв”ҖnormalizeQuery('SELECT 1 AS aComplexName123')в”Җв”¬в”ҖnormalizeQueryKeepNames('SELECT 1 AS aComplexName123')в”Җв”җ

description: The window function that provides access to a row at a specified offset before or after the current row of a given column.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: The window function that provides access to a row at a specified offset before or after the current row of a given column.<sep>[SYNTAX]: neighbor(column, offset[, default_value])<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - column вҖ” A column name or scalar expression.
- offset вҖ” The number of rows to look before or ahead of the current row in column . Int64 .
- default_value вҖ” Optional. The returned value if offset is beyond the block boundaries. Type of data blocks affected.
Returned values
- Value of column with offset distance from current row, if offset is not outside the block boundaries.
- The default value of column or default_value (if given), if offset is outside the block boundaries.<sep>[RETURN]: - Value of column with offset distance from current row, if offset is not outside the block boundaries.
- The default value of column or default_value (if given), if offset is outside the block boundaries.<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT number, neighbor(number, 2) FROM system.numbers LIMIT 10; ; в”Ңв”Җnumberв”Җв”¬в”Җneighbor(number, 2)в”Җв”җ ; SELECT number, neighbor(number, 2, 999) FROM system.numbers LIMIT 10;
example: SELECT number, neighbor(number, 2) FROM system.numbers LIMIT 10;

WITH toDate('2018-01-01') AS start_date
SELECT
    toStartOfMonth(start_date + (number * 32)) AS month,
    toInt32(month) % 100 AS money,
    neighbor(money, -12) AS prev_year,
    round(prev_year / money, 2) AS year_over_year
FROM numbers(16)

description:
Calculates the difference between two consecutive row values in the data block. Returns 0 for the first row, and for subsequent rows the difference to the previous row.
The result of the function depends on the affected data blocks and the order of data in the block.
The order of rows during calculation of runningDifference() can differ from the order of rows returned to the user. To prevent that you can create a subquery with ORDER BY and call the function from outside the subquery.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Calculates the difference between two consecutive row values in the data block. Returns 0 for the first row, and for subsequent rows the difference to the previous row.
The result of the function depends on the affected data blocks and the order of data in the block.
The order of rows during calculation of runningDifference() can differ from the order of rows returned to the user. To prevent that you can create a subquery with ORDER BY and call the function from outside the subquery.<sep>[SYNTAX]: runningDifference(x)<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT ; в”Ңв”ҖEventIDв”Җв”¬в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҖEventTimeв”Җв”¬в”Җdeltaв”Җв”җ ; SELECT
example:
SELECT
    EventID,
    EventTime,
    runningDifference(EventTime) AS delta
FROM
(
    SELECT
        EventID,
        EventTime
    FROM events
    WHERE EventDate = '2016-11-24'
    ORDER BY EventTime ASC
    LIMIT 5
)

в”Ңв”ҖEventIDв”Җв”¬в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҖEventTimeв”Җв”¬в”Җdeltaв”Җв”җ
в”Ӯ    1106 в”Ӯ 2016-11-24 00:00:04 в”Ӯ     0 в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ    1107 в”Ӯ 2016-11-24 00:00:05 в”Ӯ     1 в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ    1108 в”Ӯ 2016-11-24 00:00:05 в”Ӯ     0 в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ    1109 в”Ӯ 2016-11-24 00:00:09 в”Ӯ     4 в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ    1110 в”Ӯ 2016-11-24 00:00:10 в”Ӯ     1 в”Ӯ
в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ

SELECT
    number,
    runningDifference(number + 1) AS diff
FROM numbers(100000)
WHERE diff != 1

в”Ңв”Җnumberв”Җв”¬в”Җdiffв”Җв”җ
в”Ӯ      0 в”Ӯ    0 в”Ӯ
в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
в”Ңв”Җnumberв”Җв”¬в”Җdiffв”Җв”җ
в”Ӯ  65536 в”Ӯ    0 в”Ӯ
в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ

set max_block_size=100000 -- default value is 65536!

SELECT
    number,
    runningDifference(number + 1) AS diff
FROM numbers(100000)
WHERE diff != 1

description: Same as runningDifference , but returns the value of the first row as the value on the first row.
detail: [DESCRIPTION]: Same as runningDifference , but returns the value of the first row as the value on the first row.

description: Calculates the number of concurrent events. Each event has a start time and an end time. The start time is included in the event, while the end time is excluded. Columns with a start time and an end time must be of the same data type. The function calculates the total number of active (concurrent) events for each event start time.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Calculates the number of concurrent events. Each event has a start time and an end time. The start time is included in the event, while the end time is excluded. Columns with a start time and an end time must be of the same data type. The function calculates the total number of active (concurrent) events for each event start time.<sep>[SYNTAX]: runningConcurrency(start, end)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - start вҖ” A column with the start time of events. Date , DateTime , or DateTime64 .
- end вҖ” A column with the end time of events. Date , DateTime , or DateTime64 .
Returned values
- The number of concurrent events at each event start time. UInt32<sep>[RETURN]: - The number of concurrent events at each event start time. UInt32<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT start, runningConcurrency(start, end) FROM example_table; ; в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җstartв”Җв”¬в”ҖrunningConcurrency(start, end)в”Җв”җ
example: SELECT start, runningConcurrency(start, end) FROM example_table;
