description: Accepts a string, fixed string or numeric value and interprets the bytes as a number in host order (little endian). It returns a date from the interpreted number as the number of days since the beginning of the Unix Epoch.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Accepts a string, fixed string or numeric value and interprets the bytes as a number in host order (little endian). It returns a date from the interpreted number as the number of days since the beginning of the Unix Epoch.<sep>[SYNTAX]: reinterpretAsDate(x)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - x : number of days since the beginning of the Unix Epoch. (U)Int* , Float , Date , DateTime , UUID , String or FixedString .
Returned value
- Date. Date .
Implementation details<sep>[RETURN]: - Date. Date .
Implementation details<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT reinterpretAsDate(65), reinterpretAsDate('A'); ; в”Ңв”ҖreinterpretAsDate(65)в”Җв”¬в”ҖreinterpretAsDate('A')в”Җв”җ

description: These functions accept a string and interpret the bytes placed at the beginning of the string as a number in host order (little endian). Returns a date with time interpreted as the number of seconds since the beginning of the Unix Epoch.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: These functions accept a string and interpret the bytes placed at the beginning of the string as a number in host order (little endian). Returns a date with time interpreted as the number of seconds since the beginning of the Unix Epoch.<sep>[SYNTAX]: reinterpretAsDateTime(x)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - x : number of seconds since the beginning of the Unix Epoch. (U)Int* , Float , Date , DateTime , UUID , String or FixedString .
Returned value
- Date and Time. DateTime .
Implementation details<sep>[RETURN]: - Date and Time. DateTime .
Implementation details<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT reinterpretAsDateTime(65), reinterpretAsDateTime('A'); ; в”Ңв”ҖreinterpretAsDateTime(65)в”Җв”¬в”ҖreinterpretAsDateTime('A')в”Җв”җ

description: This function accepts a number, date or date with time and returns a string containing bytes representing the corresponding value in host order (little endian). Null bytes are dropped from the end. For example, a UInt32 type value of 255 is a string that is one byte long.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: This function accepts a number, date or date with time and returns a string containing bytes representing the corresponding value in host order (little endian). Null bytes are dropped from the end. For example, a UInt32 type value of 255 is a string that is one byte long.<sep>[SYNTAX]: reinterpretAsString(x)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - x : value to reinterpret to string. (U)Int* , Float , Date , DateTime .
Returned value
- String containing bytes representing x . String .<sep>[RETURN]: - String containing bytes representing x . String .<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT ; в”Ңв”ҖreinterpretAsString(toDateTime('1970-01-01 01:01:05'))в”Җв”¬в”ҖreinterpretAsString(toDate('1970-03-07'))в”Җв”җ

description: This function accepts a number, date or date with time and returns a FixedString containing bytes representing the corresponding value in host order (little endian). Null bytes are dropped from the end. For example, a UInt32 type value of 255 is a FixedString that is one byte long.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: This function accepts a number, date or date with time and returns a FixedString containing bytes representing the corresponding value in host order (little endian). Null bytes are dropped from the end. For example, a UInt32 type value of 255 is a FixedString that is one byte long.<sep>[SYNTAX]: reinterpretAsFixedString(x)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - x : value to reinterpret to string. (U)Int* , Float , Date , DateTime .
Returned value
- Fixed string containing bytes representing x . FixedString .<sep>[RETURN]: - Fixed string containing bytes representing x . FixedString .<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT ; в”Ңв”ҖreinterpretAsFixedString(toDateTime('1970-01-01 01:01:05'))в”Җв”¬в”ҖreinterpretAsFixedString(toDate('1970-03-07'))в”Җв”җ

description: Accepts a 16 byte string and returns a UUID by interpreting each 8-byte half in little-endian byte order. If the string isn't long enough, the function works as if the string is padded with the necessary number of null bytes to the end. If the string is longer than 16 bytes, the extra bytes at the end are ignored.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Accepts a 16 byte string and returns a UUID by interpreting each 8-byte half in little-endian byte order. If the string isn't long enough, the function works as if the string is padded with the necessary number of null bytes to the end. If the string is longer than 16 bytes, the extra bytes at the end are ignored.<sep>[SYNTAX]: reinterpretAsUUID(fixed_string)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - fixed_string вҖ” Big-endian byte string. FixedString .
Returned value
- The UUID type value. UUID .<sep>[RETURN]: - The UUID type value. UUID .<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT reinterpretAsUUID(reverse(unhex('000102030405060708090a0b0c0d0e0f'))); ; в”Ңв”ҖreinterpretAsUUID(reverse(unhex('000102030405060708090a0b0c0d0e0f')))в”Җв”җ ; WITH
example:
WITH
    generateUUIDv4() AS uuid,
    identity(lower(hex(reverse(reinterpretAsString(uuid))))) AS str,
    reinterpretAsUUID(reverse(unhex(str))) AS uuid2
SELECT uuid = uuid2;

description: Uses the same source in-memory bytes sequence for x value and reinterprets it to destination type.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Uses the same source in-memory bytes sequence for x value and reinterprets it to destination type.<sep>[SYNTAX]: reinterpret(x, type)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - x вҖ” Any type.
- type вҖ” Destination type. If it is an array, then the array element type must be a fixed length type.
Returned value
- Destination type value.<sep>[RETURN]: - Destination type value.<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT reinterpret(toInt8(-1), 'UInt8') AS int_to_uint, ; в”Ңв”Җint_to_uintв”Җв”¬в”Җint_to_floatв”Җв”¬в”Җstring_to_intв”Җв”җ ; SELECT reinterpret(x'3108b4403108d4403108b4403108d440', 'Array(Float32)') AS string_to_array_of_Float32;

tree:
CAST(x, T)
CAST(x AS t)
x::t
description: Converts an input value to the specified data type. Unlike the reinterpret function, CAST tries to present the same value using the new data type. If the conversion can not be done then an exception is raised. Several syntax variants are supported.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Converts an input value to the specified data type. Unlike the reinterpret function, CAST tries to present the same value using the new data type. If the conversion can not be done then an exception is raised. Several syntax variants are supported.<sep>[SYNTAX]: CAST(x, T)
CAST(x AS t)
x::t<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - x вҖ” A value to convert. May be of any type.
- T вҖ” The name of the target data type. String .
- t вҖ” The target data type.
Returned value
- Converted value.<sep>[RETURN]: - Converted value.<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT ; в”Ңв”Җcast_int_to_uintв”Җв”¬в”Җcast_float_to_decimalв”Җв”¬в”Җcast_string_to_intв”Җв”җ ; SELECT
example:
SELECT
    CAST(toInt8(-1), 'UInt8') AS cast_int_to_uint,
    CAST(1.5 AS Decimal(3,2)) AS cast_float_to_decimal,
    '1'::Int32 AS cast_string_to_int;

SELECT
    '2016-06-15 23:00:00' AS timestamp,
    CAST(timestamp AS DateTime) AS datetime,
    CAST(timestamp AS Date) AS date,
    CAST(timestamp, 'String') AS string,
    CAST(timestamp, 'FixedString(22)') AS fixed_string;

в”Ңв”Җtimestampв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җdatetimeв”Җв”¬в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җdateв”Җв”¬в”Җstringв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җfixed_stringв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
в”Ӯ 2016-06-15 23:00:00 в”Ӯ 2016-06-15 23:00:00 в”Ӯ 2016-06-15 в”Ӯ 2016-06-15 23:00:00 в”Ӯ 2016-06-15 23:00:00\0\0\0 в”Ӯ
в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ

description:
Converts x to the T data type.
The difference from cast is that accurateCast does not allow overflow of numeric types during cast if type value x does not fit the bounds of type T . For example, accurateCast(-1, 'UInt8') throws an exception.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Converts x to the T data type.
The difference from cast is that accurateCast does not allow overflow of numeric types during cast if type value x does not fit the bounds of type T . For example, accurateCast(-1, 'UInt8') throws an exception.<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT cast(-1, 'UInt8') AS uint8; ; в”Ңв”Җuint8в”Җв”җ ; SELECT accurateCast(-1, 'UInt8') AS uint8;
example: Code: 70. DB::Exception: Received from localhost:9000. DB::Exception: Value in column Int8 cannot be safely converted into type UInt8: While processing accurateCast(-1, 'UInt8') AS uint8.

description: Converts input value x to the specified data type T . Always returns Nullable type and returns NULL if the cast value is not representable in the target type.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Converts input value x to the specified data type T . Always returns Nullable type and returns NULL if the cast value is not representable in the target type.<sep>[SYNTAX]: accurateCastOrNull(x, T)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - x вҖ” Input value.
- T вҖ” The name of the returned data type.
Returned value
- The value, converted to the specified data type T .<sep>[RETURN]: - The value, converted to the specified data type T .<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT toTypeName(accurateCastOrNull(5, 'UInt8')); ; в”Ңв”ҖtoTypeName(accurateCastOrNull(5, 'UInt8'))в”Җв”җ ; SELECT
example:
SELECT
    accurateCastOrNull(-1, 'UInt8') AS uint8,
    accurateCastOrNull(128, 'Int8') AS int8,
    accurateCastOrNull('Test', 'FixedString(2)') AS fixed_string;

в”Ңв”Җuint8в”Җв”¬в”Җint8в”Җв”¬в”Җfixed_stringв”Җв”җ
в”Ӯ  бҙәбөҒбҙёбҙё в”Ӯ бҙәбөҒбҙёбҙё в”Ӯ бҙәбөҒбҙёбҙё         в”Ӯ
в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ

description: Converts input value x to the specified data type T . Returns default type value or default_value if specified if the cast value is not representable in the target type.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Converts input value x to the specified data type T . Returns default type value or default_value if specified if the cast value is not representable in the target type.<sep>[SYNTAX]: accurateCastOrDefault(x, T)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - x вҖ” Input value.
- T вҖ” The name of the returned data type.
- default_value вҖ” Default value of returned data type.
Returned value
- The value converted to the specified data type T .<sep>[RETURN]: - The value converted to the specified data type T .<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT toTypeName(accurateCastOrDefault(5, 'UInt8')); ; в”Ңв”ҖtoTypeName(accurateCastOrDefault(5, 'UInt8'))в”Җв”җ ; SELECT
example:
SELECT
    accurateCastOrDefault(-1, 'UInt8') AS uint8,
    accurateCastOrDefault(-1, 'UInt8', 5) AS uint8_default,
    accurateCastOrDefault(128, 'Int8') AS int8,
    accurateCastOrDefault(128, 'Int8', 5) AS int8_default,
    accurateCastOrDefault('Test', 'FixedString(2)') AS fixed_string,
    accurateCastOrDefault('Test', 'FixedString(2)', 'Te') AS fixed_string_default;

в”Ңв”Җuint8в”Җв”¬в”Җuint8_defaultв”Җв”¬в”Җint8в”Җв”¬в”Җint8_defaultв”Җв”¬в”Җfixed_stringв”Җв”¬в”Җfixed_string_defaultв”Җв”җ
в”Ӯ     0 в”Ӯ             5 в”Ӯ    0 в”Ӯ            5 в”Ӯ              в”Ӯ Te                   в”Ӯ
в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ

description: Creates an Interval data type value from a numeric value and interval unit (eg. 'second' or 'day').
detail:

'second' or 'day').<sep>[SYNTAX]: toInterval(value, unit)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - value вҖ” Length of the interval. Integer numbers or string representations thereof, and float numbers. (U)Int* / Float* / String .
- unit вҖ” The type of interval to create. String Literal . Possible values: nanosecond microsecond millisecond second minute hour day week month quarter year The unit argument is case-insensitive.
Returned value

description:
Converts a String to DateTime according to a MySQL format string .
This function is the opposite operation of function formatDateTime .
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Converts a String to DateTime according to a MySQL format string .
This function is the opposite operation of function formatDateTime .<sep>[SYNTAX]: parseDateTime(str[, format[, timezone]])<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - str вҖ” The String to be parsed
- format вҖ” The format string. Optional. %Y-%m-%d %H:%i:%s if not specified.
- timezone вҖ” Timezone . Optional.
Returned value(s)
Return a DateTime value parsed from the input string according to a MySQL-style format string.
Supported format specifiers
All format specifiers listed in formatDateTime except:
- %Q: Quarter (1-4)<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT parseDateTime('2021-01-04+23:00:00', '%Y-%m-%d+%H:%i:%s')

Alias: str_to_date .

description:
Similar to parseDateTime , except that the format string is in Joda instead of MySQL syntax.
This function is the opposite operation of function formatDateTimeInJodaSyntax .
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Similar to parseDateTime , except that the format string is in Joda instead of MySQL syntax.
This function is the opposite operation of function formatDateTimeInJodaSyntax .<sep>[SYNTAX]: parseDateTimeInJodaSyntax(str[, format[, timezone]])<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - str вҖ” The String to be parsed
- format вҖ” The format string. Optional. yyyy-MM-dd HH:mm:ss if not specified.
- timezone вҖ” Timezone . Optional.
Returned value(s)
Return a DateTime value parsed from the input string according to a Joda-style format string.
Supported format specifiers
All format specifiers listed in formatDateTimeInJodaSyntax are supported, except:
- S: fraction of second
- z: time zone
- Z: time zone offset/id<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT parseDateTimeInJodaSyntax('2023-02-24 14:53:31', 'yyyy-MM-dd HH:mm:ss', 'Europe/Minsk')
example:
SELECT parseDateTimeInJodaSyntax('2023-02-24 14:53:31', 'yyyy-MM-dd HH:mm:ss', 'Europe/Minsk')

в”Ңв”ҖparseDateTimeInJodaSyntax('2023-02-24 14:53:31', 'yyyy-MM-dd HH:mm:ss', 'Europe/Minsk')в”Җв”җ
в”Ӯ                                                                     2023-02-24 14:53:31 в”Ӯ
в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ

description: Converts a String to DateTime64 according to a MySQL format string .
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Converts a String to DateTime64 according to a MySQL format string .<sep>[SYNTAX]: parseDateTime64(str[, format[, timezone]])<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - str вҖ” The String to be parsed.
- format вҖ” The format string. Optional. %Y-%m-%d %H:%i:%s.%f if not specified.
- timezone вҖ” Timezone . Optional.
Returned value(s)
Return a DateTime64 value parsed from the input string according to a MySQL-style format string. The precision of the returned value is 6.

description: Converts a String to DateTime64 according to a Joda format string .
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Converts a String to DateTime64 according to a Joda format string .<sep>[SYNTAX]: parseDateTime64InJodaSyntax(str[, format[, timezone]])<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - str вҖ” The String to be parsed.
- format вҖ” The format string. Optional. yyyy-MM-dd HH:mm:ss if not specified.
- timezone вҖ” Timezone . Optional.
Returned value(s)
Return a DateTime64 value parsed from the input string according to a Joda-style format string. The precision of the returned value equal to the number of S placeholders in the format string (but at most 6).

description:
Converts a date and time in the String representation to DateTime data type.
The function parses ISO 8601 , RFC 1123 - 5.2.14 RFC-822 Date and Time Specification , ClickHouse's and some other date and time formats.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Converts a date and time in the String representation to DateTime data type.
The function parses ISO 8601 , RFC 1123 - 5.2.14 RFC-822 Date and Time Specification , ClickHouse's and some other date and time formats.<sep>[SYNTAX]: parseDateTimeBestEffort(time_string [, time_zone])<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - time_string вҖ” String containing a date and time to convert. String .
- time_zone вҖ” Time zone. The function parses time_string according to the time zone. String .
Supported non-standard formats
- A string containing 9..10 digit unix timestamp .
- A string with a date and a time component: YYYYMMDDhhmmss , DD/MM/YYYY hh:mm:ss , DD-MM-YY hh:mm , YYYY-MM-DD hh:mm:ss , etc.
- A string with a date, but no time component: YYYY , YYYYMM , YYYY*MM , DD/MM/YYYY , DD-MM-YY etc.
- A string with a day and time: DD , DD hh , DD hh:mm . In this case MM is substituted by 01 .
- A string that includes the date and time along with time zone offset information: YYYY-MM-DD hh:mm:ss Вұh:mm , etc. For example, 2020-12-12 17:36:00 -5:00 .
- A syslog timestamp : Mmm dd hh:mm:ss . For example, Jun 9 14:20:32 .
For all of the formats with separator the function parses months names expressed by their full name or by the first three letters of a month name. Examples: 24/DEC/18 , 24-Dec-18 , 01-September-2018 . If the year is not specified, it is considered to be equal to the current year. If the resulting DateTime happen to be in the future (even by a second after the current moment), then the current year is substituted by the previous year.
Returned value
- time_string converted to the DateTime data type.<sep>[RETURN]: - time_string converted to the DateTime data type.<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT parseDateTimeBestEffort('23/10/2020 12:12:57') ; в”Ңв”ҖparseDateTimeBestEffortв”Җв”җ ; SELECT parseDateTimeBestEffort('Sat, 18 Aug 2018 07:22:16 GMT', 'Asia/Istanbul')
example:
SELECT parseDateTimeBestEffort('Sat, 18 Aug 2018 07:22:16 GMT', 'Asia/Istanbul')
AS parseDateTimeBestEffort;

WITH
    now() AS ts_now,
    formatDateTime(ts_around, '%b %e %T') AS syslog_arg
SELECT
    ts_now,
    syslog_arg,
    parseDateTimeBestEffort(syslog_arg)
FROM (SELECT arrayJoin([ts_now - 30, ts_now + 30]) AS ts_around);

в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җts_nowв”Җв”¬в”Җsyslog_argв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”ҖparseDateTimeBestEffort(syslog_arg)в”Җв”җ
в”Ӯ 2023-06-30 23:59:30 в”Ӯ Jun 30 23:59:00 в”Ӯ                 2023-06-30 23:59:00 в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ 2023-06-30 23:59:30 в”Ӯ Jul  1 00:00:00 в”Ӯ                 2022-07-01 00:00:00 в”Ӯ
в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ

description: This function behaves like parseDateTimeBestEffort for ISO date formats, e.g. YYYY-MM-DD hh:mm:ss , and other date formats where the month and date components can be unambiguously extracted, e.g. YYYYMMDDhhmmss , YYYY-MM , DD hh , or YYYY-MM-DD hh:mm:ss Вұh:mm . If the month and the date components cannot be unambiguously extracted, e.g. MM/DD/YYYY , MM-DD-YYYY , or MM-DD-YY , it prefers the US date format instead of DD/MM/YYYY , DD-MM-YYYY , or DD-MM-YY . As an exception from the latter, if the month is bigger than 12 and smaller or equal than 31, this function falls back to the behavior of parseDateTimeBestEffort , e.g. 15/08/2020 is parsed as 2020-08-15 .
detail: [DESCRIPTION]: This function behaves like parseDateTimeBestEffort for ISO date formats, e.g. YYYY-MM-DD hh:mm:ss , and other date formats where the month and date components can be unambiguously extracted, e.g. YYYYMMDDhhmmss , YYYY-MM , DD hh , or YYYY-MM-DD hh:mm:ss Вұh:mm . If the month and the date components cannot be unambiguously extracted, e.g. MM/DD/YYYY , MM-DD-YYYY , or MM-DD-YY , it prefers the US date format instead of DD/MM/YYYY , DD-MM-YYYY , or DD-MM-YY . As an exception from the latter, if the month is bigger than 12 and smaller or equal than 31, this function falls back to the behavior of parseDateTimeBestEffort , e.g. 15/08/2020 is parsed as 2020-08-15 .

description: Same as parseDateTimeBestEffort function but also parse milliseconds and microseconds and returns DateTime data type.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Same as parseDateTimeBestEffort function but also parse milliseconds and microseconds and returns DateTime data type.<sep>[SYNTAX]: parseDateTime64BestEffort(time_string [, precision [, time_zone]])<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - time_string вҖ” String containing a date or date with time to convert. String .
- precision вҖ” Required precision. 3 вҖ” for milliseconds, 6 вҖ” for microseconds. Default вҖ” 3 . Optional. UInt8 .
- time_zone вҖ” Timezone . The function parses time_string according to the timezone. Optional. String .
Returned value
- time_string converted to the DateTime data type.<sep>[RETURN]: - time_string converted to the DateTime data type.<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT parseDateTime64BestEffort('2021-01-01') AS a, toTypeName(a) AS t ; в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җaв”Җв”¬в”Җtв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
example:
SELECT parseDateTime64BestEffort('2021-01-01') AS a, toTypeName(a) AS t
UNION ALL
SELECT parseDateTime64BestEffort('2021-01-01 01:01:00.12346') AS a, toTypeName(a) AS t
UNION ALL
SELECT parseDateTime64BestEffort('2021-01-01 01:01:00.12346',6) AS a, toTypeName(a) AS t
UNION ALL
SELECT parseDateTime64BestEffort('2021-01-01 01:01:00.12346',3,'Asia/Istanbul') AS a, toTypeName(a) AS t
FORMAT PrettyCompactMonoBlock;

в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җaв”Җв”¬в”Җtв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
в”Ӯ 2021-01-01 01:01:00.123000 в”Ӯ DateTime64(3)                  в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ 2021-01-01 00:00:00.000000 в”Ӯ DateTime64(3)                  в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ 2021-01-01 01:01:00.123460 в”Ӯ DateTime64(6)                  в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ 2020-12-31 22:01:00.123000 в”Ӯ DateTime64(3, 'Asia/Istanbul') в”Ӯ
в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ

description:
Converts input parameter to the LowCardinality version of same data type.
To convert data from the LowCardinality data type use the CAST function. For example, CAST(x as String) .
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Converts input parameter to the LowCardinality version of same data type.
To convert data from the LowCardinality data type use the CAST function. For example, CAST(x as String) .<sep>[SYNTAX]: toLowCardinality(expr)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - expr вҖ” Expression resulting in one of the supported data types .
Returned values
- Result of expr . LowCardinality of the type of expr .<sep>[RETURN]: - Result of expr . LowCardinality of the type of expr .<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT toLowCardinality('1'); ; в”Ңв”ҖtoLowCardinality('1')в”Җв”җ

description: Converts a DateTime64 to a Int64 value with fixed second precision. The input value is scaled up or down appropriately depending on its precision.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Converts a DateTime64 to a Int64 value with fixed second precision. The input value is scaled up or down appropriately depending on its precision.<sep>[SYNTAX]: toUnixTimestamp64Second(value)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - value вҖ” DateTime64 value with any precision. DateTime64 .
Returned value
- value converted to the Int64 data type. Int64 .<sep>[RETURN]: - value converted to the Int64 data type. Int64 .<sep>[DEMO]: WITH toDateTime64('2009-02-13 23:31:31.011', 3, 'UTC') AS dt64 ; в”Ңв”ҖtoUnixTimestamp64Second(dt64)в”Җв”җ
example:
WITH toDateTime64('2009-02-13 23:31:31.011', 3, 'UTC') AS dt64
SELECT toUnixTimestamp64Second(dt64);

description: Converts an Int64 to a DateTime64 value with fixed second precision and optional timezone. The input value is scaled up or down appropriately depending on its precision.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Converts an Int64 to a DateTime64 value with fixed second precision and optional timezone. The input value is scaled up or down appropriately depending on its precision.<sep>[SYNTAX]: fromUnixTimestamp64Second(value[, timezone])<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - value вҖ” value with any precision. Int64 .
- timezone вҖ” (optional) timezone name of the result. String .
Returned value
- value converted to DateTime64 with precision 0 . DateTime64 .<sep>[RETURN]: - value converted to DateTime64 with precision 0 . DateTime64 .<sep>[DEMO]: WITH CAST(1733935988, 'Int64') AS i64 ; в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җxв”Җв”¬в”ҖtoTypeName(x)в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
example:
WITH CAST(1733935988, 'Int64') AS i64
SELECT
    fromUnixTimestamp64Second(i64, 'UTC') AS x,
    toTypeName(x);

DateTime64 .<sep>[DEMO]: WITH CAST(1733935988123456789, 'Int64') AS i64 ; в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җxв”Җв”¬в”ҖtoTypeName(x)в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
example:
WITH CAST(1733935988123456789, 'Int64') AS i64
SELECT
    fromUnixTimestamp64Nano(i64, 'UTC') AS x,
    toTypeName(x);

description: Some functions might throw an exception if the data is invalid. In this case, the query is canceled and an error text is returned to the client. For distributed processing, when an exception occurs on one of the servers, the other servers also attempt to abort the query.
detail: [DESCRIPTION]: Some functions might throw an exception if the data is invalid. In this case, the query is canceled and an error text is returned to the client. For distributed processing, when an exception occurs on one of the servers, the other servers also attempt to abort the query.

description: In almost all programming languages, one of the arguments might not be evaluated for certain operators. This is usually the operators && , || , and ?: . But in ClickHouse, arguments of functions (operators) are always evaluated. This is because entire parts of columns are evaluated at once, instead of calculating each row separately.
detail: [DESCRIPTION]: In almost all programming languages, one of the arguments might not be evaluated for certain operators. This is usually the operators && , || , and ?: . But in ClickHouse, arguments of functions (operators) are always evaluated. This is because entire parts of columns are evaluated at once, instead of calculating each row separately.

keyword: Performing Functions for Distributed Query Processing
description:
For distributed query processing, as many stages of query processing as possible are performed on remote servers, and the rest of the stages (merging intermediate results and everything after that) are performed on the requestor server.
This means that functions can be performed on different servers. For example, in the query SELECT f(sum(g(x))) FROM distributed_table GROUP BY h(y),
- if a distributed_table has at least two shards, the functions 'g' and 'h' are performed on remote servers, and the function 'f' is performed on the requestor server.
- if a distributed_table has only one shard, all the 'f', 'g', and 'h' functions are performed on this shard's server.
The result of a function usually does not depend on which server it is performed on. However, sometimes this is important. For example, functions that work with dictionaries use the dictionary that exists on the server they are running on. Another example is the hostName function, which returns the name of the server it is running on in order to make GROUP BY by servers in a SELECT query.
If a function in a query is performed on the requestor server, but you need to perform it on remote servers, you can wrap it in an 'any' aggregate function or add it to a key in GROUP BY .
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: For distributed query processing, as many stages of query processing as possible are performed on remote servers, and the rest of the stages (merging intermediate results and everything after that) are performed on the requestor server.
This means that functions can be performed on different servers. For example, in the query SELECT f(sum(g(x))) FROM distributed_table GROUP BY h(y),
- if a distributed_table has at least two shards, the functions 'g' and 'h' are performed on remote servers, and the function 'f' is performed on the requestor server.
- if a distributed_table has only one shard, all the 'f', 'g', and 'h' functions are performed on this shard's server.
The result of a function usually does not depend on which server it is performed on. However, sometimes this is important. For example, functions that work with dictionaries use the dictionary that exists on the server they are running on. Another example is the hostName function, which returns the name of the server it is running on in order to make GROUP BY by servers in a SELECT query.
If a function in a query is performed on the requestor server, but you need to perform it on remote servers, you can wrap it in an 'any' aggregate function or add it to a key in GROUP BY .

description: Extracts the hostname from a URL.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Extracts the hostname from a URL.<sep>[SYNTAX]: domain(url)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - url вҖ” URL. String .
The URL can be specified with or without a protocol. Examples:
svn+ssh://some.svn-hosting.com:80/repo/trunk
some.svn-hosting.com:80/repo/trunk
https://clickhouse.com/time/
For these examples, the domain function returns the following results:
some.svn-hosting.com
some.svn-hosting.com
clickhouse.com
Returned values
- Host name if the input string can be parsed as a URL, otherwise an empty string. String .<sep>[RETURN]: - Host name if the input string can be parsed as a URL, otherwise an empty string. String .<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT domain('svn+ssh://some.svn-hosting.com:80/repo/trunk'); ; в”Ңв”Җdomain('svn+ssh://some.svn-hosting.com:80/repo/trunk')в”Җв”җ

description: Returns the "first significant subdomain". The first significant subdomain is a second-level domain for com , net , org , or co , otherwise it is a third-level domain. For example, firstSignificantSubdomain ('https://news.clickhouse.com/') = 'clickhouse', firstSignificantSubdomain ('https://news.clickhouse.com.tr/') = 'clickhouse' . The list of "insignificant" second-level domains and other implementation details may change in the future.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Returns the "first significant subdomain". The first significant subdomain is a second-level domain for com , net , org , or co , otherwise it is a third-level domain. For example, firstSignificantSubdomain ('https://news.clickhouse.com/') = 'clickhouse', firstSignificantSubdomain ('https://news.clickhouse.com.tr/') = 'clickhouse' . The list of "insignificant" second-level domains and other implementation details may change in the future.<sep>[SYNTAX]: firstSignificantSubdomain(url)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - url вҖ” URL. String .
Returned value
- The first significant subdomain. String .<sep>[RETURN]: - The first significant subdomain. String .<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT firstSignificantSubdomain('http://www.example.com/a/b/c?a=b') ; в”Ңв”ҖfirstSignificantSubdomain('http://www.example.com/a/b/c?a=b')в”Җв”җ

description:
Returns the part of the domain that includes top-level subdomains up to the first significant subdomain. Accepts custom TLD list name. This function can be useful if you need a fresh TLD list or if you have a custom list.
Configuration example
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Returns the part of the domain that includes top-level subdomains up to the first significant subdomain. Accepts custom TLD list name. This function can be useful if you need a fresh TLD list or if you have a custom list.
Configuration example<sep>[SYNTAX]: cutToFirstSignificantSubdomain(url, tld)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - url вҖ” URL. String .
- tld вҖ” Custom TLD list name. String .
Returned value
- Part of the domain that includes top-level subdomains up to the first significant subdomain. String .<sep>[RETURN]: - Part of the domain that includes top-level subdomains up to the first significant subdomain. String .<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT cutToFirstSignificantSubdomainCustom('bar.foo.there-is-no-such-domain', 'public_suffix_list'); ; в”Ңв”ҖcutToFirstSignificantSubdomainCustom('bar.foo.there-is-no-such-domain', 'public_suffix_list')в”Җв”җ

description:
Generates a version 7 UUID .
The generated UUID contains the current Unix timestamp in milliseconds (48 bits), followed by version "7" (4 bits), a counter (42 bit) to distinguish UUIDs within a millisecond (including a variant field "2", 2 bit), and a random field (32 bits). For any given timestamp (unix_ts_ms), the counter starts at a random value and is incremented by 1 for each new UUID until the timestamp changes. In case the counter overflows, the timestamp field is incremented by 1 and the counter is reset to a random new start value.
Function generateUUIDv7 guarantees that the counter field within a timestamp increments monotonically across all function invocations in concurrently running threads and queries.

description:
Converts a DateTime value to a UUIDv7 at the given time.
The generated UUID casts the 32 bit DateTime value to milliseconds and stores in the 48 bit unix_ts_ms, followed by version "7" (4 bits), a counter (42 bit) to distinguish UUIDs within a millisecond (including a variant field "2", 2 bit), and a random field (32 bits). For any given timestamp (unix_ts_ms), the counter starts at a random value and is incremented by 1 for each new UUID until the timestamp changes. In case the counter overflows, the timestamp field is incremented by 1 and the counter is reset to a random new start value.
Function dateTimeToUUIDv7 guarantees that the counter field within a timestamp increments monotonically across all function invocations in concurrently running threads and queries.

detail:

- variant вҖ” Integer, representing a variant as specified by RFC4122 . 1 = Big-endian (default), 2 = Microsoft .
Returned value
FixedString(16)
Usage examples
SELECT
    '612f3c40-5d3b-217e-707b-6a546a3d7b29' AS uuid,
    UUIDStringToNum(uuid) AS bytes<sep>[RETURN]: FixedString(16)
Usage examples
SELECT
    '612f3c40-5d3b-217e-707b-6a546a3d7b29' AS uuid,
    UUIDStringToNum(uuid) AS bytes<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT ; в”Ңв”Җuuidв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җbytesв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ ; SELECT
example:
SELECT
    '612f3c40-5d3b-217e-707b-6a546a3d7b29' AS uuid,
    UUIDStringToNum(uuid) AS bytes

SELECT
    '612f3c40-5d3b-217e-707b-6a546a3d7b29' AS uuid,
    UUIDStringToNum(uuid, 2) AS bytes

description: Accepts binary containing a binary representation of a UUID, with its format optionally specified by variant ( Big-endian by default), and returns a string containing 36 characters in text format.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Accepts binary containing a binary representation of a UUID, with its format optionally specified by variant ( Big-endian by default), and returns a string containing 36 characters in text format.<sep>[SYNTAX]: UUIDNumToString(binary[, variant = 1])<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - binary вҖ” FixedString(16) as a binary representation of a UUID.
- variant вҖ” Integer, representing a variant as specified by RFC4122 . 1 = Big-endian (default), 2 = Microsoft .
Returned value
String.
Usage example
SELECT
    'a/<@];!~p{jTj={)' AS bytes,
    UUIDNumToString(toFixedString(bytes, 16)) AS uuid<sep>[RETURN]: String.
Usage example
SELECT
    'a/<@];!~p{jTj={)' AS bytes,
    UUIDNumToString(toFixedString(bytes, 16)) AS uuid<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT ; в”Ңв”Җbytesв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җuuidв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ ; SELECT
example:
SELECT
    'a/<@];!~p{jTj={)' AS bytes,
    UUIDNumToString(toFixedString(bytes, 16)) AS uuid

SELECT
    '@</a;]~!p{jTj={)' AS bytes,
    UUIDNumToString(toFixedString(bytes, 16), 2) AS uuid

description: Accepts a UUID and returns its binary representation as a FixedString(16) , with its format optionally specified by variant ( Big-endian by default). This function replaces calls to two separate functions UUIDStringToNum(toString(uuid)) so no intermediate conversion from UUID to string is required to extract bytes from a UUID.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Accepts a UUID and returns its binary representation as a FixedString(16) , with its format optionally specified by variant ( Big-endian by default). This function replaces calls to two separate functions UUIDStringToNum(toString(uuid)) so no intermediate conversion from UUID to string is required to extract bytes from a UUID.<sep>[SYNTAX]: UUIDToNum(uuid[, variant = 1])<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - uuid вҖ” UUID .
- variant вҖ” Integer, representing a variant as specified by RFC4122 . 1 = Big-endian (default), 2 = Microsoft .
Returned value
The binary representation of the UUID.
Usage examples
SELECT
    toUUID('612f3c40-5d3b-217e-707b-6a546a3d7b29') AS uuid,
    UUIDToNum(uuid) AS bytes<sep>[RETURN]: The binary representation of the UUID.
Usage examples

description: Returns the timestamp component of a UUID version 7.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Returns the timestamp component of a UUID version 7.<sep>[SYNTAX]: UUIDv7ToDateTime(uuid[, timezone])<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - uuid вҖ” UUID of version 7.
- timezone вҖ” Timezone name for the returned value (optional). String .
Returned value
- Timestamp with milliseconds precision. If the UUID is not a valid version 7 UUID, it returns 1970-01-01 00:00:00.000. DateTime64(3) .
Usage examples
SELECT UUIDv7ToDateTime(toUUID('018f05c9-4ab8-7b86-b64e-c9f03fbd45d1'))<sep>[RETURN]: - Timestamp with milliseconds precision. If the UUID is not a valid version 7 UUID, it returns 1970-01-01 00:00:00.000. DateTime64(3) .
Usage examples
SELECT UUIDv7ToDateTime(toUUID('018f05c9-4ab8-7b86-b64e-c9f03fbd45d1'))<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT UUIDv7ToDateTime(toUUID('018f05c9-4ab8-7b86-b64e-c9f03fbd45d1')) ; в”Ңв”ҖUUIDv7ToDateTime(toUUID('018f05c9-4ab8-7b86-b64e-c9f03fbd45d1'))в”Җв”җ ; SELECT UUIDv7ToDateTime(toUUID('018f05c9-4ab8-7b86-b64e-c9f03fbd45d1'), 'America/New_York')

description:
Generates a Snowflake ID .
The generated Snowflake ID contains the current Unix timestamp in milliseconds (41 + 1 top zero bits), followed by a machine id (10 bits), and a counter (12 bits) to distinguish IDs within a millisecond. For any given timestamp (unix_ts_ms), the counter starts at 0 and is incremented by 1 for each new Snowflake ID until the timestamp changes. In case the counter overflows, the timestamp field is incremented by 1 and the counter is reset to 0.
Function generateSnowflakeID guarantees that the counter field within a timestamp increments monotonically across all function invocations in concurrently running threads and queries.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Generates a Snowflake ID .
The generated Snowflake ID contains the current Unix timestamp in milliseconds (41 + 1 top zero bits), followed by a machine id (10 bits), and a counter (12 bits) to distinguish IDs within a millisecond. For any given timestamp (unix_ts_ms), the counter starts at 0 and is incremented by 1 for each new Snowflake ID until the timestamp changes. In case the counter overflows, the timestamp field is incremented by 1 and the counter is reset to 0.
Function generateSnowflakeID guarantees that the counter field within a timestamp increments monotonically across all function invocations in concurrently running threads and queries.<sep>[SYNTAX]: generateSnowflakeID([expr, [machine_id]])<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - expr вҖ” An arbitrary expression used to bypass common subexpression elimination if the function is called multiple times in a query. The value of the expression has no effect on the returned Snowflake ID. Optional.
- machine_id вҖ” A machine ID, the lowest 10 bits are used. Int64 . Optional.
Returned value
A value of type UInt64.<sep>[RETURN]: A value of type UInt64.<sep>[DEMO]: в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җidв”Җв”җ

description: Extracts the timestamp component of a Snowflake ID in DateTime format.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Extracts the timestamp component of a Snowflake ID in DateTime format.<sep>[SYNTAX]: snowflakeToDateTime(value[, time_zone])<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - value вҖ” Snowflake ID. Int64 .
- time_zone вҖ” Timezone . The function parses time_string according to the timezone. Optional. String .
Returned value
- The timestamp component of value as a DateTime value.<sep>[RETURN]: - The timestamp component of value as a DateTime value.<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT snowflakeToDateTime(CAST('1426860702823350272', 'Int64'), 'UTC'); ; в”Ңв”ҖsnowflakeToDateTime(CAST('1426860702823350272', 'Int64'), 'UTC')в”Җв”җ

description: Extracts the timestamp component of a Snowflake ID in DateTime64 format.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Extracts the timestamp component of a Snowflake ID in DateTime64 format.<sep>[SYNTAX]: snowflakeToDateTime64(value[, time_zone])<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - value вҖ” Snowflake ID. Int64 .
- time_zone вҖ” Timezone . The function parses time_string according to the timezone. Optional. String .
Returned value
- The timestamp component of value as a DateTime64 with scale = 3, i.e. millisecond precision.<sep>[RETURN]: - The timestamp component of value as a DateTime64 with scale = 3, i.e. millisecond precision.<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT snowflakeToDateTime64(CAST('1426860802823350272', 'Int64'), 'UTC'); ; в”Ңв”ҖsnowflakeToDateTime64(CAST('1426860802823350272', 'Int64'), 'UTC')в”Җв”җ

description: Converts a DateTime value to the first Snowflake ID at the giving time.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Converts a DateTime value to the first Snowflake ID at the giving time.<sep>[SYNTAX]: dateTimeToSnowflake(value)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - value вҖ” Date with time. DateTime .
Returned value
- Input value converted to the Int64 data type as the first Snowflake ID at that time.<sep>[RETURN]: - Input value converted to the Int64 data type as the first Snowflake ID at that time.<sep>[DEMO]: WITH toDateTime('2021-08-15 18:57:56', 'Asia/Shanghai') AS dt SELECT dateTimeToSnowflake(dt); ; в”Ңв”ҖdateTimeToSnowflake(dt)в”Җв”җ
example: WITH toDateTime('2021-08-15 18:57:56', 'Asia/Shanghai') AS dt SELECT dateTimeToSnowflake(dt);

description: Returns the timestamp component of a Snowflake ID as a value of type DateTime .
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Returns the timestamp component of a Snowflake ID as a value of type DateTime .<sep>[SYNTAX]: snowflakeIDToDateTime(value[, epoch[, time_zone]])<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - value вҖ” Snowflake ID. UInt64 .
- epoch - Epoch of the Snowflake ID in milliseconds since 1970-01-01. Defaults to 0 (1970-01-01). For the Twitter/X epoch (2015-01-01), provide 1288834974657. Optional. UInt* .
- time_zone вҖ” Timezone . The function parses time_string according to the timezone. Optional. String .
Returned value
- The timestamp component of value as a DateTime value.<sep>[RETURN]: - The timestamp component of value as a DateTime value.<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT snowflakeIDToDateTime(7204436857747984384) AS res ; в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җresв”Җв”җ

description:
Calculates the distance between two points on the Earth's surface using the great-circle formula .
Input parameters
- lon1Deg вҖ” Longitude of the first point in degrees. Range: [-180В°, 180В°] .
- lat1Deg вҖ” Latitude of the first point in degrees. Range: [-90В°, 90В°] .
- lon2Deg вҖ” Longitude of the second point in degrees. Range: [-180В°, 180В°] .
- lat2Deg вҖ” Latitude of the second point in degrees. Range: [-90В°, 90В°] .
Positive values correspond to North latitude and East longitude, and negative values correspond to South latitude and West longitude.
Returned value
The distance between two points on the Earth's surface, in meters.
Generates an exception when the input parameter values fall outside of the range.

description:
Similar to greatCircleDistance but calculates the distance on WGS-84 ellipsoid instead of sphere. This is more precise approximation of the Earth Geoid. The performance is the same as for greatCircleDistance (no performance drawback). It is recommended to use geoDistance to calculate the distances on Earth.
Technical note: for close enough points we calculate the distance using planar approximation with the metric on the tangent plane at the midpoint of the coordinates.
Input parameters
- lon1Deg вҖ” Longitude of the first point in degrees. Range: [-180В°, 180В°] .
- lat1Deg вҖ” Latitude of the first point in degrees. Range: [-90В°, 90В°] .
- lon2Deg вҖ” Longitude of the second point in degrees. Range: [-180В°, 180В°] .
- lat2Deg вҖ” Latitude of the second point in degrees. Range: [-90В°, 90В°] .
Positive values correspond to North latitude and East longitude, and negative values correspond to South latitude and West longitude.
Returned value
The distance between two points on the Earth's surface, in meters.
Generates an exception when the input parameter values fall outside of the range.

description:
Checks whether the point belongs to at least one of the ellipses. Coordinates are geometric in the Cartesian coordinate system.
Input parameters
- x, y вҖ” Coordinates of a point on the plane.
- xбөў, yбөў вҖ” Coordinates of the center of the i -th ellipsis.
- aбөў, bбөў вҖ” Axes of the i -th ellipsis in units of x, y coordinates.
The input parameters must be 2+4вӢ…n , where n is the number of ellipses.
Returned values
1 if the point is inside at least one of the ellipses; 0 if it is not.

description:
Checks whether the point belongs to the polygon on the plane.
Input values
- (x, y) вҖ” Coordinates of a point on the plane. Data type вҖ” Tuple вҖ” A tuple of two numbers.
- [(a, b), (c, d) ...] вҖ” Polygon vertices. Data type вҖ” Array . Each vertex is represented by a pair of coordinates (a, b) . Vertices should be specified in a clockwise or counterclockwise order. The minimum number of vertices is 3. The polygon must be constant.
- The function supports polygon with holes (cut-out sections). Data type вҖ” Polygon . Either pass the entire Polygon as the second argument, or pass the outer ring first and then each hole as separate additional arguments.
- The function also supports multipolygon. Data type вҖ” MultiPolygon . Either pass the entire MultiPolygon as the second argument, or list each component polygon as its own argument.
Returned values
1 if the point is inside the polygon, 0 if it is not. If the point is on the polygon boundary, the function may return either 0 or 1.

If the point is on the polygon boundary, the function may return either 0 or 1.<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT pointInPolygon((3., 3.), [(6, 0), (8, 4), (5, 8), (0, 2)]) AS res ; в”Ңв”Җresв”Җв”җ

description:
Geohash is the geocode system, which subdivides Earth's surface into buckets of grid shape and encodes each cell into a short string of letters and digits. It is a hierarchical data structure, so the longer the geohash string is, the more precise the geographic location will be.
If you need to manually convert geographic coordinates to geohash strings, you can use geohash.org
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Geohash is the geocode system, which subdivides Earth's surface into buckets of grid shape and encodes each cell into a short string of letters and digits. It is a hierarchical data structure, so the longer the geohash string is, the more precise the geographic location will be.
If you need to manually convert geographic coordinates to geohash strings, you can use geohash.org

description: Encodes latitude and longitude as a geohash -string.
detail: [DESCRIPTION]: Encodes latitude and longitude as a geohash -string.<sep>[SYNTAX]: geohashEncode(longitude, latitude, [precision])<sep>[RETURN]: - Alphanumeric string of the encoded coordinate (modified version of the base32-encoding alphabet is used). String .<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT geohashEncode(-5.60302734375, 42.593994140625, 0) AS res; ; в”Ңв”Җresв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ

description: Decodes any geohash -encoded string into longitude and latitude.
detail: [DESCRIPTION]: Decodes any geohash -encoded string into longitude and latitude.<sep>[SYNTAX]: geohashDecode(hash_str)<sep>[RETURN]: - Tuple (longitude, latitude) of Float64 values of longitude and latitude. Tuple ( Float64 )<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT geohashDecode('ezs42') AS res; ; в”Ңв”Җresв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ

description: Returns an array of geohash -encoded strings of given precision that fall inside and intersect boundaries of given box, basically a 2D grid flattened into array.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Returns an array of geohash -encoded strings of given precision that fall inside and intersect boundaries of given box, basically a 2D grid flattened into array.<sep>[SYNTAX]: geohashesInBox(longitude_min, latitude_min, longitude_max, latitude_max, precision)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - longitude_min вҖ” Minimum longitude. Range: [-180В°, 180В°] . Float .
- latitude_min вҖ” Minimum latitude. Range: [-90В°, 90В°] . Float .
- longitude_max вҖ” Maximum longitude. Range: [-180В°, 180В°] . Float .
- latitude_max вҖ” Maximum latitude. Range: [-90В°, 90В°] . Float .
- precision вҖ” Geohash precision. Range: [1, 12] . UInt8 .
Returned values
- Array of precision-long strings of geohash-boxes covering provided area, you should not rely on order of items. Array ( String ).
- [] - Empty array if minimum latitude and longitude values aren't less than corresponding maximum values.<sep>[RETURN]: - Array of precision-long strings of geohash-boxes covering provided area, you should not rely on order of items. Array ( String ).
- [] - Empty array if minimum latitude and longitude values aren't less than corresponding maximum values.<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT geohashesInBox(24.48, 40.56, 24.785, 40.81, 4) AS thasos; ; в”Ңв”Җthasosв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ

description:
H3 is a geographical indexing system where the Earth's surface is divided into a grid of even hexagonal cells. This system is hierarchical, i. e. each hexagon on the top level ("parent") can be split into seven even but smaller ones ("children"), and so on.
The level of the hierarchy is called resolution and can receive a value from 0 till 15 , where 0 is the base level with the largest and coarsest cells.
A latitude and longitude pair can be transformed to a 64-bit H3 index, identifying a grid cell.
The H3 index is used primarily for bucketing locations and other geospatial manipulations.
The full description of the H3 system is available at the Uber Engineering site .

description: Verifies whether the number is a valid H3 index.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Verifies whether the number is a valid H3 index.<sep>[SYNTAX]: h3IsValid(h3index)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - h3index вҖ” Hexagon index number. UInt64 .
Returned values
- 1 вҖ” The number is a valid H3 index. UInt8 .
- 0 вҖ” The number is not a valid H3 index. UInt8 .<sep>[RETURN]: - 1 вҖ” The number is a valid H3 index. UInt8 .
- 0 вҖ” The number is not a valid H3 index. UInt8 .<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT h3IsValid(630814730351855103) AS h3IsValid; ; в”Ңв”Җh3IsValidв”Җв”җ

description: Defines the resolution of the given H3 index.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Defines the resolution of the given H3 index.<sep>[SYNTAX]: h3GetResolution(h3index)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - h3index вҖ” Hexagon index number. UInt64 .
Returned values
- Index resolution. Range: [0, 15] . UInt8 .
- If the index is not valid, the function returns a random value. Use h3IsValid to verify the index. UInt8 .<sep>[RETURN]: - Index resolution. Range: [0, 15] . UInt8 .
- If the index is not valid, the function returns a random value. Use h3IsValid to verify the index. UInt8 .<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT h3GetResolution(639821929606596015) AS resolution; ; в”Ңв”Җresolutionв”Җв”җ

description: Returns H3 point index (lat, lon) with specified resolution.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Returns H3 point index (lat, lon) with specified resolution.<sep>[SYNTAX]: geoToH3(lat, lon, resolution)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - lat вҖ” Latitude. Float64 .
- lon вҖ” Longitude. Float64 .
- resolution вҖ” Index resolution. Range: [0, 15] . UInt8 .
Returned values
- Hexagon index number. UInt64 .
- 0 in case of error. UInt64 .
Note: In ClickHouse v25.4 or older, geoToH3() takes values in order (lon, lat) . As per ClickHouse v25.5, the input values are in order (lat, lon) . The previous behaviour can be restored using setting geotoh3_argument_order = 'lon_lat' .<sep>[RETURN]: - Hexagon index number. UInt64 .
- 0 in case of error. UInt64 .
Note: In ClickHouse v25.4 or older, geoToH3() takes values in order (lon, lat) . As per ClickHouse v25.5, the input values are in order (lat, lon) . The previous behaviour can be restored using setting geotoh3_argument_order = 'lon_lat' .<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT geoToH3(55.71290588, 37.79506683, 15) AS h3Index; ; в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җh3Indexв”Җв”җ

description: Returns the centroid latitude and longitude corresponding to the provided H3 index.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Returns the centroid latitude and longitude corresponding to the provided H3 index.<sep>[SYNTAX]: h3ToGeo(h3Index)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - h3Index вҖ” H3 Index. UInt64 .
Returned values
- A tuple consisting of two values: tuple(lat,lon) . lat вҖ” Latitude. Float64 . lon вҖ” Longitude. Float64 .
Note: In ClickHouse v24.12 or older, h3ToGeo() returns values in order (lon, lat) . As per ClickHouse v25.1, the returned values are in order (lat, lon) . The previous behaviour can be restored using setting h3togeo_lon_lat_result_order = true .<sep>[RETURN]: - A tuple consisting of two values: tuple(lat,lon) . lat вҖ” Latitude. Float64 . lon вҖ” Longitude. Float64 .
Note: In ClickHouse v24.12 or older, h3ToGeo() returns values in order (lon, lat) . As per ClickHouse v25.1, the returned values are in order (lat, lon) . The previous behaviour can be restored using setting h3togeo_lon_lat_result_order = true .<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT h3ToGeo(644325524701193974) AS coordinates; ; в”Ңв”Җcoordinatesв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ

description: Returns the "great circle" or "haversine" distance between pairs of GeoCoord points (latitude/longitude) pairs in meters.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Returns the "great circle" or "haversine" distance between pairs of GeoCoord points (latitude/longitude) pairs in meters.<sep>[SYNTAX]: h3PointDistM(lat1, lon1, lat2, lon2)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - lat1 , lon1 вҖ” Latitude and Longitude of point1 in degrees. Float64 .

Returned values

description:
Returns a WKT (Well Known Text) geometric object from various Geo Data Types . Supported WKT objects are:
- POINT
- POLYGON
- MULTIPOLYGON
- LINESTRING
- MULTILINESTRING
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Returns a WKT (Well Known Text) geometric object from various Geo Data Types . Supported WKT objects are:
- POINT
- POLYGON
- MULTIPOLYGON
- LINESTRING
- MULTILINESTRING<sep>[SYNTAX]: WKT(geo_data)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: geo_data can be one of the following Geo Data Types or their underlying primitive types:
- Point
- Ring
- Polygon
- MultiPolygon
- LineString
- MultiLineString
Returned value
- WKT geometric object POINT is returned for a Point.
- WKT geometric object POLYGON is returned for a Polygon
- WKT geometric object MULTIPOLYGON is returned for a MultiPolygon.
- WKT geometric object LINESTRING is returned for a LineString.
- WKT geometric object MULTILINESTRING is returned for a MultiLineString.<sep>[RETURN]: - WKT geometric object POINT is returned for a Point.
- WKT geometric object POLYGON is returned for a Polygon
- WKT geometric object MULTIPOLYGON is returned for a MultiPolygon.
- WKT geometric object LINESTRING is returned for a LineString.
- WKT geometric object MULTILINESTRING is returned for a MultiLineString.<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT wkt((0., 0.)); ; POINT(0 0) ; SELECT wkt([(0., 0.), (10., 0.), (10., 10.), (0., 10.)]);
[DEMO]: SELECT
example:
SELECT
    toTypeName(readWKTMultiPolygon('MULTIPOLYGON(((2 0,10 0,10 10,0 10,2 0),(4 4,5 4,5 5,4 5,4 4)),((-10 -10,-10 -9,-9 10,-10 -10)))')) AS type,
    readWKTMultiPolygon('MULTIPOLYGON(((2 0,10 0,10 10,0 10,2 0),(4 4,5 4,5 5,4 5,4 4)),((-10 -10,-10 -9,-9 10,-10 -10)))') AS output FORMAT Markdown

detail: [DESCRIPTION]: String starting with MULTIPOLYGON

detail: [DESCRIPTION]: MultiPolygon
[DEMO]: readWKTPoint(wkt_string)

description: - wkt_string : The input WKT string representing a Point geometry.
detail: [DESCRIPTION]: - wkt_string : The input WKT string representing a Point geometry.

description:
S2 is a geographical indexing system where all geographical data is represented on a sphere (similar to a globe).
In the S2 library points are represented as the S2 Index - a specific number which encodes internally a point on the surface of a unit sphere, unlike traditional (latitude, longitude) pairs. To get the S2 point index for a given point specified in the format (latitude, longitude) use the geoToS2 function. Also, you can use the s2ToGeo function for getting geographical coordinates corresponding to the specified S2 point index.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: S2 is a geographical indexing system where all geographical data is represented on a sphere (similar to a globe).
In the S2 library points are represented as the S2 Index - a specific number which encodes internally a point on the surface of a unit sphere, unlike traditional (latitude, longitude) pairs. To get the S2 point index for a given point specified in the format (latitude, longitude) use the geoToS2 function. Also, you can use the s2ToGeo function for getting geographical coordinates corresponding to the specified S2 point index.

description: Returns S2 point index corresponding to the provided coordinates (longitude, latitude) .
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Returns S2 point index corresponding to the provided coordinates (longitude, latitude) .<sep>[SYNTAX]: geoToS2(lon, lat)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - lon вҖ” Longitude. Float64 .
- lat вҖ” Latitude. Float64 .
Returned values
- S2 point index. UInt64 .<sep>[RETURN]: - S2 point index. UInt64 .<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT geoToS2(37.79506683, 55.71290588) AS s2Index; ; в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җs2Indexв”Җв”җ

description: Returns geo coordinates (longitude, latitude) corresponding to the provided S2 point index.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Returns geo coordinates (longitude, latitude) corresponding to the provided S2 point index.<sep>[SYNTAX]: s2ToGeo(s2index)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - s2index вҖ” S2 Index. UInt64 .
Returned values
- A tuple consisting of two values: lon . Float64 . lat . Float64 .<sep>[RETURN]: - A tuple consisting of two values: lon . Float64 . lat . Float64 .<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT s2ToGeo(4704772434919038107) AS s2Coodrinates; ; в”Ңв”Җs2Coodrinatesв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ

description: Returns S2 neighbor indexes corresponding to the provided S2 . Each cell in the S2 system is a quadrilateral bounded by four geodesics. So, each cell has 4 neighbors.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Returns S2 neighbor indexes corresponding to the provided S2 . Each cell in the S2 system is a quadrilateral bounded by four geodesics. So, each cell has 4 neighbors.<sep>[SYNTAX]: s2GetNeighbors(s2index)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - s2index вҖ” S2 Index. UInt64 .
Returned value
- An array consisting of 4 neighbor indexes: array[s2index1, s2index3, s2index2, s2index4] . Array ( UInt64 ).<sep>[RETURN]: - An array consisting of 4 neighbor indexes: array[s2index1, s2index3, s2index2, s2index4] . Array ( UInt64 ).<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT s2GetNeighbors(5074766849661468672) AS s2Neighbors; ; в”Ңв”Җs2Neighborsв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ

description: Determines if the two provided S2 cells intersect or not.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Determines if the two provided S2 cells intersect or not.<sep>[SYNTAX]: s2CellsIntersect(s2index1, s2index2)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - siIndex1 , s2index2 вҖ” S2 Index. UInt64 .
Returned value
- 1 вҖ” If the cells intersect. UInt8 .
- 0 вҖ” If the cells don't intersect. UInt8 .<sep>[RETURN]: - 1 вҖ” If the cells intersect. UInt8 .
- 0 вҖ” If the cells don't intersect. UInt8 .<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT s2CellsIntersect(9926595209846587392, 9926594385212866560) AS intersect; ; в”Ңв”Җintersectв”Җв”җ

description: Determines if a cap contains a S2 point. A cap represents a part of the sphere that has been cut off by a plane. It is defined by a point on a sphere and a radius in degrees.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Determines if a cap contains a S2 point. A cap represents a part of the sphere that has been cut off by a plane. It is defined by a point on a sphere and a radius in degrees.<sep>[SYNTAX]: s2CapContains(center, degrees, point)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - center вҖ” S2 point index corresponding to the cap. UInt64 .
- degrees вҖ” Radius of the cap in degrees. Float64 .
- point вҖ” S2 point index. UInt64 .
Returned value
- 1 вҖ” If the cap contains the S2 point index. UInt8 .
- 0 вҖ” If the cap doesn't contain the S2 point index. UInt8 .<sep>[RETURN]: - 1 вҖ” If the cap contains the S2 point index. UInt8 .
- 0 вҖ” If the cap doesn't contain the S2 point index. UInt8 .<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT s2CapContains(1157339245694594829, 1.0, 1157347770437378819) AS capContains; ; в”Ңв”ҖcapContainsв”Җв”җ

description: Determines the smallest cap that contains the given two input caps. A cap represents a portion of the sphere that has been cut off by a plane. It is defined by a point on a sphere and a radius in degrees.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Determines the smallest cap that contains the given two input caps. A cap represents a portion of the sphere that has been cut off by a plane. It is defined by a point on a sphere and a radius in degrees.<sep>[SYNTAX]: s2CapUnion(center1, radius1, center2, radius2)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - center1 , center2 вҖ” S2 point indexes corresponding to the two input caps. UInt64 .
- radius1 , radius2 вҖ” Radius of the two input caps in degrees. Float64 .
Returned values
- center вҖ” S2 point index corresponding the center of the smallest cap containing the two input caps. UInt64 .
- radius вҖ” Radius of the smallest cap containing the two input caps. Float64 .<sep>[RETURN]: - center вҖ” S2 point index corresponding the center of the smallest cap containing the two input caps. UInt64 .
- radius вҖ” Radius of the smallest cap containing the two input caps. Float64 .<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT s2CapUnion(3814912406305146967, 1.0, 1157347770437378819, 1.0) AS capUnion; ; в”Ңв”ҖcapUnionв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ

description: Increases the size of the bounding rectangle to include the given S2 point. In the S2 system, a rectangle is represented by a type of S2Region called a S2LatLngRect that represents a rectangle in latitude-longitude space.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Increases the size of the bounding rectangle to include the given S2 point. In the S2 system, a rectangle is represented by a type of S2Region called a S2LatLngRect that represents a rectangle in latitude-longitude space.<sep>[SYNTAX]: s2RectAdd(s2pointLow, s2pointHigh, s2Point)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - s2PointLow вҖ” Low S2 point index corresponding to the rectangle. UInt64 .
- s2PointHigh вҖ” High S2 point index corresponding to the rectangle. UInt64 .
- s2Point вҖ” Target S2 point index that the bound rectangle should be grown to include. UInt64 .
Returned values
- s2PointLow вҖ” Low S2 cell id corresponding to the grown rectangle. UInt64 .
- s2PointHigh вҖ” Height S2 cell id corresponding to the grown rectangle. UInt64 .<sep>[RETURN]: - s2PointLow вҖ” Low S2 cell id corresponding to the grown rectangle. UInt64 .
- s2PointHigh вҖ” Height S2 cell id corresponding to the grown rectangle. UInt64 .<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT s2RectAdd(5178914411069187297, 5177056748191934217, 5179056748191934217) AS rectAdd; ; в”Ңв”ҖrectAddв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ

description: Determines if a given rectangle contains a S2 point. In the S2 system, a rectangle is represented by a type of S2Region called a S2LatLngRect that represents a rectangle in latitude-longitude space.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Determines if a given rectangle contains a S2 point. In the S2 system, a rectangle is represented by a type of S2Region called a S2LatLngRect that represents a rectangle in latitude-longitude space.<sep>[SYNTAX]: s2RectContains(s2PointLow, s2PointHi, s2Point)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - s2PointLow вҖ” Low S2 point index corresponding to the rectangle. UInt64 .
- s2PointHigh вҖ” High S2 point index corresponding to the rectangle. UInt64 .
- s2Point вҖ” Target S2 point index. UInt64 .
Returned value
- 1 вҖ” If the rectangle contains the given S2 point.
- 0 вҖ” If the rectangle doesn't contain the given S2 point.<sep>[RETURN]: - 1 вҖ” If the rectangle contains the given S2 point.
- 0 вҖ” If the rectangle doesn't contain the given S2 point.<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT s2RectContains(5179062030687166815, 5177056748191934217, 5177914411069187297) AS rectContains; ; в”Ңв”ҖrectContainsв”Җв”җ

description: Returns the smallest rectangle containing the union of this rectangle and the given rectangle. In the S2 system, a rectangle is represented by a type of S2Region called a S2LatLngRect that represents a rectangle in latitude-longitude space.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Returns the smallest rectangle containing the union of this rectangle and the given rectangle. In the S2 system, a rectangle is represented by a type of S2Region called a S2LatLngRect that represents a rectangle in latitude-longitude space.<sep>[SYNTAX]: s2RectUnion(s2Rect1PointLow, s2Rect1PointHi, s2Rect2PointLow, s2Rect2PointHi)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - s2Rect1PointLow , s2Rect1PointHi вҖ” Low and High S2 point indexes corresponding to the first rectangle. UInt64 .
- s2Rect2PointLow , s2Rect2PointHi вҖ” Low and High S2 point indexes corresponding to the second rectangle. UInt64 .
Returned values
- s2UnionRect2PointLow вҖ” Low S2 cell id corresponding to the union rectangle. UInt64 .
- s2UnionRect2PointHi вҖ” High S2 cell id corresponding to the union rectangle. UInt64 .<sep>[RETURN]: - s2UnionRect2PointLow вҖ” Low S2 cell id corresponding to the union rectangle. UInt64 .
- s2UnionRect2PointHi вҖ” High S2 cell id corresponding to the union rectangle. UInt64 .<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT s2RectUnion(5178914411069187297, 5177056748191934217, 5179062030687166815, 5177056748191934217) AS rectUnion; ; в”Ңв”ҖrectUnionв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ

description: Returns the smallest rectangle containing the intersection of this rectangle and the given rectangle. In the S2 system, a rectangle is represented by a type of S2Region called a S2LatLngRect that represents a rectangle in latitude-longitude space.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Returns the smallest rectangle containing the intersection of this rectangle and the given rectangle. In the S2 system, a rectangle is represented by a type of S2Region called a S2LatLngRect that represents a rectangle in latitude-longitude space.<sep>[SYNTAX]: s2RectIntersection(s2Rect1PointLow, s2Rect1PointHi, s2Rect2PointLow, s2Rect2PointHi)<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - s2Rect1PointLow , s2Rect1PointHi вҖ” Low and High S2 point indexes corresponding to the first rectangle. UInt64 .
- s2Rect2PointLow , s2Rect2PointHi вҖ” Low and High S2 point indexes corresponding to the second rectangle. UInt64 .
Returned values
- s2UnionRect2PointLow вҖ” Low S2 cell id corresponding to the rectangle containing the intersection of the given rectangles. UInt64 .
- s2UnionRect2PointHi вҖ” High S2 cell id corresponding to the rectangle containing the intersection of the given rectangles. UInt64 .<sep>[RETURN]: - s2UnionRect2PointLow вҖ” Low S2 cell id corresponding to the rectangle containing the intersection of the given rectangles. UInt64 .
- s2UnionRect2PointHi вҖ” High S2 cell id corresponding to the rectangle containing the intersection of the given rectangles. UInt64 .<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT s2RectIntersection(5178914411069187297, 5177056748191934217, 5179062030687166815, 5177056748191934217) AS rectIntersection; ; в”Ңв”ҖrectIntersectionв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ

description: Returns a string of select SVG element tags from Geo data.
detail:
[DESCRIPTION]: Returns a string of select SVG element tags from Geo data.<sep>[SYNTAX]: Svg(geometry,[style])<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: - geometry вҖ” Geo data. Geo .
- style вҖ” Optional style name. String .
Returned value
- The SVG representation of the geometry. String . SVG circle SVG polygon SVG path<sep>[RETURN]: - The SVG representation of the geometry. String . SVG circle SVG polygon SVG path<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT SVG((0., 0.)) ; <circle cx="0" cy="0" r="5" style=""/> ; SELECT SVG([(0., 0.), (10, 0), (10, 10), (0, 10)])
example: <g fill-rule="evenodd"><path d="M 0,0 L 0,10 L 10,10 L 10,0 L 0,0M 4,4 L 5,4 L 5,5 L 4,5 L 4,4 z " style=""/></g>
